3. Sources of Misconceptions about Teach.desc:
- Misconception of the teaching profession emanates from the [attitude of teachers], the [government], and the [general public].
- Teachers themselves have a [poor self-image] of their profession. They do not want their children to become teachers too.
- Many people regard teaching as a vocation and believe their reward is in heaven.
- Some people feel that teaching is the most comfortable way of living poor.
- Most of these claims are baseless; some of the best brains can be seen in the teaching field especially at higher levels.
- To many people, teaching remains a negative choice to be made when other occupations are available.
ex: Sources: attitudes of teachers, government, and general public.
Teachers' poor self-image contributes to misconceptions.
Teachers discouraging own children from joining the profession.
Perception of teaching as a vocation with heavenly reward.
View that teaching leads to poverty.
Teaching seen as a last-resort career.

4. Ways of Addressing the Misconceptions.desc:
To overcome these misconceptions, teachers need to put a high price tag on themselves and not underrate themselves.

1. In order to develop an identity in the teaching profession, members are to consistently focus on learning. Thus, in-service training sessions need to be offered on regular bases to help the untrained teachers to do the work professionally. Again, teachers who have been trained must have intermittent training to boost their pedagogical skills and knowledge.

2. Teachers should be given better conditions of service in terms of salaries, good accommodation to boost their status, study leave into relevant programmes. In short, they are to be treated as other professions like doctors, lawyers and engineers.

3. The numerous associations in the teaching profession in Ghana seem to create a lot of challenges for the profession. In order to have an identity, GNAT/ and NAGRAT are expected to merge to form a formidable front and avoid the cliques that divide the teachers front, values and potentials. In fact, it will do the profession good if all and sundry close ranks in terms of qualifications, level of teaching and come together to fight a common cause.

4. Teachers should be given the freedom to practice once they are guided by the code of ethics of the profession. Other bodies like PTA, Old students Associations are to give teachers room to operate. There should be a limit to how far they can operate and allow the schools to practice without much interference.

5. The public should revisit the high recognition they were giving to teachers. They are to recognize and hold the profession in high esteem despite the fact that teachers' remuneration is relatively low. They should not forget that the services teachers render to the public is very crucial. Teachers render sacrificial job to the public, as indicated by Aristotle, 'Teachers who educate children deserve more honour than parents who merely gave birth to them, for the latter provided mere life, while the former ensure a good life'.

6. Teachers are expected to boost their confidence level. They need to put a price on themselves and not to underrate themselves. Teachers can only achieve a better identity if they focus on learning and aim at giving the best to the students they handle.
ex: Teachers must value themselves and focus on continuous learning.
Regular in-service training for both untrained and trained teachers.
Improve conditions of service: salaries, accommodation, study leave.
Merge teacher associations (GNAT/NAGRAT) for united front.
Give teachers freedom to practice with minimal external interference.
Public should restore high recognition and esteem for teachers.
Teachers need to boost confidence and self-worth.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
- You are most welcome to the first session of this course. This session focuses on exploring various [conceptions], [misconceptions], [prejudices] and [biases] student teachers, who have just transited into a College of Education from Senior High Schools may have about the Teaching Profession.

- This would enable the student teachers reflect on and interrogate their thinking, develop ways of addressing their misconceptions, in order to develop positive orientations towards teaching.

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. Write down some of the misconceptions and prejudices about teaching.
2. Discuss how to address the misconceptions about teaching.
3. Develop individual reflective notes on their perceptions about teaching from interactions with resource persons and videos.

ex: Conceptions = accurate ideas; Misconceptions = inaccurate ideas.
Misconception: A teacher's work ends at 3pm.
Misconception: Good teaching guarantees high test scores.
Misconception: Anyone can teach easily.
Misconception: Teachers alone are responsible for learning.
Misconception: All teachers are the same.
Misconception: Today's teachers are less dedicated.
Additional misconceptions: teachers are poor, teaching is for ladies, teaching is for failures, untrained teachers exist, low status, open entry, poor conditions, weak ethics enforcement.
2. Conceptions and Misconceptions about.desc:
- [Conceptions] about teaching are ideas, opinions, notions that people have about the profession which are accurate or true.
- [Misconceptions] about teaching are ideas, opinions, notions that people have about the profession which are not accurate.
- Some of these ideas, opinions have been used to condemn the teaching profession.

Common misconceptions include:
- A teacher's day ends when school closes: The belief that teachers stop working when school closes, ignoring preparation, grading, and professional development.
- If teachers are good at what they do, students' grades and test scores will be good as well: The best teachers instill belief and resilience, not just test scores.
- Teaching is easy, and anyone can do it: Teaching requires mastery of pedagogical theories, passion, and skills in managing learners.
- Teachers are solely responsible for learning: Parents must also play an active role in their child's education.
- Teachers are all the same: Every teacher has unique attitudes, skills, and beliefs.
- Today's teachers are not as good as they used to be some decades ago: Today's teachers are dedicated despite increased responsibilities.

Other common misconceptions:
- Teachers are poor and stingy.
- Teaching is for ladies.
- Teaching is regarded as a refuge for failure for students who couldn't make it to the university as a result of low grade.
- It is the academically weak students who find their way into Colleges of Education to become teachers.
- Presence of numerous untrained members.
- Low social esteem and status.
- Entry to the profession is not restricted, anybody can teach.
- Poor conditions of service.
- Code of ethics not rigidly enforced.
- Professional standards are not maintained always.

5. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:

# Self-Assessment

ex: Misconceptions are inaccurate ideas about teaching.
Sources of misconceptions: teachers' attitudes, government, general public.
Self-assessment includes listing misconceptions, distinguishing terms, and identifying sources.
Ways to address misconceptions include better conditions, unity among teachers, and public recognition.
Profession requires specific training and regulated standards.
Farrant: profession provides special service based on accumulated knowledge.
Antwi: profession requires advanced education and special training.
Profession has legal backing and offers skilled services for fees.

3. Teaching as a Profession.desc:
- A profession is a job that requires specific training and is regulated by certain standards.
- It is a vocation founded upon specialized educational training, the purpose of which is to provide objective counsel and service to others.
- A profession is something little more than a job, it is a career for someone who wants to be part of society, who becomes competent in a chosen sector through training; maintains skills through continuing professional development (CPD); and commits to behaving ethically to protect the interests of the public.
- According to Farrant (1981) as cited in Nyavor and Dusu (2020) a profession is a body that provides a special service to the community based on accumulated knowledge and wisdom. It also controls the entry qualification and work standards of its members.
- Antwi (1992) also posited that a profession is an occupation especially one requiring an advanced education and special training in its relevant discipline. For instance, law, architecture, medicine, pharmacy, among others.
- A profession is backed by legal system. This means that there are laws protecting and guiding it therefore it is highly recognized by the society in which it is practiced.
- Another unique feature of a profession is that it offers skilled services and for those services, fees are usually charged. This is also recognized by the society and generally protected by a legal system.
- The professional body ensures that its members upgrade themselves to meet the demands of this dynamic world.
- Teaching profession began to develop with the emergence of education as a field of profession and vocation.
- It has been argued for a long time whether teaching is a profession or not. In the end, it was agreed that teaching is a distinctive profession and that it possesses all of the qualities that a profession should possess (Tezcan, 1996).
- Teaching profession could be described as 'a professional occupational group of education sector possessing social, cultural, economic: scientific and technological dimensions' (Erden, 2007).
- A profession involves acquisition of knowledge, range of skills and their application in service of humanity. It also requires commitment to the profession and willingness to advance its growth and effectiveness. A profession must also have a great regard for the interest and welfare of its clients.

# Characteristics of the Teaching Profession in Ghana

i. Specialized body of knowledge: the major setback is the large number of untrained teachers, especially in deprived areas and private schools.
ii. Registration of Entry: Entry requirements are restricted but some practitioners lack stipulated qualifications.
iii. Conditions of Service: The Ghana Education Council has a booklet on conditions of service and code of professional conduct.
iv. Code of Ethics: Laid down directives used to govern the behavior of members.
v. Professional Associations: Teachers have associations to address their interests and regulate activities.
vi. Legal Backing: The profession is accorded legal recognition.
vii. Freedom to Practice the Profession: Members can practice within guidelines of conditions of service and code of ethics.
viii. Status before the Public: Associations seek to improve professional status through better conditions of service.

2. Concept of Teaching.desc:
- Teaching is an interpersonal influence aimed at changing the way or behaviour in which other persons can or will behave.
- Teaching is an activity aimed at the achievement of learning and it is practiced in such a way as to respect the learners' intellectual integrity and capacity.
- Abolade (2000) describes teaching as a set of activities that are designed to bring about changes in the behaviour of learners.
- Okoye (2010) stated that the main aim of teaching is to help someone acquire or change some skills, attitude, knowledge, idea or appreciation. Teaching is said to be effective only when the learners have been able to achieve the set behavioral objectives.
- According to Dewey (1916) teaching is a process of arranging the environment within which the pupils can interact.
- Teaching is a means whereby an experienced member of a group guides and directs pupils in their total growth and development. It is a system of actions intended to induce learning.
- Teaching as a concept is subject to a variety of interpretations depending on the angle of consideration.

# Teaching defined by some authors

- Nwachi (1991) defined teaching as the imparting of knowledge from one person to another and the guiding of someone to behave in a particular manner.
- Nacino-Brown, Oke and Brown (1982) defined teaching as an attempt to help someone acquire, or change some skills, attitude, knowledge, ideas or principles.
- Farant (1980) defines teaching as a process that facilitates learning through knowledge and skills acquired by a learner from an experienced person.
- Denga (2001) defined teaching as a social process in communication and interaction between the teacher and the learner with a view to improving the standard of the learner in the cognitive, affective and psychomotor areas of learning.
- Moore (2007) defines teaching as the actions of someone who is trying to assist others to reach their fullest potential in all aspect of development.
- Buba (2013) sees teaching as an attempt to guide and help people through planned activities to acquire or change some skills, attitudes, knowledge, ideas or appreciation.
- Koomson, Brown, Anyagre, Amoah & Frimpong, (2001) defined teaching as an activity, which is performed by a more experienced and knowledgeable person with a view of helping the less experienced and knowledgeable person to learn.

# Common issues about these definitions of teaching

1. The teacher can be identified (the person directing the activities).
2. The learner can also be identified (the person receiving the directions and reacting to the activities).
3. The intentions or purpose of the activities can be identified (the outcomes, learning or behaviour change).
4. Teaching usually involves face-to-face relationship between a teacher and a learner.

- Contemporary teaching involves creating situations to facilitate learning and motivating learners to have interest in what is being transmitted to them. It recognizes that teaching is a process that involves the whole of the personality of the teacher and the pupils.
ex: Teaching is interpersonal influence aimed at behavior change.
Abolade: teaching brings about changes in learner behavior.
Okoye: effective teaching achieves set behavioral objectives.
Dewey: teaching is arranging environment for pupil interaction.
Teaching involves guiding pupils in total growth and development.
Key elements: teacher, learner, intentions, face-to-face relationship.
Contemporary teaching: facilitating learning and motivating learners.

4. Teaching as an Art or Science.desc:
- The art and science of effective teaching may be defined from many points of view.
- There are those who believe that good teachers are born, not made. At the opposite side are those who claim that teaching is nothing more than the simple application of the correct reinforcers.

# Teaching as an Art

- Teaching as an Art is when the teacher uses [creativity], [flexibility] and [natural ways] to present his/her lesson so it is interesting for his/her students.
- It is the natural traits in some teachers that predispose them to perform effectively.
- The art of teaching was demonstrated by ancient philosophers, writers and religious personalities such as Aristotle, Plato, Socrates, John Dewey, Pestalozzi, Jesus Christ and Mohammed.
- Some teachers in primary and secondary schools display good teaching skills and styles even before training.

# Teaching as a Science

- Teaching as a Science consists of [knowledge], [skills] and [scientific principles] or scientific bases which can be learned.
- Those holding that teaching is a science believe that good teaching is the result of having deep knowledge of the subject matter and a solid understanding of the principles of teaching and learning.
- They believe that it is possible to learn and master the skills and strategies needed to be a successful teacher.
- Teaching as a science is seen in terms of the application of systematically tested theories and approaches to teaching.
- Teachers could not begin to practice teaching without having some sound knowledge of the context of their practice and some ideas about what can and should be done.
- They have to go through intensive training to acquire the necessary skills needed for teaching before they can teach.

- These two positions are two extremes of a long continuous process. In this process, the teacher has an important role to play because he/she acts like a catalyst actively stimulating learning.
- It is possible that there are natural traits in some teachers that predispose them to being good teachers, but it is also essential that all teachers master a set of tested teaching skills to be successful.
- It is worth stating that the [combination] of the art and science of teaching will make one an effective teacher.
ex: Debate: teaching as art (born) vs. teaching as science (made).
Art: uses creativity, flexibility, natural traits; examples: ancient philosophers.
Science: consists of knowledge, skills, scientific principles learned through training.
Effective teaching requires combination of both art and science.

6. Qualities of a Good Teacher.desc:
- Qualities are distinctive attributes or characteristics possessed by an individual. It is a special trait that makes a person, thing or group different from others.

# Professional Qualities of a Good Teacher

Farrant (1980) deducing from different researches came with various professional qualities/skills of a teacher to include the following:

1. Establishing a productive classroom atmosphere from the beginning by means of good organization and careful planning of classroom structures.
2. Creating excellent teacher-pupil relations. The teacher should be friendly and humorous.
3. Accepting constructive suggestions and ideas from the learners as much as possible.
4. Giving rewards, praises frequently to deserving learners during the teaching learning sessions.
5. Being confident when teaching. He/she should control all classroom activities effectively.
6. Explaining instructional activities clearly and including a variety of children's activities in his/her learning.
7. Handling problems promptly before they get out of hands.
8. The teacher should always be fair and firm. Inconsistency exposes weaknesses; preferential treatment loses respect.
9. Using efficient means to deal with routine administrative matters such as registration, giving out books, tidying up after practical lessons.
10. Avoiding over-reaction to learners' misbehaviour.
11. Uses appropriate punishment in dealing with students' misbehaviour.
12. Inspire and encourage students in his/her teaching.
13. Exhibit such qualities as empathy, dedication and zeal to discharge duties diligently to society.

# Acquiring Professional Skills

These skills can best be acquired through the following:

1. Attending professional teacher education institutions.
2. Participating regularly in in-service training.
3. Attendance at staff meetings regularly.
4. Reading professional books, journals.
5. Mentoring student, etc.

# Personal Characteristics of a Good Teacher

These are characteristics that relate to the personality of the teacher that brings about good classroom relationships.

Makinde (1991) has suggested that the personal characteristics of good teacher should include the following:

- Be emotionally stable.
- Be a good listener.
- Must be warm and responsive.
- Must have sense of humour.
- Must be patient, tolerant, neat, pleasant and broadminded.
- Must be morally upright so as not to exploit the situation of the students.
- Must genuine, honest, and sincere.
- Must have great sense of humor.
- Must be empathetic.
- Must be accessible, enthusiastic and caring.
ex: Professional qualities: productive atmosphere, good relations, accepting suggestions, giving rewards, confidence, clear explanation, prompt problem handling, fairness, efficient administration, no over-reaction, appropriate punishment, inspiration, empathy, dedication.
Acquiring skills: training institutions, in-service, staff meetings, reading, mentoring.
Personal qualities: emotional stability, good listener, warm, humorous, patient, tolerant, neat, broadminded, morally upright, honest, sincere, empathetic, accessible, enthusiastic, caring.

7. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

In the foregoing section, we learned the Concept of teaching as an activity that induces learning. A profession stands for an occupation which requires some specialized study and training, with the purpose of generally providing skilled services and guidance for a definite fee or remuneration. We also looked at the concept of teaching being an art or science.

# Self-Assessment

1. Discuss briefly the concept of teaching.

2. State and explain FOUR (4) characteristics of teaching as a profession.

3. What is the meaning of a profession?

4. As a student teacher, distinguished between teaching as an art and teaching as a science.

5. List FIVE (5) professional characteristics and FIVE (5) personal characteristics of a good teacher.
ex: Teaching: activity that induces learning.
Profession: occupation requiring specialized study and training for skilled service.
Teaching as art: natural traits, creativity.
Teaching as science: learned knowledge, skills, principles.
Self-assessment covers concept, characteristics, art vs science, and teacher qualities.
Good teachers: warm, accessible, enthusiastic, caring.
Characteristics: deep subject knowledge, good communication, encouraging, affectionate, humorous, prepared, ethical, participatory.
Subject knowledge requires 3-4 years training and continuous updating.
Communication involves verbal, nonverbal, and visual skills.
Supportive teachers improve self-confidence and reduce disciplinary problems.
Affection and humor create positive classroom environment.
Preparation includes lesson planning and professional development.
Ethics: work according to code of conduct.
Participatory learning: group work, role play, demonstrations.
5. Characteristics of a Good Teacher.desc:
- Good teachers form strong relationships with their students and show that they care about them as people.
- Good teachers are warm, accessible, enthusiastic and caring.
- For an individual to be regarded as a good teacher, he/she needs to possess a number of skills, characteristics and qualities.

i. Deep knowledge in the subject matter: Requires formal training (3-4 years in Ghana). Teachers must update knowledge to keep abreast with current issues.
ii. Good communication skills: Includes verbal, nonverbal, and visual. Teachers must convey knowledge in understandable and engaging ways.
iii. Encouraging and supportive: Support students to have self-confidence, learn by themselves, and develop positive academic self-concept.
iv. Being affectionate, understanding and humorous: Affection develops students' personality; humor creates friendly classroom atmosphere.
v. Preparation and organizational skills: Good teachers spend hours preparing, designing lessons, and participating in professional development.
vi. A strong professional ethics: Work and conduct according to ethics; always willing to help students.
vii. Creating a participatory learning environment: Actively involve students through group work, role play, demonstrations.

3. Misconceptions and Traditional Belief.desc:
- Many people in Ghana expect every child to be born normal and grow up normal; therefore, when a woman is pregnant it brings a lot of joy and high expectations into family.
- Nobody is ready to understand any reason for which a child should be born with disability or developed disability after birth.
- However, when a child is born with any form of disability, there is a general disappointment which brings about undecided reactions.

Various communities in Ghana hold certain beliefs about children with disabilities or special needs. These beliefs include the following:

- Witchcraft is believed to be one of the causes of disability.
- Curses upon a family or an individual.
- Disrespecting the traditional gods, as for example Tigare.
- Disagreed with taboo.
- Worried women asking favour from the gods for children could be given disabled children.
- Parents sacrificing their children spiritually for money.
- Normal children can also become exceptional as a result of laughing at other children with disabilities.
- For example, a pregnant woman who ridicules a person with disability can give birth to a handicapped child.
- A pregnant woman eating some forbidden foods.
- Mothers who are cruel to animals are likely to give birth to children with disabilities.
- Exceptional children are symbols of bad omen.
- They are also regarded as manifestation of reincarnation especially in the case of gifted people.
- A pregnant woman having affairs with other men other than her husband.
- Normal persons marrying handicapped persons.
- Refusing to take traditional medicines when pregnant.
- Failure to sacrifice.
- The negative attitudes and opinions about the determinants of disability.
- Disability is commonly misbelieved to be the result of past sins or bad luck.
- Disability is considered as contagious.
- Children with disabilities are affected by their condition particularly in every aspect of life, being exposed to social rejection, poverty, isolation, ignorance, lack of proper education, services and assistance.

- Traditional beliefs about the causes of disability, misunderstanding of the dimensions of impairment and the lack of knowledge of SEN lead to their invisibility and exclusion within the education arena and the wider community.

- Exclusion and marginalization make them uniquely vulnerable, affecting their dignity, individuality, and chances to have a normal life.

- Planning for inclusion at the school level means thinking about how the curriculum can be designed to match the needs and interests of the full range of learners. These will need to be addressed both inside and outside the classroom.

The learners with special needs may include:

- The gifted and the talented
- Learners with learning difficulties and disabilities
- The different needs of boys and girls
- Learners with social, emotional and behavioural difficulties
- Marginalized learners (e.g. orphans and vulnerable children, street children)

4. Conceptions and Misconceptions about.desc:
- Gender is a set of roles given to people because they are males or females.
- Gender roles are the different things that a male or female is considered capable of doing. It is usually influenced by societal beliefs.

- The society sees males as high performers, great achievers and should be the heads among groups of people.
- Meanwhile, they see females as weak, low achievers and responsible for performing household chores.
- Our traditions usually discriminate against girls.
- Males are heads and breadwinners of families. Provision of money for the upkeep of the family is perceived as the sole role of the father.
- Males need to be educated and not females. Society holds the thought that, since a female will be married in the future and taken care of by the husband, then there is no need for her to be educated.
- Females are seen as not having any economic responsibility.
- Females are seen to be feeble and too emotional.
- Females are not to be part of making key decisions for the family or for the community.
- Females are the ones who should perform household chores such as cooking, washing and sweeping.
ex: Gender: societal roles assigned to males and females.
Misconception: males are high performers and achievers; females are weak and low achievers.
Misconception: males are heads and breadwinners; females are for household chores.
Misconception: males need education; females do not because they will be married.
Misconception: females have no economic responsibility.
Misconception: females are too emotional for key decisions.
Result: traditions discriminate against girls.

2. Special Educational Needs (SEN).desc:
- Special Educational Needs (SEN) has generated an extensive debate in the literature on special education, and a myriad of definitions have been suggested internationally.
- The Education for Persons with Special Educational Needs Act 2004 described the SEN concept as 'a restriction in the capacity of the person to participate in and benefit from education on account of an enduring physical, sensory, mental health or learning disability or any other condition which results in a person learning differently from a person without that condition'.
- National and global efforts to guarantee inclusivity for pupils with SEN in mainstream schools are expressed in the legislation across the world, and in statements and reports from international organizations.
- Children with special education needs are 'those who have long-term physical, mental, intellectual, or sensory impairments, which in interaction with various barriers may hinder their full and effective participation in society on an equal basis with other'.
ex: SEN: restriction in capacity to participate in and benefit from education due to enduring disability.
SEN includes physical, sensory, mental health, or learning disabilities.
Children with SEN have long-term impairments that may hinder full societal participation.
International and national efforts aim to guarantee inclusivity for SEN learners.
SEN causes: genetic/biological factors and environmental factors.
Genetic: inherited from parents through genes and chromosomes.
Down syndrome: extra chromosome 21 (47 chromosomes); mental retardation, speech problems.
Fragile X Syndrome: damaged X chromosome; mental retardation, facial deformities.
Turner syndrome: XO in females; missing X chromosome.
Klinefelter syndrome: XXY in males; extra X chromosome.
Environmental causes: prenatal (maternal health, nutrition, emotions), perinatal (birth process), postnatal (after birth).

Environmental factors: maternal infections, unprescribed drugs, FAS, malnutrition, attempted abortion, lack of antenatal care, radiation, maternal emotions, trauma, maternal age, accidents, drugs, hazardous work, child abuse.
6. Causes of Special Education Needs (SE.desc:
Broadly speaking, the causes of special needs/exceptionality may be grouped under two main categories:

i. genetic/biological factors and
ii. environmental factors.

# Genetic/Biological Factors

- At conception, the newly created child receives 23 chromosomes from each parent, making a total of 46 chromosomes.
- Thousands of genes are located in the chromosomes of the ovum as well as the sperm.
- It is the genes that contain the codes of biological development of the individual. These genes transmit the hereditary characteristics to the newly conceived individual, and determine his/her inborn traits and characteristics.
- Hereditary characteristics are those that are inherited from parents, grandparents, great grandparents and other more remote ancestors.
- Genetic disorders are those that are inherited from parents.
- Variations in the chromosomes and defective genes at conception have adverse effects on the development of the human organism and may cause various degree of exceptionality.

Conditions where defective genes and chromosomal defect lead to exceptionality include:

1. Down's syndrome: results from addition or presence of an extra chromosome to the 21st pair chromosomes (47 chromosomes). Persons with Down syndrome often have mental retardation, speech problems, limited vocabulary and slow motor development.
2. Fragile X Syndrome: the 'X' chromosome of either parent is damaged and transmitted to the child. Persons have mental retardation and mild facial deformities, including enlarged ears and forehead.
3. Turner's syndrome (usually found in females): missing sex chromosome X making it (XO) instead of XX.
4. Klinefelter's syndrome (usually found in males): extra chromosome (X) making it (XXY) instead of (XY).

# Environmental Causes of Special Needs

The term environment refers to the external conditions that influence development. The child's environment consists of everything that surrounds him/her from the moment s/he begins life inside the mother's womb.

- The state of the mother during pregnancy, including her health, nutritional and emotional state, affects the child who is developing in the womb (Pre-natal causes).
- Environmental factors during (Peri-natal causes) and after birth (Post-natal causes) are also very important and influence the development of the child.

The causes include:

- Maternal infections and diseases.
- Unprescribed drugs taken by the pregnant mother.
- Fetal Alcoholic Syndrome (FAS).
- Maternal malnutrition.
- Attempted abortion.
- Insensitive to anti-natal care by the expectant mother.
- Radiation (i.e. X-ray).
- Emotional state of the mother.
- Trauma.
- Maternal age.
- Maternal accidents.
- Drugs.
- Hazardous work.
- Child abuse.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
# Introduction

Dear learner, welcome to session 4 of Unit 1. Our discussion in this session is on the forms and causes of the following disabilities; intellectual disability (ID), visual impairment (VI), hearing impairment (HI), physical disability (PD). We will also examine the physiological and environmental causes of ID, HI, VI and PD. This is going to be exciting and interesting session. Relax and enjoy your studies.

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. identify the causes of intellectual disability (ID), visual impairment (VI), hearing impairment (HI), physical disability (PD).
2. explain the characteristics of person living with intellectual disability (ID), visual impairment (VI), hearing impairment (HI), physical disability (PD)
ex: Session covers forms and causes of ID, VI, HI, and PD.
Examines physiological and environmental causes of disabilities.
Learning indicators: identify causes, explain characteristics of persons with ID, VI, HI, PD.
PD: condition interfering with ability to use body, muscles, joints, or bones.
Causes: congenital (premature birth, chromosome aberration, attempted abortion, Spina Bifida, Cerebral Palsy) or acquired (brain injuries, malnutrition, diseases like polio, German measles).
Characteristics depend on affected body part.
Cerebral Palsy: problems in voluntary movement, motor delays.
Epilepsy: temporary muscle control loss, sudden jerking.

General signs: fatigue, frequent absences, need for accommodations, poor motor coordination, limping, speech difficulty, pain during exercise, sitting/standing difficulty, low school performance, social delay.
5. Physical Disability (PD).desc:
- A physical disorder according to Kirk, Gallagher and Anastasiow (1997) is a condition that interferes with a child's ability to use his/her body.
- The child suffers from a defect that inhibits the normal exercise of his/her muscles, joints or bones.
- Physical disabilities place some limitation on a person's ability to move about, use the limbs or hands or control own movement.
- Most of these conditions affect the nervous, muscular or skeletal system and have mild, moderate or severe impact on mobility and motor skills.
- The disorder could be the result of amputation. There is no physical disorder if artificial devices are worn and the person is able to perform activities.

# Causes of Physical Disability

The causes of physical disorder can either be Congenital or Acquired.

- Congenital may be due to prenatal period or complications resulting from the birth process.
- Acquired condition arises principally from diseases and accident.

Specific causes include:
- Children born premature.
- Chromosomes aberration.
- Attempted abortion.
- Spina Bifida (SB): range of developmental birth defects that affect the spinal cord, leaving nerves open to damage.
- Cerebral Palsy: affects the way the brain controls the body's muscles resulting in speech, movement and posture difficulties; caused by brain injury or abnormal development before birth or before one month of age.
- Acquired brain injuries: occur after birth as a result of damage to the brain through accidents, strokes, tumors, infections, degenerative neurological diseases, or lack of oxygen.
- Malnutrition
- Diseases such as poliomyelitis, mother suffering from German measles.

# Characteristics of Individual with Physical Disorder

The characteristic is dependent on the part of the body which has a defect.

- Individuals with cerebral palsy experience problems in voluntary movement and delays in gross and fine motor development.
- Epilepsy causes person to lose control of the muscles temporarily; during major crisis the person experiences sudden jerking motions.
- Children with musculoskeletal conditions generally have physical limitations; may encounter difficulties holding pencil to write.

# Other Possible signs or characteristics of Physical Disability and Health Impairment

- Tire more quickly than others in the class.
- Have excessive absences due to medical appointments.
- Need for physical accommodations to participate in school activities.
- Physical presence but mental absence.
- Have poor motor control or co-ordination.
- Walk awkwardly or with a limp.
- Speech difficult to understand.
- Show signs of pain during physical exercise.
- Using a stick to walk.
- Difficulty in sitting, standing and walking.
- Due to less strength and vitality they show low ability in school work.
- Jerks in walking/ move in a shaky fashion.
- Show postural and writing defects due to skeletal deformities.
- Are socially delayed due to a lack of interaction with other children.

7. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

This session has exposed us to the forms/types of disabilities, their causes and their characteristics. We have also learnt that factors that influence the development of the child can be group into two. These are genetic/heredity and environmental influence. Genetic factors are characteristics inherited from the parents through the genes whilst environmental factors are those after conception (Pre-natal, Peri-natal and Post-natal influences). Finally, we learnt that the type of environment in which the individual is brought up also influences his/her development.

# Self-Assessment

1. Explain the following:
   - Hearing impairment (HI)
   - Intellectual disability (ID)
   - Visual impairment (VI)

2. Discuss with examples the environmental causes of SEN.

3. State FIVE (5) characteristics each of HI, ID, VI and PD and explain how they create barriers to learning.
ex: Session covered forms, causes, and characteristics of ID, VI, HI, PD.
Two main factors: genetic/heredity and environmental.
Environmental: pre-natal, peri-natal, post-natal influences.
Self-assessment: define HI, ID, VI; discuss environmental causes; list characteristics of each disability and explain learning barriers.

2. Intellectual Disability (ID).desc:
- Intellectual Disability is a condition characterized by significant limitations both in intellectual functioning and in adaptive behavior as expressed in conceptual, social, and practical adaptive skills. The disability manifest before age 18.
- The level of development of the mental/intellectual abilities of the child is known as his/her mental age.
- In the case of children who are not disabled, the chronological age and the mental age go together. Such a child is designated as having normal mental development or average intelligence.
- But in case with a child with mental retardation, there is a slowing down in the development of the mental abilities, and the mental age is lower than the chronological age.
- For example, a child who is six years old may have the mental abilities level of a three-year-old.

# Causes of Intellectual Disability

- Genetic condition such as chromosomal disorder like downs syndrome.
- Drugs used by mother during prenatal period (smoking, alcohol).
- Maternal malnutrition.
- Diseases such as syphilis, mumps, rubella (German measles), encephalitis, and meningitis.
- Over-exposure to radiation (X-ray).
- Lead poisoning: develops when a child repeatedly licks, eats or inhales chips of lead-based paints, causing anemia, seizures, brain damage.
- Severe head injuries from accident or child abuse.
- Children born premature.
- Abnormality in the position of the foetus at the start of delivery.
- Prolonged labour leading to anoxia and hypoxia.
- ID can sometimes result from problems associated with the placenta.

# Characteristics of Learners with Intellectual Disability

- Short attention span; difficulty keeping focus on a task for long.
- Unable to transfer learned information to new situation.
- Have difficulty in the use of their gross and fine motor skills especially the severe and profound ID. e.g. catching, throwing, writing, buttoning, zipping, running.
- Poor memory; easily forget what has been learnt.
- Have difficulty thinking abstractly.
- Find it difficult to complete task given them.
- Restless; difficulty sitting at one place for a long time.
- Engage in self-injurious activities such as biting part of the body, head banging.
ex: ID: significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behavior, manifest before age 18.
Mental age lower than chronological age in ID.
Causes: genetic conditions (Down syndrome), prenatal drug use, maternal malnutrition, diseases (rubella), radiation, lead poisoning, head injuries, prematurity, birth abnormalities, prolonged labour.
Characteristics: short attention span, poor transfer of learning, motor skill difficulties, poor memory, abstract thinking difficulty, task completion problems, restlessness, self-injurious behavior.
VI: inability to use vision without stress for normal tasks.
Causes: eye injury, infections (German measles), inherited conditions, malnutrition (vitamin A deficiency), drug abuse, nystagmus, strabismus, cataract, glaucoma.

Characteristics: writing mistakes, reading difficulty, dizziness, reddened eyes, light sensitivity, squinting, covering one eye, inflamed eyes, mucus formation, excessive blinking, holding books close, frequent stumbling.
3. Visual Impairment (VI).desc:
- It is a general term for a visual loss that affects learning in a school environment.
- Ocloo, Morttey and Boison, (2015) define visual impairment as a person's inability to use vision to do what the normal person can use eyesight for without stress.
- It is concerned with losses in vision.
- The term visual impairment is used to refer to the condition where an individual has a limitation or total loss in using his/her vision to perform task as expected.
- Common types of visual impairments involve problems in refraction and are typically corrected through the use of corrective glasses or lenses.

# Causes of Visual Impairment

- Injury to the eyes while playing, at work, or due to accidents.
- Infections of the eyes: viral infection like German measles transmitted from mother to developing fetus during pregnancy.
- Inherited conditions of blindness and vision impairment.
- Malnutrition e.g. lack of vitamin A.
- Drug abuse.
- Nystagmus: uncontrollable movement of the eye.
- Strabismus: visual problem in which the eyes are not aligned properly and point in different directions.
- Cataract: Clouding of part or the entire lens of the eye.
- Glaucoma: raised pressure within the eyes damaging the optic nerve.

# Characteristics of Learners with Visual Impairment

- They write with a lot of mistakes.
- They read with difficulty.
- They experience dizziness or nausea after straining their eyes.
- They read materials drawn closer to or far from them.
- They may have reddened eyes.
- They scratch their eyes.
- They are very sensitive to light and sometimes squints or frowns when gazing at object.
- Shuts or covers one eye, tilts head or thrusts head forward.
- Eyes are inflamed or watery.
- Frequent formation of mucus on the corner of the eye.
- Blinks more than usual or is irritable when doing close work.
- Holds books close to eyes.
- Stumbling or tripping often.

ex: HI: complete or partial loss of ability to hear from one or both ears.
Causes: ear infections, eardrum perforation, wax buildup, ossicle dislocation, foreign objects, otosclerosis, tumors, noise pollution, heredity, diseases (mumps, German measles), presbycusis.

Characteristics: poor attention, poor speech development, difficulty following instructions, turning head to hear, moving closer to speaker, asking for repetition, watching others for cues, preferring front row, social withdrawal, misinterpretation of non-verbal cues, frequent ear infections.
4. Hearing Impairment (HI).desc:
- World Health Organization (WHO) (2005) defined hearing impairment as complete or partial loss of ability to hear from one or both ears.
- Wilson and Waugh (1996), defined hearing impairment as a general term which describes an abnormality in the hearing levels of man. It prevents a person from perceiving sound and understanding speech.
- Hearing impairment is the inability of an individual to hear sounds adequately. This means that a person described as hearing impaired is one who has problems hearing well or using the ears to hear speech sounds.

# Causes of Hearing Impairment

- Infections of the ear canal or middle ear resulting in fluid or pus buildup
- Perforation or scarring of the eardrum
- Wax buildup
- Dislocation of the middle ear bones (ossicles)
- Foreign object in the ear canal
- Otosclerosis (an abnormal bone growth in the middle ear)
- Abnormal growths or tumors
- Noise pollution
- Heredity
- Diseases such as mumps, German measles
- Presbycusis: a hearing loss as a result of old age.

# Characteristics of the Hearing Impaired

- Poor attention; may not hear what is being said or sound is distorted.
- Poor speech development; immature, unusual or distorted speech, very loud or soft voice.
- Difficulty in following instructions; responds better when teacher is close or to written tasks.
- Cocks or turns head to one side to hear well.
- Moves nearer to a speaker.
- Frequently asks for repetition of everything that is said.
- Watches what other students are doing before starting work or looks for cues.
- Difficulty hearing text read by others or requests peers/teachers to speak louder.
- Gives inappropriate answers or fails to answer.
- Prefers to work in small groups, sit in quiet area or front row.
- Shy, withdrawn, or appears stubborn and disobedient as reaction to hearing loss.
- Reluctant to participate in oral activities, fails to laugh at jokes.
- Tends to isolate from social activities.
- Interprets facial expressions, body movements instead of spoken language, leading to false conclusions.
- Medical indications: frequent earaches, sore throats, fluid from ears.
- Puts hand over pinna of ear to hear properly.
- Poor at dictation despite good academic performance.

6. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

You have learned in this session, conceptions about inclusion education, characteristics that serve as barriers to inclusion education and how they can be addressed. We also discussed the strategies for promoting inclusion in schools. As individual, identify and reflect on your own characteristic/ attitude and how these can create potential advantages or barriers when working with pupils with SEN. Congratulation, we have come to the end of unit 1. Well done for coming this far.

# Self-Assessment

1. In your own wards explain inclusion education.

2. List 5 importance of inclusion education.

3. State and explain 5 strategies you would use in promoting inclusion in your school.
ex: Session covered: meaning of inclusion, barriers, and strategies.
Reflect on personal attitudes and how they affect work with SEN pupils.
Self-assessment: explain inclusion, list 5 importance, state and explain 5 strategies.
Inclusive education: addressing diversity of all learners through inclusive practices.
Involves changes in content, approaches, structures, and strategies.
Regular system is responsible for educating all children.
Strengthens education system capacity to reach all learners.
Requires positive attitudes from administrators, teachers, and students.
2. What is Inclusion Education?.desc:
- According to UNESCO (2005), Inclusive education is a process of addressing and responding to the diversity of needs of all learners through inclusive practices in learning, cultures and communities and reducing exclusion within and from education.
- It involves changes and modifications in content, approaches, structures and strategies, with a common vision which covers all children of the appropriate age range and a conviction that it is the responsibility of the regular system to educate all children.
- Inclusive education is a process of strengthening the capacity of the education system to reach out to all learners.
- This involves restructuring the culture, policies and practices in schools so that they can respond to the diversity of students in their locality.
- For a school to be inclusive, the attitudes of everyone in the school, including administrators, teachers, and other students, are positive towards students with disabilities.

ex: Promotes self-esteem and equal worth.
Develops awareness, sensitivity, and tolerance.
Teachers meet diverse needs through varied lessons.
Reduces stigmatization.
Effective use of teaching materials.
Enhances skill acquisition and generalization.
Encourages social relationships and friendships.
Increases parent involvement.
Raises expectations for all children.
Develops individual strengths and gifts.
Reduces financial burden (one program for all).
4. The Need for Inclusion.desc:
- Inclusion promotes the growth of self-esteem. No student wants to be singled out.
- It helps all students learn the meaning of equal worth and equal opportunities.
- Inclusive schooling can help all students learn to be aware, sensitive, and tolerant to one another.
- It helps them learn that all people have abilities and disabilities and that they need to work together to survive and be happy.
- In inclusive classrooms, teachers meet everyone's needs by presenting lessons in different ways.
- Inclusive education reduces stigmatization since all learners see themselves as one people.
- Inclusive classrooms ensure effective use of teaching and learning materials because the same resources are used to teach different ability learners.
- Inclusion enhances skill acquisition and generalization.
- It encourages social initiations, relationships and networks.
- It provides all children with opportunities to develop friendships with one another. Friendships provide role models and opportunities for growth.
- It encourages the involvement of parents in the education of their children and the activities of their local schools.
- The expectations of all the children are higher.
- Successful inclusion attempts to develop an individual's strengths and gifts.
- It helps to reduce financial burden on society and government by providing one programme for all children rather than separate programmes.

3. Barriers to Inclusion.desc:
- Negative Attitudes: Societal norms often are the biggest barrier to inclusion. Prejudices against those with SEN can lead to discrimination, which inhibits the educational process. Negative attitudes can be found at all levels: parents, community members, schools and teachers, government officials and even disabled children themselves.
- Physical barriers: In some schools, students with physical disabilities are expected to attend schools that are inaccessible to them. Many schools don't have the facilities to properly accommodate students with special needs. Environmental barriers include doors, stairs, ramps, and recreational areas, creating barriers to simply enter the school building or classroom.
- Curriculum: A rigid curriculum that does not allow for experimentation or the use of different teaching methods can be a barrier to inclusion. Study plans that do not recognize different styles of learning hinder the school experience for all students.
- Teachers: Teachers who lack the skills or who are unwilling to work with students with disability may reject inclusion. Also, lack of special education teachers to support regular school teachers.
- Language and communication: Many students are expected to learn while being taught in a language that is new and unfamiliar to them, hindering their ability to learn successfully.
- Socio-economic factors: Areas that are traditionally poor and those with higher unemployment rates tend to have schools that reflect that environment: poor facilities, students unable to afford basic necessities, poor health services, and other social factors may create barriers to the learning process.
- Funding: Adequate funding is a necessity for inclusion and yet it is rare. Schools often lack adequate facilities, qualified teachers, educational materials and general support for inclusion due to inadequate funding.
- Lack of policies to guide the development and implementation of inclusive education.
ex: Negative attitudes: biggest barrier; prejudices lead to discrimination.
Physical barriers: inaccessible buildings, stairs, lack of ramps.
Curriculum: rigid, does not allow different teaching methods or learning styles.
Teachers: lack skills or unwillingness; shortage of special education teachers.
Language and communication: instruction in unfamiliar language.
Socio-economic factors: poor facilities, inability to afford basics.
Funding: inadequate for facilities, teachers, materials.
Lack of policies for inclusive education implementation.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
# Introduction

You are welcome to session 5 of Unit 1 of this course. How did you find the previous session? I hope you found it quite interesting. Session 5 promises to be even more interesting. Here in this session will talk about providing education for all irrespective of individual characteristics. Thus, inclusion education, its meaning, barriers affecting inclusion in schools, the need for inclusion and the strategies for promoting inclusion in schools. Just relax and enjoy the session.

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. Explain the meaning of inclusion education.
2. Identify the barriers affecting inclusion education.
3. Explain the need for inclusion education.
4. Describe the strategies for promoting inclusion in schools.
ex: Session focuses on inclusive education: meaning, barriers, need, and strategies.
Learning indicators: explain inclusion, identify barriers, explain need, describe strategies.
Differentiate instruction: use visual, tactile, kinesthetic materials; adjust task difficulty.
Develop positive attitude: sets tone, accepts all learners.
Use evidence-based practices based on individual needs.
Create inclusive environment: clear communication, respectful interaction.
Include diverse content: materials reflecting underrepresented groups.
Foster learning community: encourage collaboration and cooperation.
Encourage student interactions: provide connection opportunities.
5. Strategies for Promoting Inclusion in.desc:
- Differentiate instruction: Teachers separate instructions for a variety of learners' needs by incorporating visual, tactile, and kinesthetic materials and experiences. For example, if the rest of the class is doing complex multiplication, the child with SEN may be given addition and subtraction problems at his or her level. When teachers differentiate instructions, all students can participate and work at their current abilities.
- Teachers should develop a positive attitude: Your positive attitude about inclusion sets the tone for the rest of the class. It helps you accept each learner, regardless of his or her ability.
- Teachers use evidence-based instructional practices that are based on individual student learning styles and needs.
- Create an inclusive environment: Communicate clearly for students to understand. Set and enforce ground rules for respectful interaction. Show respect for all questions and comments. Encourage students to 'think out loud' and consider different perspectives.
- Include diverse content, materials, and ideas: Select topics and materials that reflect contributions and perspectives from groups that have been historically underrepresented.
- Foster a community of learners: Encourage students to collaborate and cooperate with their classmates. As students experience the benefits of being part of a classroom community, they are more likely to participate and become more involved in learning.
- Encourage student interactions: The more academically and socially connected students feel to their colleagues, the more interactive they become. Provide opportunities for students to meet and connect with each other.

5. The Relationship between Culture, Cur.desc:
- Culture constitutes the content of education of any particular society; different societies have different educational systems with varied patterns of organization.
- The education of a given society is determined by the culture of that society.
- Education is the process by which society through schools, colleges, universities and other institutions deliberately transmits its cultural heritage.
- What schools do essentially is that they transmit the society's cultural heritage.
- R. S. Peters sees education as initiation, that is, initiation into the culture of the particular society into which a child is born.
- Culture is the content of education and it is through the process of education that culture is transmitted to the members of society.
- Sociologically, education and socialization mean essentially the same thing. They are processes through which tradition, norms and cultural values of a society are passed on to the up-and-coming members.
- Education transmits culture through formal and informal curriculum.
- The culture determines the content to be included in the curriculum of a particular nation and Education serves as a means of transmitting the knowledge and content from generation to generation.
ex: Culture determines content of education (curriculum).
Education transmits cultural heritage through institutions.
Peters: education is initiation into culture.
Education and socialization: processes of passing on traditions, norms, values.
Education transmits culture via formal and informal curriculum.
Culture determines curriculum content; education transmits it across generations.

4. What is Culture?.desc:
- The word 'culture' derives its root from French and Latin 'colere', which means to tend to the earth and grow, or cultivation and nurture.
- One of the earliest anthropological and sociological definitions of culture was given by E. B. Taylor in 1891 as 'that complex whole which includes knowledge, belief, art, morals, law custom and any other capabilities and habits acquired by man as a member of society'.
- This definition views culture as a body or corpus of ideas and knowledge to be handed on and education is seen as the means to help in the accurate assimilation of this corpus.
- Another definition of culture has been given by George F. Kneller. According to him, culture is the total shared way of life of a given people, comprising their modes of thinking, acting and feeling, which are expressed for instance, in religion, law, language, art and custom as well as in material products, such as houses, clothes and tools.
- Several points can be made from this definition:
  - The total shared way of life is the behaviour of the people including their way of thinking and doing which have to be learned.
  - The material objects also represent the culture, being the results of thinking and doing.
  - Archeologists reconstruct ways of life from material relics.
  - There is need to share and transmit the elements of culture.
ex: Culture from Latin 'colere': to tend, cultivate, nurture.
E.B. Taylor (1891): complex whole including knowledge, belief, art, morals, law, custom, capabilities acquired as society member.
Kneller: total shared way of life including modes of thinking, acting, feeling expressed in religion, law, language, art, custom, and material products.
Culture includes behavior (learned) and material objects.
Requires sharing and transmission.

3. Concept of Curriculum.desc:
- The term curriculum has been derived from a Latin word 'Currere' which means a 'race course' or a runway on which one runs to reach a goal.
- Accordingly, a curriculum is the instructional and the educative programme by following which the pupils achieve their goals, ideals and aspirations of life.
- The term curriculum refers to the lessons and academic content taught in a school or in a specific course or program.
- In dictionaries, curriculum is often defined as the courses offered by a school, but it is rarely used in such a general sense in schools.
- Depending on how broadly educators define or employ the term, curriculum typically refers to:
  - The knowledge and skills students are expected to learn, including learning standards or objectives.
  - The units and lessons that teachers teach.
  - The assignments and projects given to students.
  - The books, materials, videos, presentations and readings used in a course.
  - The tests, assessments, and other methods used to evaluate student learning.
- Curriculum may also encompass a school's academic requirements for graduation, such as the courses students have to take and pass, the number of credits students must complete and other requirements.
ex: Curriculum from Latin 'currere': race course to reach a goal.
Instructional programme for achieving life goals and aspirations.
Refers to lessons, academic content taught in school.
Includes knowledge, skills, learning objectives, units, lessons, assignments, materials, assessments.
Encompasses graduation requirements: courses, credits.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
# Introduction

Dear learner, in this session we shall look at the Meaning and Relationship between Education, Curriculum and Culture. We shall also discuss the role of various philosophical and sociological perspectives that have influenced education in Ghana.

# Learning Indicators

1. Explain the relationships between culture, education and the curriculum using a concentric model.
2. Discuss the role of the various philosophical and sociological perspectives that have influenced education in Ghana.
ex: Session covers meaning and relationship between education, curriculum, and culture.
Discusses philosophical and sociological perspectives influencing Ghanaian education.
Learning indicators: explain relationships using concentric model, discuss influencing perspectives.
Culture must be transmitted and preserved through education.
Culture is content of education (curriculum).
Education is process of transmitting culture.
Self-assessment: define education, curriculum, culture; explain their relationship using concentric model.
6. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

For a society to continue to exist, its culture must be transmitted and preserved through education. Education is a necessary condition for this transmission and conservation of culture. We have look at the meaning and relationship among culture, curriculum and education. Culture, is the content of education called curriculum and it is transmitted in the process of education.

# Self-Assessment

1. Explain the terms:
   a. Education
   b. Curriculum
   c. Culture

2. Explain the relationship between education, curriculum and culture using a concentric model.

ex: Education from Latin 'Educatum': to lead out inner abilities.
Function: draw out potentialities, not just impart knowledge.
From 'educere' (development) and 'educare' (enhancement).
Education is a process, not a product.
Systematic process of acquiring knowledge, skills, attitudes.
Goal: make individual fit into society.
Key to solving societal problems.
2. What is Education?.desc:
- Etymologically, the word 'Education' has been derived from the Latin word 'Educatum' which again comprises two words 'e' and 'duco' meaning 'out of' and 'to lead' respectively.
- The word 'Educatum' means to lead out the inside to outside (to bring out the inner abilities).
- The function of education is therefore to draw out rather than to put in. This means through education inner capacities or potentialities of the pupil are brought out, developed and highlighted. To impart knowledge is not so important.
- Educationists also trace out the root of education in two other Latin words 'educere' and 'educare' the former meaning development or bringing out and the later meaning enhancement, improvement and progress.
- The word 'education' means the art of developing and enhancing the cultivation of various physical, mental and moral powers of the child. Education as such is to be taken as a [process], not as a product.
- Education therefore is a systematic process through which a child or an adult acquires knowledge, experience, skill and sound attitude.
- It makes an individual civilized, refined, cultured and educated.
- For a civilized and socialized society, education is the only means. Its goal is to make an individual fit into the society in which he lives.
- Every society gives importance to education because it is the key to solve the various societal problems.

ex: Philosophy from Greek 'philos' (love) and 'sophia' (wisdom) = love of wisdom.
Educational Philosophy: comprehensive theories of education.
Statement clarifying beliefs, values about education.
Influences what and how subjects are taught, including implicit values.
Philosophical process of solving educational problems.
2. Meaning of Educational Philosophy.desc:
- The word philosophy comes from a combination of two Greek words- 'philos' meaning 'love' and 'sophia' meaning 'wisdom.' Etymologically, philosophy means the love of wisdom.
- The phrase 'Philosophy of Education' has been used to replace the phrase 'Educational Philosophy'.
- 'Educational Philosophy' stands for comprehensive theories of education. It also refers to the general theories which try to deal with education, like metaphysics and the reality.
- A philosophy of education is a statement that identifies and clarifies the beliefs, values and understanding of an individual or group with respect to education.
- A philosophy of education may influence what subjects or topics are taught, how they are taught, and perhaps more importantly, the supporting beliefs and values that are taught, both implicitly and explicitly, within and around the core curriculum.
- In brief, educational philosophy is a philosophical process of solving educational problems through philosophical method, from a philosophical attitude to arrive at philosophical conclusions and results. Thus, it aims at achieving general as well as comprehensive results.

4. Relevance of Educational Philosophy.desc:
- By learning philosophy, a teacher would be able to view and analyze from the perspective of their students. Apart from understanding why students are behaving in a particular way, teachers would also be able to know how students perceive their actions.

# a. Determining the aims of education

- Philosophy of education provides original ideas regarding all aspects of education particularly educational aims.
- Educational philosophy gives different views, but this situation is not harmful, rather it helps in providing education according to the need of society.
- The difference in view of philosophy of education reflects the multiplicity and diversities of human life.
- Philosophy of education guides the process of education by suggesting suitable aims from the diversities of life and selecting the means accordingly.

# b. Harmonizing old and new traditions in the field of education

- In the process of social development, the old traditions become outdated and are replaced by new traditions. But this process of replacement is not always smooth, faced with opposition from orthodox sections.
- At the same time, every 'old' is not outdated and every 'new' is not perfect. Therefore, there is a need of co-ordinating the two to maintain harmony. This function can be performed by philosophy of education.

# c. Providing vision to achieve educational development

- Philosophy of education provides the educational planners, administrators and educators with the right vision which guides them to attain the educational goals efficiently.

# d. Preparing the young generation to face challenges of modern time

- Social commentators have given many labels to the present period: information age, post modernity, age of uncertainty, age of globalization.
- Philosophy of education is a guiding, steering and liberating force that helps young people and society at large to face the challenges of the modern time.
ex: Helps teachers understand student perspectives and perceptions.
Determines aims of education: provides ideas for educational aims.
Reflects multiplicity and diversities of human life.
Harmonizes old and new traditions in education.
Provides vision for planners, administrators, educators.
Prepares youth to face modern challenges (globalization, information age).
Three branches: metaphysics, epistemology, axiology.
Metaphysics: study of reality, nature of being, existence, free will.
Axiology: study of art and beauty (aesthetics).
Ethics: study of right and wrong actions, moral problems.
Epistemology: study of knowledge, distinguishes true from false knowledge.
Epistemology relates to scientific methodology and cognition.
Branches overlap.
3. Nature of Educational Philosophy.desc:
- Philosophy of education is one of the areas of applied philosophy. There are three branches of philosophy namely 'metaphysics, epistemology and axiology.

# Metaphysics

- Metaphysics is a branch of philosophy that investigates principles of reality transcending those of any particular science. It is concerned with explaining the fundamental nature of being and the world.
- Metaphysics is the study of the nature of things. Metaphysicians ask what kinds of things exist, and what they are like. They reason about such things as whether or not people have free will, in what sense abstract objects can be said to exist, and how it is that brains are able to generate minds.

# Axiology

- Axiology is the branch of philosophical enquiry that explores the study of basic philosophical questions about art and beauty. Sometimes philosophy of art is used to describe only questions about art, with 'aesthetics' the more general term.

# Ethics

- Ethics: the study of what makes actions right or wrong, and of how theories of right action can be applied to special moral problems. Sub disciplines include meta-ethics, value theory, theory of conduct, and applied ethics.

# Epistemology

- Epistemology is the branch of philosophy that studies knowledge. It attempts to answer the basic question: what distinguishes true (adequate) knowledge from false (inadequate) knowledge?
- Practically, this question translates into issues of scientific methodology: how can one develop theories or models that are better than competing theories?
- It also forms one of the pillars of the new sciences of cognition, which developed from the information processing approach to psychology, and from artificial intelligence.
- When we look at the history of epistemology, we can discern a clear trend from a static, passive view of knowledge towards a more and more adaptive and active one.

- As you can tell, the different branches of philosophy overlap one another.

ex: Session covered meaning and relevance of educational philosophy and aims.

Ghana promotes education at basic, secondary, tertiary levels.

5. Meaning of Educational Aims.desc:
- Although the noun forms of the three words aim, objective and goal are often used synonymously, professionals in organized education define the words aim and objective more narrowly and consider them to be distinct from each other.
- Aims are concerned with [purpose] whereas objectives are concerned with [achievement].
- Usually, an educational objective relates to gaining an ability, a skill, some knowledge, a new attitude etc. rather than having merely completed a given task.
- Since the achievement of objectives usually takes place during the course and the aims look forward into the student's career and life beyond the course, one can expect the aims of a course to be relatively more long term than the objectives of that same course.
- Sometimes an aim sets a goal for the teacher to achieve in relation to the learners, sometimes course aims explicitly list long-term goals for the learner and at other times there is a joint goal for the teacher and learner to achieve together.
- While the aim may be phrased as a goal for the teacher within the scope of the course it can also imply goals for the learner beyond the duration of the course.

6. Relevance of Aims of Education.desc:
- Education is an essential tool in every modern society because it has a long-term impact on the way a society is built and developed.
- It acts as a platform for imparting skills and knowledge to the entities of the society.
- From a more holistic point of view, education is necessary to make human beings more rational and informed members of society.
- A member of a society who is educated has the potential to contribute more to the society as compared to an uneducated member.
- It also helps a person to contribute and participate in the developments that the society undergoes at different points in time.
- Education is one of the core pillars of human society which play a major role in its progress.

# 1. Education provides knowledge

- Education provides one with knowledge and as everyone knows 'Knowledge is Power'. When a person is educated, he has the power of freedom, choice, information, expression and decision making.

# 2. Education helps to build a better society

- Educated people can contribute more efficiently to a society as compared to the uneducated people. In Ghana, providing the right to education would improve the literacy rate of the population.

# 3. Education helps to build career

- Formal education is the primary tool which defines the career path and career choices of an individual. A stable and fulfilling career is important for both individual members and society as a whole.

# 4. Education helps in earning a living

- Earning a living is one of the primary requirements of human beings. Education makes people more skilled, knowledgeable and literate enough to compete in labour markets.

# 5. Education helps to make informed choices

- Education makes a human being more aware and rational. It gives him the ability to fulfil his/her dreams. Ghana recognizes the importance of education to improve standards of living and develop human resources.

# 6. Education helps us to find our purpose

- Education gives one the wings to fly and the courage to pursue one's dreams. The importance of quality education is reflected in the quality of life of a society's people.

# 7. Education empowers people

- In today's world, making choices in professional and personal life becomes more challenging. An educated person is expected to make more rational and correct choices because he has the necessary knowledge.

# 8. Education is a tool for national development

- The presence of educated citizens can help to achieve multidirectional growth and development in the society covering economic, socio-cultural, technological and political aspects of regional and national development.

# 9. Education helps in personal development

- Education imparts knowledge, awareness and proper judgment which make a person more confident and prepared to meet challenges. Education is about being able to distinguish between right and wrong and being aware of multiple facets of human life and society.

- Today, education has become an indispensable aspect of a civilized society. The importance of education is being highlighted in all countries across the world. Developing countries like Ghana are focusing on educating their citizens with the aim of achieving sustainable social and economic development.

7. Strengths of Traditional African Educ.desc:
- African Traditional Education was comprehensive in nature and therefore holistically develops every aspect of the personality of the individual i.e. individuals produced under this system are physically, emotionally, intellectually, economically, politically, socially and morally as well as religiously and spiritually prepared for life.
- African traditional education developed in the members of society, natural collective consciousness i.e. each was his/her brother's keeper and support for one another in all situation was assured.
- The education made everybody a functional member of the society, thereby minimizing unemployment and social unrest.
- Affordable in terms of cost. Every responsible adult member of the society had the responsibility of a teacher and as such there was nothing like shortage of teachers.
- Integrative as it prepared the individual to fit into society.
- A lot of moral lessons were learnt by young people.
- Life-long and rewarding.
- It had no drop outs.

5. Teachers in Traditional Africa Educat.desc:
- In traditional African Education, the entire society, family members and all responsible adult in society are teachers.
- It is important, however, to note that there were areas that needed specialized skills whose training needed special facilities and also took a long time.

We can mention vocations like:

- blacksmithing and goldsmithing.
