3. Aims of Traditional Education.desc:
# Development of Character

- Indigenous African education places considerable emphasis on character-training. Indeed, it is the corner-stone of African education.
- The parents, siblings and other members of the community participate in the education of the child.
- Everyone wants their child to be sociable, honest, courageous, humble, persevering and of good reputation at all times.

# Respect for Elders and Peers

- Closely related to character-training is the respect for elders or those who are in authority, particularly the chief, relatives (especially uncles) and other neighbours.
- Greetings play a major role here. The African has a complicated greeting system. There are special polite greetings or salutations for parents, elders, peers, and chiefs. There are morning, afternoon and evening greetings among others.

# Political Training

- In African Traditional societies both males and females have distinct political roles to play and are therefore nurtured into the knowing and appreciation of their political roles.

# Physical training

- In traditional African society the child naturally jumps, climbs a tree, dances or performs a balancing act because his siblings or his elders do the same.
- Every child discovers his limbs and in no time at all he/she also discovers their uses and engage in other activities like games, running, wrestling etc.

# Intellectual training

- Traditional African education can be said to encourage intellectual growth and development.
- The African child learns the local geography and history of his community.
- Local history is taught by the elders in each household and the songs of praise which accompany many of the historical events make the oral traditional history a stimulating experience which is hard to forget.

# Economic/Vocational training

Such traditional vocations can be divided into:

1. Agricultural education: for example, farming, fishing and veterinary science (animal care and animal rearing).
2. Trades and crafts: for example, weaving (baskets and cloth), smithing (iron, silver, gold, etc.), hunting, and carving. Sculpturing, painting and decorating, carpentry, building, barbering, drumming, dancing, hair plaiting, dress-making, leatherworking, soap-making, singing, pottery-making, mat-making, bead-working etc.
3. Professions: for example, native doctors, priests, village heads, chiefs etc.

# Religious Preparation

- Religion covers every aspect of the life of the African and this can be seen from the names of persons and towns, names of symbols, greetings etc.
- As children participate in festival and other local ceremonies and worship, they are trained religiously.
ex: Character development: cornerstone; parents and community participate.
Respect for elders: complex greeting system for different categories.
Political training: distinct roles for males and females.
Physical training: jumping, climbing, games, running, wrestling.
Intellectual training: local geography, history, oral traditions.
Vocational training: agricultural (farming, fishing), trades/crafts (weaving, smithing, carving), professions (native doctors, priests, chiefs).
Religious preparation: through festivals, ceremonies, worship.

# Introduction

Student teachers will be exposed to the historical, sociological and ideological issues that influenced the various changing phases of Ghana's educational system before independence. We shall discuss the rationale, philosophy and aims of traditional (indigenous education).

6. Methods of Teaching in Tradition Afri.desc:
In Traditional African Education, children learned a lot through:

- Observation: children closely watched adults as they went about daily responsibilities.
- Imitation: young ones copied adult behaviour.
- Participation: learners took part in the activities of adults.
ex: Observation: watching adults perform daily tasks.
Imitation: copying adult behaviour.
Participation: taking part in adult activities.

8. Weaknesses of Traditional African Edu.desc:
- African Traditional Education in its true sense of word is indigenous and local; as such, most of the knowledge acquired through it could only be used in the local environments and not meet the demands of another environment.
- Much attention was focused on group as against individual development.
- The knowledge, skills and values were so localised and could not be applied or appreciated outside a given community.
- There was little room for a new career for the individual. In effect, there was lack of motivation to change one's environment.
- It was so rigid or conservative and consequently, resistant to change. Consequently, new ideas innovations were not readily embraced.
- No figures or letters were learnt to prepare them for further encounter with the outside world.
- African traditional education by its nature exhibited the problem of lack of the sense of comparison and estimation. This weakness is still evident among Ghanaian illiterates; one says 'just there' and that will be about three kilometres away.
ex: Knowledge localized; not applicable in other environments.
Focus on group rather than individual development.
Skills and values not transferable outside community.
Little room for new careers; lack of motivation for change.
Rigid, conservative, resistant to innovation.
No literacy or numeracy for engagement with outside world.
Lack of sense of comparison and estimation (e.g., vague distances).

4. Characteristics of Traditional Africa.desc:
- African traditional education was non-literate - it did not involve reading and writing.
- African traditional education was indigenous in nature - the educational goals, methods and content information to be learned differed from one local area to another.
- African traditional education embraced all aspect of the personality of the individual - e.g. physical, social, economic, political, religious.
- African traditional education was closely related to the life in the community.
- There was no prescribed timetable or curriculum.
- There were no examinations or awarding of certificates.
ex: Non-literate: no reading or writing.
Indigenous: goals and methods varied by locality.
Holistic: embraced physical, social, economic, political, religious aspects.
Community-related: closely tied to community life.
No prescribed timetable or curriculum.
No examinations or certificates.
Traditional/indigenous education: Ghana's original way of nurturing youth.
Definition: process of transmitting culture, knowledge, skills, values.
Rationale: embrace social, moral, spiritual, intellectual, psychomotor development.
Provides occupational training based on locality (fishing in riverside, farming in forest).
Philosophy: learner as receptacle, teacher as possessor of knowledge making all decisions.
2. Nature of Traditional African Educati.desc:
- Have you heard of the traditional education before? I hope your answer is yes, Good.
- Let us explain what traditional or indigenous education is.
- Education therefore is a systematic process through which a child or an adult acquires knowledge, experience, skill and sound attitude.
- It makes an individual civilized, refined, cultured and educated.
- For a civilized and socialized society, education is the only means. Its goal is to make an individual fit into the society in which he lives.
- Every society gives importance to education because it is the key to solve the various societal problems.

# The Rationale of Traditional Africa Education

- Ghanaians had their own way of nurturing the young ones into responsible adults. This form of education has been described as the traditional or informal education.
- The traditional or indigenous education can be defined as a process by which an existing society transmitted learnt culture, knowledge, skills and values from one individual to another.
- The rationale and philosophy underpinning traditional education is to embrace the social, moral, spiritual, intellectual and psychomotor development of young ones and to nurture them in all activities and behaviours that were relevant to the stability and improvement of the society.
- Traditional education provides for occupational and professional training. For instance, fishing is emphasized in the riverside areas while farming is emphasized in the forest areas.
- The main philosophy behind traditional view of education is that the learner is a receptacle and the teacher is the possessor of knowledge, he has knowledge about what doses of knowledge the child needs. He/she makes all the decisions about the learning situation.

6. Challenges Faced by Castle Schools.desc:
Being a new thing altogether, there were going to be several challenges. Here are some of them:

- There was poor enrolment. The local people refused to get their wards enrolled in the castle schools.
- Can you think of any reasons why they did not cooperate with the castle school authorities? One reason was that the children would be losing their cultural identity if they were converted into Christianity.
- The second reason was that if girls got educated, they would not respect their uneducated husbands.
- The Anglo-Dutch wars created a state of insecurity along the coast. These also disturbed effective and continued academic work.
- The Asante-Fante wars also created instability along the coast.
- Later when Africans, like Philip Quaque ran the Cape Coast castle school, he was poorly supported financially.

3. Aim of Establishing the Castle School.desc:
Dear learner, I am sure what is bothering your mind now is why the European traders shifted some of their attention to education when in actual sense their main objective was trade.

We hope your answer is captured in the suggested reasons given below:

1. The European traders had children with the African women and they considered it a moral obligation to give their children (mulattos) the type of education found in Europe.
2. Training the sons of local chiefs and wealthy traders.
3. The merchants needed interpreters, clerks and trade representatives for their trading activities and therefore needed to train such people through the schools.
4. The schools were used as a tool for the spreading of Christianity and also for colonizing the local people.
ex: Aim 1: Educate mulatto children (offspring of European traders and African women).
Aim 2: Train sons of local chiefs and wealthy traders.
Aim 3: Produce interpreters, clerks, and trade representatives.
Aim 4: Spread Christianity and facilitate colonization.

16th century: European settlers introduced formal education in castles.

2. Castle Schools.desc:
- Pre-independence is a period before Ghana attained freedom from the colonial masters. This period saw a rapid transformation in the history of educational system in Ghana, particularly; teacher education.
- Do you know what brought about this transformation? Before the colonial times, education in Ghana was informal with knowledge and competencies transmitted through apprenticeships and orally by older men and women (Ameyaw & Sarpong, 2011).
- During the 16th century when European settlers arrived in the country, new forms of learning called formal education was introduced in the form schools. This schools were established in the castles hence the name castle schools.
- Castle schools were the types of schools established in the castles along the coast of Ghana. These schools were established by the European trading merchants and companies. This establishment marked the introduction of formal education in this country.
- The first castle school was founded in 1529 in the Elmina Castle through an order from King George III of Portugal.
- Between 1529 and 1794, several efforts were made by the Portuguese, the Dutch and English to found schools in the castles at different periods. Though these schools experienced difficult moments, their presence made a big impression in the beginning of literacy on the country.
- The prominent castles that provided education for African children were Cape Coast, Elmina and Christiansburg at Osu.
- The Dutch, in 1637, started a school in the Elmina castle for African children.
- Between 1712 and 1794, the British Royal African Company, a trading company, ran the Cape Coast castle school under different teachers, who were mostly reverend ministers, who often accompanied the merchants to Africa.

7. Achievements of the Castle Schools.desc:
- The school introduced the idea of full-time qualified teachers who are paid for their work.
- A good number of brilliant students enjoyed the idea of scholarship abroad for further studies. Names like Jacobus Capitain, Anthon Wilhelm Amo (Anthony William Amo), and Philip Quaque could be mentioned as some of the beneficiaries.
- Wilhelm Amo studied and later obtained a doctorate degree in Germany. He lectured at the Universities of Hall, Jena and Wurttenburg in Germany.
- The castle schools were founded along the coast of Ghana. The trading merchants used the schools to educate and evangelize. Mulattoes and children of wealthy African merchants were the students.
- The schools trained clerks and interpreters as well.
- The curriculum comprised the 3Rs and religious instruction.
- There was poor enrolment because of insecurity and disinterest.
- They marked the beginning of literacy and western education in Ghana.
- Jacobus Capitein, Wilhelm Amo and Philip Quaque and others went abroad for further studies.
ex: Introduced full-time qualified teachers paid for work.
Provided scholarships abroad for brilliant students (Jacobus Capitein, Wilhelm Amo, Philip Quaque).
Wilhelm Amo earned doctorate in Germany, lectured at German universities.
Trained clerks and interpreters.
Marked beginning of literacy and western education in Ghana.
Beneficiaries studied abroad and achieved academic success.

I know many of you would describe the experience as memorable. Very good, in this session you will be introduced to the emergence of schools in the castles, the rationale, philosophy and aims of the establishment of castles schools their curriculum, the products, challenges and achievements.

4. The Reasons for the Introduction of W.desc:
- In the first place since the white man and the African are from different cultures, and therefore cannot communicate effectively, there was the need to find the best way to interact. There was therefore the need for interpreters to be trained to facilitate communication.
- Clerks were needed to keep records.
- Moreover, the merchants, who were mostly from Portugal, Holland and England, came along with priests who served as chaplains in the castles. One of their major objectives was to convert the African to Christianity. The African had always been considered 'heathen'. Western education was the tool to be used to facilitate the spread of Christianity in Africa.
- Human beings will continue to be human despite our colour or how we perceive issues and wherever we find ourselves. Despite the fact that the African could not speak the white man's language and was even considered not 'civilized', the white man could not resist the beauty of the African woman.
- Many of the traders had relations with the local women and produced a crossbreed of children; mulatto is the term used to describe children between white and black parents. They are often called the half-caste.
- If you ever visited places like Cape Coast, Elmina, Osu in Accra and other coastal towns, you would see a lot of these fair-skinned individuals who are descendants of these mulattoes.
- The castle schools were established so that these children could be offered western education. Some children of the wealthy African merchants were also admitted into the castle schools to undergo western education.
ex: Need for interpreters to facilitate communication between Europeans and Africans.
Need for clerks to keep records.
Spread Christianity; convert Africans considered 'heathen'.
European traders had children with African women (mulattoes/half-caste).
Castle schools established to educate mulatto children and children of wealthy African merchants.
Descendants of mulattoes still visible in coastal towns today.

6. The Wesleyan Missionary Society.desc:
- The Wesley missionaries opened their first school in the existing British settlement at Cape Coast, Discove, Anomabu and Accra.
- They also opened schools inland and by 1880, the Wesley mission had more schools than any other body. The mission had 83 schools with an enrolment of over 3,000.
- This dynamic pioneer missionary society started its activities in Cape Coast in 1835.
- Like the other early missionaries, Reverend Thomas Dunwell, Mr. & Mrs. Wrigley and Mr. & Mrs. Harrop died a few months after arriving in the country.
- Reverend Thomas Birch Freeman, who arrived in 1838, however, survived to start the activities of the society.
- The activities of the Wesleyan mission were mainly around Cape Coast, Anomabu and Saltpond and later Accra and Kumasi.

# Teacher Training Efforts

- When the Wesley mission found that their enrolment figures were increasing, they saw the need to train teachers in their schools.
- Rev Thomas Birch Freeman started a theological seminary in Accra in 1842 to prepare two students to become teachers. The school was headed by Rev Samuel Shipman.
- This effort at teacher training was abandoned due to the death of Shipman in 1842 and the paucity of students.
- The Wesleyan mission decided to abandon the idea of training teachers locally in favour of training them abroad at the Fourah Bay College in Sierra Leone. They continued this until 1918.
- In 1918, the Aburi 'Kemp', originally established as a girls' school, was chosen as an institution for the training of teachers and catechists.
- This teacher training institution was moved to Kumasi in 1924 to be known as Wesley College, with 26 students (five training as ministers, twelve as teachers, nine as catechists).

# Educational Institutions

- In 1876, Mfantsipim School came into being. The first headmaster was Reverend W.T. Balmer.
- Wesley Girls High School was founded in 1925.
- Wesley College, formerly at Aburi, was relocated in Kumasi in 1924.
- The medium of instruction in most basic Wesleyan schools was English.

# Prominent Products

- Some prominent products of the Wesleyan mission were:
  - Kofi Asaam and E.S. Hayford (trained as teachers for Cape Coast School)
  - Reverend Attoh-Ahumah
  - George Ekem Ferguson
  - Reverend Egyir-Asaam
  - J. Mensah Sarbah and J.E. Casely Hayford (products of Cape Coast High School, became political icons)
  - Dr. James Emman Kwegyir Aggrey of Africa, the great Ghanaian scholar, was a product of the Wesleyan school in Cape Coast.

7. The Bremen (North German) Missionary.desc:
- The society started work in Togo, and by 1880, it had only four schools in the part of Togo that became part of the Gold Coast Colony.
- It was not until the First World War of 1914-18 that part of Togo and consequently its schools came under the Gold Coast Government's control.
- Its pioneers did not start their work from existing European-controlled bases on the coast. They went directly some seventy miles inland to Peki in Eweland at the invitation of the chief.
- Their attempt at development of education was not easy due to constant death of the missionaries, distance from the coast, and outbreaks of tribal wars.
- The missionaries made their next attempt at developing education at Keta in 1855. From Keta they steadily advanced inland once more.

# Educational Work Pattern

The educational work of the Bremen missionary society in Togoland was similar in pattern to that of the Basel mission in the Gold Coast:
- They emphasized trade instructions, particularly in building.
- All the main mission stations had seven-class schools.
- After a hill-station was opened at Amedzofe in 1890, a seminary was established there to train teacher-catechists. These teacher-catechists went out to open single-class village schools.
- By 1906, about 3,000 pupils were attending Bremen missionary schools in the Gold Coast.

# Geographic Focus

- These missionaries from Germany concentrated their activities in Togoland and modern-day Volta region of Ghana. They evangelized and provided education at Peki, Ho, Kpando, Hohoe, Keta and Amedzofe.
- The Reverend Lorenz Woff is greatly remembered for his pioneering work in the region.

# Achievements

- By 1906, the mission had about 3,000 pupils attending its schools.
- In 1890, a seminary was opened at Amedzofe to train teachers and catechists. The seminary is now serving as the premises of Amedzofe Teacher Training College.
- Schetyel produced the first Ewe grammar in 1858.
- In 1905, Westermann produced the Ewe dictionary.
- One notable achievement of the Bremen missionary education was their preference for the use of the vernacular in schools. The Bible was translated into Ewe and the language was developed into writing.

4. The Basel Mission.desc:
- The Basel mission originated from Germany but had its headquarters in Basel, Switzerland.
- Governor Richelieu of Christiansburg Castle (1822-25) returned to Denmark and requested that educational and missionary work at the castle should be put on a permanent basis.
- In 1828, at the invitation of Governor Richelieu of Christiansburg (Osu), four pioneer missionaries were sent down to evangelize and establish schools. Governor Richelieu took part in the teaching himself.
- The Basel missionary arrived a few years before the Wesleyan missionaries. They opened the first boy's school at Akropong in 1843, and girls' school in 1847. The girls' school was re-located to Aburi in 1854. This school remained at Aburi till 1930 when it became the nucleus of the training college and secondary school.
- By 1880, the Basel mission had established 45 schools with 1,200 pupils.

# Principles of Basel Mission Education

The Basel missionaries realized that:
- Any thorough system of education depended on the supply of trained teachers.
- Girls' education is just as important as that of boys'.
- Training of the individual must not be confined to academic subject alone.

These three principles were the foundation of Basel mission education.

# Educational Developments

- Five years after the boys' school at Akropong, a seminary was started at the same town in 1848 for the training of catechists and teachers.
- Another seminary was opened at Abetifi in 1896. In 1924, this seminary was merged with the one at Akropong.
- Brighter pupils from primary schools were sent to middle schools at Akropong, Christiansburg and Begoro for three years.
- Those wishing to be teachers or catechists had one-year preparatory course before entering seminaries.
- Teachers were given a two-year course in the teacher training school.

# Girls' Education

- Although the Basel mission met great opposition, by 1918 their schools in Akuapem area had almost as many girls as boys.
- The ratio for all their schools was seven boys to one girl, compared to six to one in government and seven to one in Wesleyan schools.
- This supported Governor Guggisburg's comment that 'nothing is more detrimental to the progress of these race than the old system of educated husbands and illiterate wives'.
- It also added meaning to Dr. Kwegyir Aggrey's saying: 'when you educate a man you educate an individual but when you educate a woman you educate the whole nation'.

# Technical Education

- The Basel mission made effort in technical education, opening industrial establishment in Christiansburg where joiners, carpenters, locksmiths, blacksmiths, shoemakers and book binders were trained.
- This made people self-supporting and improved standard of living and the building industry.

# Other Significant Contributions

- Unfortunately, all the four pioneer missionaries died. The sole survivor, Reverend Andreas Riis moved from Christiansburg to Akropong in 1835, marking the beginning of the mission's work in Ghana.
- In 1848, the first teacher training college was opened at Akropong, Akwapim.
- The mission adopted the vernacular as the medium of instruction in its schools.
- The mission made Twi and Ga written languages.
- J.G. Christaller, between 1853-1868, translated the whole Bible in 1871 to Twi and created a Twi dictionary.
- Zimmermann translated the four gospels into Ga, produced a Ga grammar and dictionary and translated the whole Bible into Ga.
- Basic education was divided into primary and middle schools.
- The curriculum was improved at the middle school with introduction of subjects like history and Greek and Hebrew in some schools for catechists.
- Vocational and technical education was encouraged with studying of subjects like agriculture, woodwork, blacksmithing, bookbinding, metalwork, shoemaking, pottery and painting.
- Girls were encouraged to learn needlework, dressmaking and home science.

5. The Roman Catholic Mission.desc:
- In 1573, the first attempt was made by the Augustanian Friars in the Gold Coast to start evangelical work and open schools. Their efforts were short-lived as the people of Komenda and Eguafo murdered all the missionaries.
- It was not until 1880 that Fathers Argoste Moreau and Eugene Morat arrived in Elmina to set the foundation for Catholic evangelism.
- The activities of the mission within a short period had been extended to Cape Coast, Amisano, Agona, Komenda and Shama.
- The Sisters of Our Lady of Apostles (OLA) and Fathers of the Society of African Missions (SMA) spread catholic activities to the Volta region.
- The activities of the mission spread to the north through the white Fathers Mission.

# Important Activities in the 1880s

- By 1881, the first catholic school had been founded in Elmina by Fathers Morat and Moreau. At a point there were 150 students.
- In 1884, another school was founded by Our Lady of Apostles sisters in Elmina to offer opportunities for girls.
- In 1889, Father Granier and Wilhem Haizel, an African, founded a Boys' school in Cape Coast.
- OLA sisters opened another Girls' school in Cape Coast. The subjects read included sewing and housecraft.
- It is gratifying to note that the Catholics were also interested in the use of the vernacular in their schools, like the Basel.
- The schools founded by the Roman Catholic missions have survived the 19th century to become great institutions in modern Ghana today.

2. Missionaries' Schools.desc:
- The term 'missionaries' refer to a body or a group of individuals who are Christians and are committed to converting non-Christians to Christianity through the medium of evangelization and education.
- It is expedient to conclude by saying that the missionaries laid the front for educational development in the country for the colonial and postcolonial governments to build upon. Thus, missionary education has significant impact on present system of education.
- We shall look at the activities of five pioneer missionary societies, which are:

1. The Society for the Propagation of the Gospel (SPG).
2. The Basel Mission.

3. The Society for the Propagation of th.desc:
- The Anglican Church started in Ghana as the Society for the Propagation of the Gospel (SPG). The missionaries came from the Church of England in 1752.
- Reverend Thomas Thompson, an English priest who used to work with slaves on the Plantations in North America and West Indies was the first minister of the society in Ghana.
- At the age of fourteen, the young Ghanaian Philip Quaque was sent to Islington, England to be trained as a minister of the society.
- Philip graduated with a Master of Arts degree from Oxford and became a minister (Priest) of the SPG. Philip returned to the Cape Coast castle to work as a teacher and priest.

# Philip's work suffered because:

- The society could not sufficiently support his school financially. Even his salary was in arrears of £369 at the time of his death.
- The constant Asante wars with the British and the coastal states disrupted effective school work.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
# Introduction

Student teachers will be exposed to the historical, sociological and ideological issues that influenced the various changing phases of Ghana's educational system before independence. They would have the opportunity to interrogate and critique the educational philosophy of the colonial governments. This would enable them to appreciate the significance of educational philosophies out of which they would be able to develop their personal teaching philosophies in future.

The unit has been divided into four (4) sessions:

Session 1: Changing Phases of Ghana's Educational System before Independence.
Session 2: Colonial Education Reforms.
Session 3: Changing Phases of Ghana's Educational System after Independence.
Session 4: Developing Personal Teaching Philosophies.

Let us begin by looking at session 1.

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. Identify the various perspectives that have influenced education in Ghana before independence.
2. Discuss the reasons why the colonial government became interested in education in the colony.
3. Discuss the effects of the ordinances passed from 1852 to 1887 on the development of education in the colony.

ex: Education ordinances: early measures announcing colonial government presence in education.
Major educational policies passed in 19th century as ordinances.
Definition: document backed by law stating direction of education.
3. The Education Ordinances of the 19th.desc:
- This aspect looks at some of the very early measures that the colonial government used to announce its full presence in the field of education.
- This will tackle major educational policies passed in the 19th century (1801-1900). These policies were passed in the form of education ordinances.
- An education ordinance can be described as a document backed by law stating the direction of education in a given place.

6. The Education Ordinance of 1887.desc:
- Governor Brandford Griffith had to pass the ordinance for Gold Coast colony alone because Lagos was separated from the Gold Coast in 1886 and from that time onwards, Lagos had its own administration and its own ordinance.
- This ordinance addressed some of the weaknesses that led to the failure of the previous ones.

# Main Features of the 1887 Ordinance

- There was to be established a new Board of Education for the Gold Coast alone with the power to make rules for the inspection of schools and certification of teachers.
- The administration of all assisted schools to be placed in the hands of Managers instead of local school Boards. The school Managers had power to appoint local managers whenever the need arose. By this amendment, the sole responsibility for the management of schools was placed in the hands of the missions.
- Industrial schools were to be established for boys and girls.
- It also called for the admission of all pupils irrespective of religion or race into grants-in-aid schools.
- Payment by results was introduced. Under this arrangement, grants to schools depended on passes pupils made in the subjects taught, during the annual examination conducted by the inspector of schools.
- Grants to schools were increased.
- The first Gold Coast Director of Education was appointed in 1890.

# Factors that led to the Failure of the Education Ordinance of 1887

- Financial constraints: as this made it impossible for the government to extend financial aid to all schools.
- The negative effects of the payment by results system, which included:
  - Overloaded school time-tables in order to get more aid.
  - Encouraging rote learning and memorization.
  - Arbitrary use of the cane as a means of forcing children to learn.
  - Enmity developed between teachers and school inspectors, bringing about lack of co-operation between the two partners in education.
ex: 1887 Ordinance: passed after Lagos separated (1886); addressed previous weaknesses.

Features: new Board of Education for Gold Coast alone, school Managers (missions) in charge, industrial schools for boys/girls, all pupils admitted irrespective of religion, payment by results introduced, grants increased, first Director of Education appointed (1890).
Failure factors: financial constraints, negative effects of payment by results (overloaded timetables, rote learning, corporal punishment, enmity between teachers and inspectors).
1882 Ordinance passed under Governor Rowe; covered Gold Coast and Lagos.
Aims: raise education standards, single control system, grants-in-aid basis.
Grants conditions: 3Rs + needlework for girls, optional history/geography/grammar, suitable buildings, English medium.

Provisions: Board of Education, two school types (government and assisted), Inspector of Schools (Rev. Sunter), grants for buildings/salaries, special grants for industrial schools, improved teacher training.
Failure causes: lack of funds, administration overload, no teacher training college (lack staff/funding), industrial schools never established.
5. The 1882 Education Ordinance.desc:
- The education ordinance of 1882 was passed under the direction of Governor Rowe.
- This ordinance covered Gold Coast and Lagos which were under one colonial administrative authority.

# Aims of the 1882 Ordinance

The aims of the ordinance included:
- Raising the standard of education in mission and private schools.
- Introducing a system of single control of education.
- Creating a systematic basis for providing grants-in-aid for schools. This was money meant to support the little funds available to the mission and private schools.

# Conditions for grants-in-aid

The conditions for giving grants-in-aid to schools included:
- implementation of the compulsory curriculum of the 3Rs (reading, Writing and Arithmetic) plus needle work for girls.
- an optional curriculum of history, geography and English Grammar.
- suitable building and equipment.
- English as a medium of instruction.

# Provisions of the 1882 Ordinance

With the above stated aims in mind the 1882 education ordinance put forward the following provisions:

- A board of education was to be set up to supervise and inspect schools.
- Two types of schools were recommended: government schools (maintained entirely through public funds) and assisted schools (founded by missions or private individuals and aided from public funds according to efficiency).
- Appointment of an Inspector of Schools for all the British West Africa. When appointed the Inspector would spend most of his time in the Gold Coast. Lagos would contribute one-third of the inspector's fixed annual salary of £400. Rev. Sunter (Principal of Fourah Bay College) was appointed.
- There was to be paid regular grants-in-aid to schools if they opened themselves up to all pupils irrespective of religion.
- Grants to be used for school buildings and teachers' salaries.
- Special grants to be made to industrial schools.
- The quality for the training of teachers was to be improved.

# Causes of the Failure of the Education Ordinance of 1882

- Lack of funds.
- The administration of education in the Gold Coast and Lagos was too much for the General Board of Education and the Inspector of Schools.
- Government could not set up the proposed teacher training college because of lack of tutorial staff and funding.
- The proposed industrial schools were never established.

4. The 1852 Education Ordinance.desc:
- In 1850, Governor Stephen Hill was appointed solely for the Gold Coast. He worked with an Executive council and an appointed Legislative Council (similar to modern-day parliament).
- In 1852, an Education Ordinance was passed, to make way for the improvement of education in the colony.

# Aims of the 1852 Ordinance

The ordinance sought to:
- provide for the education of the inhabitants of Her Majesty's (Queen's) forts and settlements on the Gold Coast.
- provide education of neglected children of wealthy African traders.
- make room for girls' education.
- provide for the training of teachers.

- In order to provide money to support the goals of this ordinance the government had to pass a poll tax. The poll tax required that every man, woman and child paid one shilling per head.
- Governor Hill indicated that the levy would be used to finance the courts, health, welfare projects and also provide better education to the inhabitants of the forts and the surrounding settlements.
- It is important to take note that the proposals made were to be supported with revenue from the poll Tax.

# Challenges

The 1852 ordinance failed to achieve the desired results. The following reasons account for the failure:

- the people of the Gold Coast refused to pay the poll tax of one shilling, complaining that it was not affordable.
- the people of the Gold Coast complained that they did not understand the purpose of the tax.
- Mr. and Mrs. Vinall, who were to train teachers, could not survive the weather conditions here. Mrs. Vinall died a few weeks on arrival and Mr. Vinall had to go back home because of poor health.

The failure of the 1852 education ordinance led to the passing of another one in 1882, after the Asante-British war of 1874.
ex: 1852 Ordinance passed under Governor Stephen Hill to improve education.
Aims: educate inhabitants of forts, neglected children of wealthy traders, girls' education, teacher training.
Funded by poll tax (one shilling per person).
Failure reasons: people refused to pay tax (unaffordable, didn't understand purpose), Vinalls (teacher trainers) died or returned due to illness.
Failure led to 1882 ordinance.

2. The Reasons why the Colonial Governme.desc:
The reasons why the colonial administration, after disinterest, decided to play a more active role in education in the colony:

1. In the first place, by 1850, the competing European countries namely Portugal, Denmark and Holland had left the shores of modern Ghana. The British therefore had complete control over activities in the colony.
2. After the 'Sagrenti War' fought between the Asante and the British, the whole of southern Ghana and Asante were annexed to become part of the British colony.
3. Furthermore, by 1871, the Fante Confederacy, which was a threat to the activities of the British in southern Ghana, had been suppressed.
4. Again, the need for more clerks and administrators which the missions alone could not satisfy, called for the intervention.
ex: By 1850: Portugal, Denmark, Holland left; British had complete control.
After Sagrenti War: southern Ghana and Asante annexed as British colony.
By 1871: Fante Confederacy suppressed.
Need for more clerks and administrators beyond what missions could provide.

3. Governor Frederick Gordon Guggisberg.desc:
- Guggisberg was born in Canada on 20th July 1869 and taken to England at age ten. He entered the army and was commissioned in 1889.
- After Guggisberg completed his service in Singapore with the Royal Engineers he returned to Britain and became a teacher in his former college, the Royal Military Academy at Woolwich.
- This British army officer, of Canadian origin assumed office as governor of the Gold Coast immediately after the war of 1919.
- In colonial Ghana, Guggisberg's contribution to education, health and transportation was unmatched.

# The Educationists Committee

- Guggisberg's work in education was informed by the work of the Educationists Committee he had put in place to advise him.
- The Chairman of the Educationist committee was J. D. Oman, the Director of Education.
- Josiah Spio-Garbrah, a Ghanaian headmaster of Cape Coast Boys' School was a member.
- The members were to investigate past attempts at education in the Gold Coast, their successes and failures and look at wide range of issues bothering education.
- The Committee, set up in March 1920, presented its report in May the same year. 52 recommendations and 53 suggestions were made.
- The recommendations of the Phelps-Stokes Commission Report, and a later visit by J. D. Oman to Tuskegee and Hampton in the USA, enriched the work of the committee.

Three important recommendations that were all in line with Guggisberg's vision were:
- the use of the vernacular in lower primary.
- the need for teachers and better conditions of service.
- the need for a Boys' Boarding School in the country.
ex: Guggisberg: born Canada (1869), army officer, teacher, Governor of Gold Coast (1919-1927).
Unmatched contributions to education, health, transportation.
Educationists Committee (1920): chaired by J.D. Oman, member Josiah Spio-Garbrah.
Investigated past education attempts; 52 recommendations, 53 suggestions.
Informed by Phelps-Stokes Commission and visit to Tuskegee/Hampton (USA).
Three key recommendations: vernacular in lower primary, better teacher conditions, Boys' Boarding School.
Background: increased government revenue, extension of British rule to Asante and North.
1908: Governor Rodger established committee on education.
Provisions: end of Payment by Results, emphasis on agriculture (school farms), introduction of technical education.
Grants based on teaching efficiency, not pupil passes.
Government Technical School founded in Accra (1909), later Takoradi Secondary Technical School.
Accra Training Institute for teacher training opened (1909).
Emphasis on literacy and development of African culture.
