2. Governor Rodger's 1909 Reforms.desc:
- During the first five years of the 1900s, the Gold Coast experienced a steady growth in government revenue and government was able to meet its recurrent expenditure without recourse to the British treasury.
- This means that the government was able to pay its expenses in terms of salaries of workers, budget on education, health and other social services without asking for help from the British treasury.
- On the political front, British rule was gradually extended to Asante and the North.
- In 1908, Governor John Rodger put a committee in place to consider various matters in connection with education in the Gold Coast.
- The committee's report, which was accepted by the secretary of state in Great Britain, had a big impact on the colony.

# Provisions of Governor Rodger's 1909 Reforms

The recommendations provided by the committee included:

1. End of the Payment by Results system.
2. Emphasis on agriculture education. This led to introduction of school farms.
3. Introduction of technical education by the government to supplement the effort of the Basel mission.

- The 1909 Education Rules aimed at improving teaching methods, and making primary school programme less bookish. Hence payment by results was abolished.
- Grants from government now depended on efficiency of teaching but not the passes made by pupils.
- The government was advised to take more interest in technical education.
- In 1909, the Government Technical School, the first of its kind was founded in Accra. It was later moved to new premises, now known as Takoradi Secondary Technical School at Takoradi.
- The Accra Training Institute for the training of teachers was opened in 1909.
- Governor Rodger whilst emphasizing the acquisition of literacy, called for the development of the best of African culture.

# Challenges of Governor Roger's Reforms

Every educational programme no matter how well it is designed is bound to encounter some difficulties:

1. The end to the 'Payment by Results' and introduction of the new set of educational rules did not eliminate the 'parrot learning' in schools.
2. Lack of funds.
3. The agriculture education that was introduced suffered serious set-back because of lack of funds to operate and maintain the school farms.

- Had it not been the First World War between 1914 to 1919, Governor Rodger would have done far better.

ex: Improved primary education; closed 150 bush schools.
Achimota College founded (1924) with Rev. A.G. Frazer as principal; represented co-education.
Teacher salaries increased; qualified teacher register opened.
Vernacular used in first three years of primary (12th principle).
Four government trade schools opened (1922) at Yendi, Mampong, Kibi, Ausansi.
Three-year and Four-year teacher training introduced.
Wesley College moved to Kumasi (1924); Presbyterian Training College, Akropong renovated (1928).
Principles continued influence after Guggisberg left (1927).
5. Implementation of Guggisberg's Princi.desc:
- In response to the first principle, efforts were made to improve primary education; inferior teachers were driven out and Guggisberg closed down about 150 'bush schools'.
- Achimota College was founded in 1924, with Reverend A. G. Frazer as the first principal. Achimota also represented the idea of co-education and equal opportunities for boys and girls.
- On raising the status of the teaching profession, the salaries of teachers were considerably increased and a register of teachers was opened and only those who were qualified were allowed to register.
- The use of the vernacular as the medium of instruction during the first three years of public primary school education had its root in the 12th principle.
- In Guggisberg's time, technical education at a lower level (his 16th principle) was greatly expanded by the opening of four government trade schools in 1922 at Yendi, Mampong, Kibi and Ausansi.
- Guggisberg closed down 150 'bush schools'.
- Achimota College was founded in 1924, with Reverend A.G. Frazer as the first principal.
- Inferior teachers were dismissed from the service and a register was opened for qualified teachers.
- The Three-year and Four-year Teacher training programmes were introduced.
- In 1924, Wesley College was provided with new buildings and moved from Aburi to Kumasi.
- The Presbyterian Training College at Akropong was also provided with new building in 1928.

It must be noted that though Governor Guggisberg left colonial Ghana in 1927, many of his Principles continued to receive attention in later years.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
# Introduction

Dear learner, congratulation on how far you have come. In our last session, we discussed the education ordinance from 1852 to 1887. At the beginning of the 20th century, conditions in the country and other political development caused the need to reform education in the country. This session shall look at those reforms before independence. We shall discuss the Governor Rodger's educational reforms of 1909, the contributions of Governor Guggisberg from 1919 to 1927 to education and his achievements. We shall also look at the Accelerated Development Plan of 1951.

Relax and enjoyed the session, it promised to be very interesting.

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. Discuss at least 3 provisions recommended in the Rodger's reforms in education.
2. Outline the challenges which the 1909 educational reforms encountered.
3. Discuss the provisions in the 16 Principles of Guggisberg.
4. Describe the achievements and challenges of Guggisberg educational reforms.
ex: Session covers Governor Rodger's reforms (1909), Governor Guggisberg's contributions (1919-1927), and Accelerated Development Plan (1951).
Learning indicators: discuss Rodger's provisions, outline challenges, discuss Guggisberg's 16 principles, describe achievements and challenges.

6. The Accelerated Development Plan (ADP.desc:
- By 1950, western education had taken deep roots in colonial Ghana. The work of Sir Gordon Guggisberg had given greater hope for development of a good foundation for education.
- It was now the responsibility of future administrations to maintain this interest.
- In 1951, after strong agitations by several nationalist groups, a new constitution came into being. Dr. Kwame Nkrumah became the leader of Government Business.
- The then minister of education Mr. Kojo Botsio laid before parliament, the Accelerated Development Plan for Education.

# Main Objective of the ADP

The main objective of the ADP by quoting Graham (1976) was: '... to help develop a balanced system, working towards universal primary education as rapidly as consideration of finances and teacher training allowed, but maintaining at the same time, proportionate facilities for further education for those most fitted to receive it'.

# Main Proposals of the ADP

Graham (1976) further provides the main proposals as the following:

- A six-year basic primary course for all children at public expense; primary school fees to be abolished as from 1st January 1952.
- Infant-junior schools were to be known as primary schools. Senior primary schools were to be known as middle schools, and were to be regarded as part of the post-primary system.
- Facilities for the training of teachers were to be increased by the addition of ten new colleges and the doubling in size of the six existing ones.
- Additional day secondary schools were to be provided, and certain non-assisted secondary schools were to be assisted.
- Four secondary-technical schools were to be provided including the conversion of the government technical school at Takoradi. Technical institutes were to be established at Tarkwa, Accra, Kumasi, Sekondi-Takoradi.
- All teachers in training, except those possessing a school certificate, were to take the certificate 'B' course, and entry to the certificate 'A' course was to be made from among certificate B teachers who had taught for a period.
- The middle schools in the northern territories were to be increased in number as quickly as possible and more potential teachers were to be provided. A new training college was to be opened at Pusiga. Primary schools in the north were to be increased as teachers became available.
- The salaries of teachers trained and untrained were to be reviewed. It was proposed that in future all teachers in training would be treated as if on study leave and would draw the salaries they would have received if they had been teaching in a school.
- Considerable increases in scholarships to secondary schools, technical and trade schools were recommended.

# Implementation of ADP

From 1952, many provisions made in the ADP were implemented:

- More primary and middle schools were opened.
- Fees were abolished for elementary school children of ages six to twelve (6-12).
- The certificate 'B' Two-Year Teachers programme was introduced to increase the rate and produce more teachers.
- The certificate 'A' Post 'B' course was introduced.
- A secondary school for girls was opened by the sisters of St. Louis at Kumasi and a Teachers college was later founded.
- Archbishop Porter founded St. Johns day school for boys in Sekondi.
- In 1952 Opoku Ware Secondary School was founded by the Catholic mission.
- The same year Bishop Herman's Secondary was founded at Kpando and a girl's school founded at Ho in 1953 by the OLA sisters.
- The first secondary school for boys was opened at Tamale in 1951.

# Achievements by 1957/58

- By 1957 when Ghana attained independence there had been a marked improvement in the number of schools.
- By 1958, there were 3,402 primary schools and 1030 middle schools.
- Between 1951-1957, secondary schools increased from 12 to 38.
- The 6th form course was introduced.
- In 1958, the National Teacher Training Council was set up to address teacher education and quality products.

# Challenges

- As usual, lack of funds was a limiting factor for the implementation of the ADP.
- There was also the need for qualified teachers.
ex: Background: western education established; Guggisberg's foundation; new constitution (1951); Nkrumah as leader, Kojo Botsio as Education Minister.
Objective: balanced system, universal primary education as finances and teacher training allowed.

Proposals: 6-year primary free from 1952, primary/middle school renaming, 10 new teacher training colleges, secondary schools assisted, 4 secondary-technical schools, Certificate B and A courses, northern schools expansion, teacher salary review, increased scholarships.
Implementation: more schools opened, fees abolished (6-12 years), Certificate B (2-year) and A Post B introduced, new schools founded (St. Louis, Opoku Ware, Bishop Herman's, Tamale boys).
Achievements by 1958: 3,402 primary, 1,030 middle schools; secondary from 12 to 38; 6th form introduced; National Teacher Training Council established.
Challenges: lack of funds, need for qualified teachers.

7. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

We have read on the various perspectives that have influenced education in Ghana before independence through. We also study how Governor Rodger's reforms and Sir Guggisberg reforms influence education in Ghana. The sixteen principles of education marked a dynamic period of education delivery in Gold Coast. These principles continued to direct educational policy in the Gold Coast until independence in 1957. The accelerated development plan of 1951 was also discussed.

The Accelerated Development Plan was a dynamic move by Nkrumah administration to fast-track the pace of educational development in Ghana. The enthusiasm of the people and support from government, the missions, the Ghana Cocoa Marketing Board and others, helped to meet to a great extent, the expectations of the plan.

# Self-Assessment

1. Discuss any THREE (3) challenges which frustrated the implementation of the 1909 educational reforms.
2. Critically examine the Guggisberg's 13th principle in relation to the current policies on education in the basic schools.
3. State any TWO (2) events in the Gold Coast that necessitated the implementation of the Accelerated Development Plan of 1951.
4. Discuss any THREE (3) challenges in education that were encountered as a result of the implementation of ADP.
5. Discuss FIVE (5) principles of Guggisberg which are still very relevant and are making an impact on modern day education in Ghana.
6. Discuss the efforts made in the area of teacher education under the ADP.
ex: Covered: Governor Rodger's reforms, Guggisberg's 16 principles, Accelerated Development Plan 1951.
Guggisberg's principles directed policy until 1957 independence.
ADP fast-tracked educational development with public and stakeholder support.
Self-assessment: 1909 challenges, examine 13th principle, ADP necessitating events, ADP challenges, relevant Guggisberg principles, ADP teacher education efforts.
16 Principles presented to Legislative Council (1925).
Principle 1: thorough primary education; best teachers at kindergarten level.
Principle 2: secondary schools for university entry; Achimota School built.
Principle 3: provision of a university.
Principle 4: equal education opportunities for girls.
Principle 5: co-education desirable at certain stages.
Principle 6: highest quality teachers; minimum wages, registration, closed 150 bush schools.
Principle 7: character training important.
Principle 8: religious teaching.
Principle 9: organized games.
Principle 10: curriculum include local health, welfare, industries.
Principle 11: African inspectors trained.
Principle 12: English education based on vernacular.
Principle 13: education not compulsory nor free.
Principle 14: cooperation with missions, subsidized.
Principle 15: government ultimate control for uniform standards.
Principle 16: trade schools; engineering school at Achimota (1931), four Government Trade Schools.
4. Guggisberg's Sixteen (16) Principles.desc:
In 1925, after a careful study of the report, the governor presented to the legislative council, what had been termed, Guggisberg's Sixteen (16) Principles of Education.

1. Primary education must be thorough and be from bottom to the top: The formation of a child's life is based on the period in kindergarten. The best teachers should teach at this level and be at least as highly paid as those who teach higher levels.

2. The provision of secondary schools with an educational standard that will fit young men and women to enter a university. In accordance with this principle a co-educational institution, Achimota School was built as a model to other institutions.

3. The provision of a university.

4. Equal opportunities to those given to boys should be provided for the education of girls.

5. Co-education is desirable during certain stages of education.

6. The staff of teachers must be of the highest possible quality: To raise fallen standards, minimum wages fixed, registration of qualified teachers, streamlining government grants, increased training years. Qualified teachers registered, unqualified driven out. Minimum wage fixed (£100-£180 for mission trained, £98-£208 for government trained). 150 substandard (bush) schools closed.

7. Character training must take an important place in education.

8. Religious teaching should form part of school life.

9. Organized games should form part of school life.

10. The course in every school should include special references to the health, welfare and industries of the locality. Curriculum was to include work dealing with life, welfare and pursuits of the village or town. Schools to take active part in surroundings.

11. A sufficient staff of African inspectors of schools must be trained and maintained.

12. Whilst an English education must be given, it must be based solidly on the vernacular. It was suggested that all European teachers should have a grasp of the local language to understand students' background.

13. Education cannot be compulsory nor free. This principle was based on the circumstance, not a way of limiting education.

14. There should be cooperation between the government and the missions and later should be subsidized for educational purposes.

15. The Government must have the ultimate control of education throughout the Gold Coast to ensure uniform standard.

16. The provision of trade schools with a technical and literary education that will fit young men to become skilled craftsmen and useful citizens. To promote technical education, an engineering school was opened at Achimota in 1931, producing first group of Ghanaian engineers. Guggisberg opened four Government Trade Schools.

ex: 1961 Act: first major post-independence act; gave legal backing to earlier provisions.
Provisions: minister's powers defined, compulsory/free basic education, 6-year primary + 4-year middle, teacher service terms, freedom to open schools, freedom of worship, Boards of Governors.
Achievements: primary schools 3,514 to 8,144 (1966), middle 1,234 to 2,777, secondary 36 to 59, teacher salaries on study leave, certificate A colleges 19 to 82.
Challenges: inadequate finance, teacher shortage (rural vs urban), church complaints of ministerial interference.
Impact: minister role strengthened, LEA involvement increased, enrolments doubled, teacher training tripled, opportunities expanded.
2. The 1961 Education Act.desc:
- This Act was passed to give legal backing to earlier educational provisions and to make room for more reforms. This was the first major act passed in the immediate post-independent Ghana.
- In this session, we shall look at measures that were put in place to direct educational practice in Ghana. The powers of the minister of education, Local Education Authorities (LEAs) compulsory education among others shall be discussed.
- The 1961 Act provided far-reaching measures that made the running of education in Ghana, more organized than before. Mr. Kojo Botsio outlined the provisions of this legal instrument before Parliament in 1961.

# Key Provisions of the 1961 Act

Among the key provisions made were the following:

- The powers of the minister of education were defined to generally deal with matters relating to education. These powers included areas like examination, appointment of teachers, curriculum, admission and promotion of pupils and defining duties of managers.
- Compulsory and fee-free basic education for all children of school-going age.
- Basic education was structured into two stages - 6 years primary school and 4 years middle school.
- Institution of terms and conditions of service for teachers. A teachers' council was to be set up.
- Freedom of private groups and individual to open schools.
- Freedom of worship was to be ensured in all schools. No pupil was to be denied admission on religious grounds.
- The establishment of Board of Governors for all non-government secondary and teacher training colleges.
- The establishment of Board of Governors for schools.

# Achievements of the 1961 Act

Though there were several challenges, the 1961 Act achieved some of its objectives as presented below:

- The number of public primary schools increased from 3514 to 8144 by 1966.
- Public middle schools increased from 1234 to 2777 during the same period.
- Public and private secondary schools increased from 36 in 1961 to 59 by 1966.
- Teachers received salaries as if on study leave, which situation is still practiced.
- By 1966, the number of certificate 'A' colleges had grown from 19 to 82.

# Challenges of the 1961 Act

The challenges that confronted the full implementation of the provisions under the 1961 Act were as follows:

- There was inadequate financial resource to meet the great demand for infrastructure and other resources.
- The increase in pupil enrolment did not go with corresponding training and recruitment of adequate teachers for the classrooms.
- Rural schools suffered the non-availability of teachers as against the urban schools.
- The churches also complained about interference by the Minister of Education in the running of their school.

# Summary of 1961 Act Impact

- The 1961 Education Act gave legal backing to the 1951 Education Act such that:
  - The role of the minister of Education became much more felt.
  - The local Education Authorities played a more involving part in education.
  - Between 1961 and 1966, enrolment in both primary and middle schools as well as secondary schools doubled.
  - The number of students in teacher training colleges tripled.
  - Free and compulsory primary and middle school education offered a lot of opportunities for the people of Ghana.

ex: Session covered: 1961 Education Act, Kwapong (1966), Dzobo (1974), Evans Anfom (1987), Anamuah-Mensah (2002).
Shows how successive governments reformed education.

Self-assessment: provisions of four reviews and their relevance today, 1961 Act achievements/challenges, Dzobo underlying principles, reasons for continuous curriculum review, need for review after 1987.
7. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

We have come to the end of the session. I am sure you are learning a lot about the changes that have come about in education. We have seen, according to their orientations, how succeeding governments have tried to reform education in Ghana. This session took us through the Kwapong Education Review Committee, the 1974 reforms under Prof. K.N Dzobo, Evans Anfom Committee of 1987 and Anamuah Mensah Committee of 2002.

# Self-Assessment

1. State any two provisions prescribed by the following reviews and show to what extent do you think these provisions are still relevant in Ghana today?
   a. Kwapong committee review of 1966.
   b. Dzobo committee reforms of 1974.
   c. Evans Anfom committee review of 1987.
   d. Anamuah-Mensah committee review of 2002.

2. Identify any two achievements and any two challenges of the Education Act of 1961.

3. Discuss any 4 underlying principles on which the Dzobo committee on education based their proposals.

4. Why do you think government must continue to review the existing curriculum in the country?

5. Why has there been the need for curriculum review after the 1987 reforms.

ex: Anamuah-Mensah Committee (2002): inaugurated by President Kufour; chaired by Prof. Jophus Anamuah-Mensah (Vice-Chancellor, UEW).
Task: review entire educational system to make it responsive to current challenges.

Concerns addressed: FCUBE content, limited further education/skills for JSS/SSS products, inadequate TVET at second cycle, unstructured apprenticeship, tertiary crisis (insufficient places, financing), depletion of trained teachers, upgrading teacher colleges to tertiary.

Structural recommendations (2007): universal basic education 4-15 years (2 years KG, 6 years primary, 3 years JHS), 4-year SHS replacing 3-year SSS, multiple streams at SHS (general, technical, vocational, agricultural, apprenticeship).

Curriculum: KG/lower primary - Ghanaian language + English; basic level - literacy, numeracy, creative arts, PE, ICT, problem-solving; primary - English, Ghanaian languages, science, religious/moral, hygiene, culture; upper primary - maths, integrated science; JHS core - English, maths, social studies, integrated science, agriculture, TVET, ICT, Ghanaian language, French; SHS core - English, maths, integrated science, social studies, ICT + electives; TVET emphasis; special education and French at basic level.
Tertiary: specialized institutions, open universities/distance education, post-graduate training/research, resource polytechnics.
Management: District Education Departments under Assemblies, National Inspectorate Board, National Council for TVET, National Apprentice Training Board, National Teaching Council.
Cross-cutting: ICT, distance education, library services, science/technology, guidance/counselling, private participation.
6. Anamuah Mensah Committee Review of 20.desc:
- On January 17, 2002, President J.A. Kufour inaugurated the Committee on Review of Education reforms in Ghana.
- Prof. Jophus Anamuah-Mensah, the Vice-Chancellor of the University of Education, Winneba was the chairman of the 29-member committee.
- The task of the committee was 'to review the entire educational system in the country with the view to making it more responsive to current challenges'.

# Concerns Addressed by the Committee

The Review Committee, inter alia, was required to address the following concerns:

- Examine the content to be given to the constitutionally mandated FCUBE.
- The limited provision of further education and skills training facilities for most JSS and SSS products.
- The inadequate provision of technical and vocational education at the second cycle level.
- The unstructured provision of apprenticeship training for majority of school leavers.
- The crisis at the tertiary level, with insufficient places to meet the needs of a modernizing economy; particularly limited opportunities for post-secondary education for products of vocational, technical and agricultural education.
- The problem of finding a sustainable financing of tertiary education.
- The continuous depletion of basic schools of trained teachers.
- The upgrading of teacher training colleges into the tertiary system.

# Recommendations on Structure (Implemented from September 2007)

In October 2002, the committee presented its report to government. On the structure, the government accepted the recommendation that follow, to be implemented from September 2007:

- Universal and continuous basic education from 4-15 years, and thereafter, a new four-year Senior High School (SHS) system to replace the present primary, JSS and SSS structure. This comprises:
  - 2 years of kindergarten (4-6 years)
  - 6 years primary education (6-12 years)
  - 3 years Junior High School (12-15 years)
- The present three-year senior secondary school will become a 4-year Senior High School (SHS)
- After Junior High School (JHS), students may choose to go into different streams at senior high school, comprising general education, technical, vocational and agricultural education; others may enter apprenticeship schemes with some support from government.

# Curriculum Recommendations

Curriculum for the different levels of education was presented in the report as follows:

- At the kindergarten and lower primary levels, the medium of instruction shall be Ghanaian language and English.
- The basic level shall place emphasis on Literacy, Numeracy, Creative activities - Music and Dance, Physical Education, ICT and problem-solving skills.
- Primary education shall stress on the teaching of English language and Ghanaian Languages, with subjects like Science, Religious and Moral Education, Hygiene and Culture at the lower level. Basic Mathematical Skills and Integrated Science shall be emphasized at the upper primary.
- English language shall be the medium of instruction from upper primary.
- At the Junior High School (JHS) level, the core subjects shall be English, Mathematics, Social Studies and Integrated Science, including Agriculture, Technical Vocational, ICT, Ghanaian language and French shall be offered.
- At the SHS level, the core subjects shall be English, Mathematics, Integrated Science, Social Studies and ICT. In addition, elective courses in Agriculture, Business, Technical, Vocational, General Arts or Science programmes shall be offered.
- Technical/Vocational (TVET) shall provide employable skills through formal and informal apprenticeship.
- Special Education and French are to be emphasized at the basic level.

# Tertiary Education Recommendations

Tertiary education, in order to meet the changing needs of society are to be given the needed attention. There shall be:

- Specialized institutions.
- Open Universities and Distance Education.
- Post-graduate training and research.
- Resourcing of Polytechnics.

# Management Arrangements

The management arrangements in place to ensure the effective implementation of the reforms include the following:

- District Directorates of Education shall become the education departments of the Decentralized Assemblies.
- A new National Inspectorate Board (NIB) outside the GES but under the Ministry of Education Science and Sports shall supervise basic and secondary schools.
- A National Council for Technical and Vocational Education and Training shall be established.
- A National Apprentice Training Board, among other things, shall supervise and regulate apprentice training.
- The National Teaching Council shall be established to be a licensing coordinating body and be responsible for teacher education.

# Cross-Cutting Issues

The Reviewed Committee has also recommended 'cross-cutting' to be considered in the implementation of the reforms. These include:

- Information and Communication Technology (ICT).
- Distance Education.
- Library and Information Services.
- Science and Technology.
- Guidance and Counselling.
- Private Participation in education.

These are important considerations, which if properly established, shall complement efforts at working toward the goals of the reforms.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
# Introduction

Hello, you are welcome to another interesting session on educational trends in Ghana. in this session, Student teachers will be exposed to the historical, sociological and ideological issues that influenced the various changing phases of Ghana's educational system after independence. This session will discuss the overview and the passing of the 1961 Educational Act, look at rationale and key issues in Committee Review of 1966, Dzobo Committee Reforms of 1974, Evans Anfom Committee review of 1987 and Anamuah Mensah Committee Review of 2002.

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. List the major provisions of the 1961 Education Act.
2. Identify the challenges that affected implementation of the provisions.
3. Explain the rationale and aims for the institution of the following:
   a. Kwapong Committee 1966.
   b. Dzobo Committee Reforms of 1974.
   c. Evans Anfom Committee Review of 1987.
   d. Anamuah Mensah Committee Review of 2002.
ex: Session covers changing phases of Ghana's educational system after independence.
Discusses 1961 Education Act and major review committees (1966-2002).
Learning indicators: list 1961 Act provisions, identify challenges, explain rationale/aims of four major committee reviews.
Dzobo Committee (1974): chaired by Prof. K.N. Dzobo (University of Cape Coast).

Challenges addressed: purely academic curriculum (no employable skills), need for science/technology emphasis, literacy for community participation, children's right to numeracy/literacy, cultural identity for unity, environmental consciousness.

Recommendations: kindergarten (4-6 years), 6-year primary + 3-year junior secondary, 4-year undergraduate degrees, vocational/technical from JSS, artisans as resource persons, upgrade polytechnics for middle-level manpower.
4. Dzobo Committee Reform of 1974.desc:
- By 1974, succeeding governments had not stopped trying to fine-tune education delivery in Ghana.
- Prof. K.N. Dzobo of the University of Cape Coast was the chairman of the committee that was to address the following challenges:

# Challenges Addressed

- The purely academic curriculum that did not provide school leavers with employable skills.
- The need for changes in the system to place more emphasis on science and technology.
- The urgency for as many Ghanaians as possible to become literate and be able to participate in the day-to-day administration of their communities.
- Ensuring that the basic human rights of all children to be provided opportunities to acquire numeracy and literacy is fulfilled.
- The need for school-children to have a better cultural identity to ensure national unity.
- Making Ghanaians conscious of the environment, ensuring agricultural productivity and conserving our forests.

# Recommendations of the Dzobo Committee

The recommendations made by the Dzobo Committee to address the issues raised were as follows:

- Kindergarten education for children between the ages of 4 and 6 years must be introduced.
- First cycle education of 6 years primary and a new 3-year junior secondary education.
- Undergraduate courses for secondary school leavers at the tertiary level to last 4 years.
- Vocational and technical courses to start at the junior secondary school.
- Artisans and tradesmen were to act as resource persons in the technical/vocational courses.
- Upgrading of polytechnics to facilitate the supply of middle level manpower.

ex: Evans Anfom Committee (1987): under PNDC (Rawlings).
Objectives: reduce pre-university from 17 to 12 years (6-3-3), increase access, improve quality/relevance, ensure prudent use of funds.

Measures: World Bank loan, classroom/workshop construction, teacher retraining (JSS), continuous assessment, BECE/SSSCE replaced old exams, new subjects (life skills, religious/moral, music/dance), University Rationalization Committee.
Achievements: community support, improved textbooks/equipment, circuit supervisors, 17 to 12 years reduction.

Limitations: insufficient textbooks/equipment, inadequate classrooms, too many pupil teachers, JSS graduates unskilled (street hawking), JSS not viable exit point, cost-sharing barriers, quality concerns.
5. Evans Anfom Committee Review of 1987.desc:
- In December 1981, Ft. Lt. J. J. Rawlings came back to power as the chairman of the Provisional National Defence Council (PNDC).
- In 1987, this government took another look at the existing structure of education with the objective of achieving the following:

# Objectives of the 1987 Reforms

- reducing the period of pre-university education from 17 to 12 years (6 years primary education, 3 years junior secondary school, 3 years senior secondary education and 3 or 4 years tertiary education).
- increasing access to basic and secondary levels of schooling.
- improving quality, efficiency and relevance of pre-university education.
- ensuring prudent use of monies put into education, and recovery of such monies.

# Measures Taken by PNDC Government

The then PNDC government took the following measures:

- World Bank loan was contracted to facilitate implementation.
- Good number of classrooms and workshops were constructed.
- Many teachers were retrained, especially for the JSS.
- Continuous assessment became part of assessment policy in schools.
- Basic Education Certificate Examination (BECE) and the Senior Secondary School Certificate Examination (SSSCE) replaced the Common entrance, Middle School Leaving Certificate (MSLC) examinations and the GCE.
- New subjects such as life skills, religious and moral education and music and dance were introduced.
- The University Rationalization Committee (URC) was established to:
  - ensure that monies given to universities were effectively used.
  - ensure courses run in the universities met national needs.
  - ensure that enough graduates were locally trained.

# Achievements of the 1987 Educational Reforms

- support by communities in putting up school buildings and running the schools.
- improvement in the supply of textbooks and equipment.
- appointment of circuit supervisors and improvement of supervision.
- reduction of school years from 17 to 12 years.

# Limitations of the 1987 Reforms

These limitations included:

- insufficient textbooks and technical skills equipment to go round the schools.
- inadequate classrooms and workshops.
- too many pupil teachers, especially in the rural areas.
- many JSS graduates who could not gain access to SSS ended up in the streets selling dog chains.
- the JSS could not become an exit point because the products did not have adequate skills.
- cost-sharing made it difficult for brilliant but poor students to move on.
- there was a question mark over the quality of the graduates of the system.

ex: Kwapong Committee (1966-67): under NLC, chaired by Prof. E.A. Kwapong (Vice Chancellor, University of Ghana).
Reasons for review: Act didn't address church needs, falling standards, indiscipline.

Recommendations: textbook fees (primary 1.50, middle 3.00), 8-year integrated course replacing 6-4 structure, common entrance (WAEC) to secondary, 2-year continuation school for non-secondary pupils, 5-year secondary (O-level) + 2-year sixth form (A-level), teacher training (4 years post-middle, 2 years post-secondary), specialist programmes transferred to Winneba.
Mills-Odoi Commission (1967): examined public service structure/remuneration; found teacher wastage due to poor pay.
Mills-Odoi recommendations: decentralization of schools/colleges, Teaching Service Division, improved teacher salaries/conditions.
3. The Kwapong Education Review Committe.desc:
- In June 1961, the National Liberation Council (NLC), the first military government in Ghana, put in place a committee under Prof. E.A. Kwapong, the Vice Chancellor of the University of Ghana, to look at the existing educational system and make recommendations to the government.

# Reasons for the Review

The main reasons why the government wanted some reforms were that:

- the 1961 Act did not address the needs of Ghanaians especially the churches, with respect to the management of schools.
- falling of standards in education.
- general indiscipline among pupils, students and teachers.

# Recommendations of the Kwapong Committee

In July 1967, the committee presented its report. The nature of the recommendations made were:

- Textbooks fees of 01.50 per child in the primary school and 03.00 for middle schools were proposed.
- An integrated 8-year course was to replace the 6 and 4 years primary and middle school respectively.
- Pupils were to continue to the secondary school through the common entrance examination conducted by WAEC.
- Some basic schools were to run the 6-year primary and 4-year middle school programme.
- Pupils who could not enter secondary school, were to pursue a two-year continuation school programme to be equipped with skills to be able to enter the job market.
- The period for secondary education for the GCE ordinary level, was to be 5 years and a 2-year sixth form programme for the GCE 'A' level.
- Post-middle leavers were to train for 4 years and post-secondary leavers, 2 years, to become professional teachers.
- Specialist programmes in basic school English, Mathematics, History, Geography and Science being taught in nine colleges were to be transferred to Winneba.

# Mills-Odoi Commission (1967)

- Before the Education Review Committee presented its report, the NLC had in January 1967 set up a commission under Justice G.C. Mills-Odoi, Judge-Advocate-General.
- The Commission was to look at the structure and remuneration of Public services in Ghana.
- The commission saw that there was too much wastage in the teaching service because of the high rate at which teachers leave for other areas due to poor remuneration.

The Commission, among other things, recommended:
- the decentralization of secondary schools and training colleges from the Ministry of Education to the regional and district authorities.
- establishment of a Teaching Service Division within the public service.
- improvement in the salaries and service conditions of teachers.

4. Components of a Teaching Philosophy S.desc:
A teaching philosophy statement should include an introduction, body and conclusion with specific components that you need to include in each of them:

# 1. Introduction

- Here you will discuss your general belief about education as well as your ideals in relation to teaching.
- A brief description of your conception of teaching and learning, including the elements of your field that most shape your approach to teaching.
- This might also include a description of your students, and their most important learning goals and challenges.

# 2. The Body

- In this part of the statement, it contains a description of what kind of teacher you want to be.
- Your definition of good teaching, with an explanation of why you have developed or adopted this particular definition.
- Justification for why you want to teach that way.
- A discussion of your teaching methods: how do you implement your definition of good teaching?
- Discuss what you see as the ideal classroom environment and how it makes you a better teacher, addresses student needs and their interactions.
- Discuss how you would facilitate age-appropriate learning and how you involve students in the assessment process.
- Explain how you would put your educational ideals into practice.

# 3. Conclusion

- In this section, talk about your goals as a teacher, how you have been able to meet them in the past and how you can build on these to meet future challenges.
- Focus on your personal approach to pedagogy and classroom management as well as what makes you unique as an educator and how you wish to advance your career to further support education.
- Describe what content, skills, or values should students leave your classroom with?
ex: Introduction: general beliefs, conception of teaching/learning, description of students, learning goals/challenges.

Body: description of teacher you want to be, definition of good teaching with justification, teaching methods, ideal classroom environment, age-appropriate learning, student involvement in assessment.
Conclusion: goals as teacher, past achievements, future challenges, personal approach, uniqueness, career advancement, what students should leave with.

7. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

We have come to the end of this session. In this session we look at the concept of developing personal teaching philosophy, the components and it's important. 3. Why is it important to develop your o.desc:
Teaching philosophy is a purposeful and reflective essay about the teacher's teaching beliefs and practices. It is an individual narrative that includes not only one's beliefs about the teaching and learning process but also concrete examples of the ways in which he/she enacts these beliefs in the classroom. The following are some of the reasons for developing personal teaching philosophy:

1. A well-crafted teaching philosophy statement gives a clear and unique portrait of the author as a teacher.
2. A clear philosophy of teaching can lead to a change in teaching behavior and foster professional and personal growth.
3. The teacher's beliefs about teaching and learning usually have a positive impact on the students and the classroom environment.
4. It helps you to organize your evaluation and to guide your behavior as a teacher, the teaching philosophy allows to document beliefs, values, and teaching approaches, and it contributes to articulate a view of teaching for administrative decisionmaking.
5. Teachers tend to implement classroom practices that reflect their philosophical beliefs.
6. A well-done teaching philosophy helps the teacher to reflect and act appropriately in the classroom.
7. The teaching philosophy statement gives you a starting point for examining your teaching practices, allows you to share your ideas with others, and allows you to monitor the progress of your own development as a teacher.
8. By writing a teaching philosophy, you also make your thinking public, open to discussion or comment and this is important because it codifies your ideas at a particular time.
9. It helps you to observe what you do in your lessons, so you learn to monitor your work.
10. Creating a teaching philosophy makes you reflect about your values and beliefs as a teacher, this motivates you to set new goals and it helps you to improve as a professional and as a human and to guide your teaching.

6. Sample Teaching Philosophy Statements.desc:
# Sample 1

'I believe that all children are unique and have something special that they can bring to their own education. I will assist my students to express themselves and accept themselves for who they are, as well embrace the differences of others. Every classroom has its own unique community; my role as the teacher will be to assist each child in developing their own potential and learning styles. I will present a curriculum that will incorporate each different learning styles as well as make the content relevant to the students' lives. I will incorporate hands-on learning, cooperative learning, projects, themes and individual work that engage and activate students learning'.

# Sample 2

My philosophy of education is that a teacher is morally obligated to enter the classroom with only the highest of expectations for each and every one of her students. Thus, the teacher maximizes the positive benefits that naturally come along with any self-fulfilling prophecy. With dedication, perseverance, and hard work, his/her students will rise to the occasion. I aim to bring an open mind, a positive attitude and high expectations to the classroom each day. I believe that I owe it to my students as well as the community to bring consistency, diligence and warmth to my job in the hope that I can ultimately inspire and encourage such traits in the children as well.

Congratulation, I hope you find developing your personal teaching philosophy very interesting.

2. What is a Teaching Philosophy?.desc:
- A teaching philosophy is a brief essay that all newly prospective teachers are required to write.
- A teaching philosophy is a statement of reflection and a philosophical framework of your personal approach to teaching and student learning.
- It explains the rationale behind what guides your practice, what factors impact you as an educator and the values underlying your practice.
- Schonwetter et al. (2002, p. 84) define a teaching philosophy statement as, 'a systematic and critical rationale that focuses on the important components defining effective teaching and learning in a particular discipline and/or institutional context.'
- A teaching philosophy statement clearly and logically communicates what your fundamental values and beliefs are about teaching and learning, why you hold these values and beliefs, and how you translate these values and beliefs into your everyday teaching and learning experiences.
- Natasha Kenny (2008), defines a teaching philosophy statement 'as a clear and logical statement that communicates what your fundamental values and beliefs are about teaching and learning, why you hold these values and beliefs, and how you translate these values and beliefs into your everyday teaching and learning experiences.'
- Your statement should not simply describe your experiences and initiatives in teaching, but should provide a systematic and critical rationale that focuses on the important components defining effective teaching and learning in a particular discipline and/or institutional context. It is personal and reflective, drawing on your own experiences as a teacher.

5. Format of your statement.desc:
A common question among first-time writers of teaching philosophy statements has to do with format. There is no standard format for such statements, however some general rules to follow when writing a teaching philosophy statement. Things to consider are:

1. Use present tense and write the statement in the first person. Generally, it is recommended that your teaching philosophy should employ a first-person (e.g., 'I,' not 'You,' 'Teachers,' or 'He/She'), present tense point of view (e.g., 'teach' not 'taught'). This approach accommodates a variety of disciplines and is easiest to.

2. Keep it brief. Many institutions or committees might offer a recommended page length, and if they do, be sure to adhere to it. However, if no length is suggested, it is wise to keep your statement brief so as not to overwhelm your readers. One to two pages is the generally accepted length. However, you should use your own best judgment to determine an appropriate length, but when in doubt, a briefer statement is likely going to be more effective.

3. Know your audience. As with any piece of writing or presentation, it is vital to keep in mind the needs and values of those who will be reading your statement - especially if you are on the job market. Be aware that different institutions will have differing missions and expectations, particularly regarding the role of teaching.

4. 'Own' your statements. Make sure you talk about your experiences and your beliefs. Ensure your statement is original and truly describes the methods and philosophy you would employ in teaching. This awareness should be reflected in the language you use in your statement. In other words, don't write about what 'all teachers should do.' Instead, write about what you do and what you believe.

5. Avoid jargon. Use common, everyday language not 'technical terms'.

6. Make sure it is well written. Make sure the writing is well organized, comprehensive and communicate to your readers who you are. If they can't understand your writing, then you are failing to communicate.

7. Do not make empty statements. While many or even most teaching statements might start with broad philosophical ideas, you must move beyond the general and anchor your statement with concrete details.

5. Rationale, Aims and Structure of the.desc:
# Early Grade

- Early Years curriculum covers subjects at Kindergarten and Basic 1 - 3.
- At this stage the child requires basic needs like nutrition, warmth, health, security, interaction and stimulation for social, emotional, psychological, physical and cognitive development.
- It is during this stage that the child establishes learning patterns, attitudes, personality and a sense of being.
- Children generally learn by doing and this is done naturally and best through play. They are very active, curious, and explorative and enjoy listening to stories.
- During the Early Years, learners learn better in positive learning environments where they feel emotionally and physically safe and enjoy learning.
- Using an integrated, inquiry and discovery-based approach to teaching and learning will foster caring, supportive and committed relationships between teachers and learners, as well as between learners and their peers.
- Teachers become more confident in their practice, feel trusted by learners, and can develop better classroom management and positive discipline practices.
- Play-based learning further helps to develop the inherent potentials of learners, including critical thinking and imagination.
- The learners' ability to make, re-create, and acquire knowledge and meaning develops in them a spirit of innovation, creativity and resourcefulness alongside practical skills and more complex ways of thinking.

# Upper Primary and High School Subjects

The Upper Primary curriculum covers subjects for Basic 4 to 6, whilst the High School curriculum is divided into Junior High School (Basic 7 to 9) and Senior High School (SHS 1 to 3).

The rationale for each school Subject of study at these levels within the pre-tertiary education structure, shall centre around the overall curriculum learning outcomes where:

a. Learners are fluent in the 4Rs - Reading, wRiting, aRithmetic and cReativity.
b. Learners have a broad up-to-date knowledge, skills, values and attitudes in subject disciplines and in a range of foundational literacies - Scientific, Mathematical, Financial, Digital and Entrepreneurship.
c. Learners are developed with the ability to apply what they have learned with confidence and competence in future learning and in the world of work.
d. Learner have understanding of Ghana's history, culture and traditions and of their rights and responsibilities as citizens.
e. Learners have understanding of learning as a lifelong endeavour through opportunities provided them to fully participate in the learning process.
f. Learners have appreciation of equality, equity and unity in diversity because they have experienced an inclusive educational system where individual strengths and potentials are valued and maximized, regardless of ethnicity, religion, gender, or geographical location.
g. Learners are enthused to learn Mathematics as a foundational building block to learning other subjects.
h. Learners have the sense of making responsible choices regarding the environment and climate.
i. Learners learn a Ghanaian language and a modern foreign language as part of becoming global citizens.
j. It is expected that: All learners will have access to quality core competences education provision in all subjects at every level.
k. Learners will be enthused about the value and importance of core competences as a lifelong endeavour and are empowered to be global citizens.
l. Opportunity should be created to build on the use of learner's first language and culture as an approach to equipping learners with effective communication skills that will inculcate in them appreciation of the values embodied in their language and culture.
m. Learners are to become multilingual through the study of Modern Foreign languages - French and Arabic.
n. Exposing all learners to the history of Ghana, nurturing all learners to become honest, creative and responsible citizens.
o. Learners must be developed into individuals who are systematic, creative, have self-confidence, skills and attributes, and positive attitudes towards science, mathematics and technology that are needed to succeed in life as Ghanaians and global citizens.

3. The Philosophy of Basic Education.desc:
- The philosophy of Basic Education is to ensure effective teaching and learning that responds to the opportunities and challenges facing Ghana currently and in the future.
- The philosophy requires the harmonious development of the physical, mental and spiritual capabilities of learners who undergo studies based on the National Curriculum.
- It requires that, Ghana's education system creates well-balanced (intellectually, spiritually, emotionally, and physically) individuals with the requisite knowledge, skills, values and aptitudes for self-actualisation and for the socio-economic and political transformation of the nation. (Anamuah-Mensah Committee Report, 2002).
- It is also to ensure that, the education system produce honest, creative and responsible citizens for both Ghana and the world.
- It is the belief that, irrespective of the diverse needs of learners in basic school classrooms, every student should be supported and given the opportunity to achieve their full potential and become a productive citizen.

# Requirements of the Philosophy

The philosophy of basic education requires that:

- The educational system should be flexible and encourage teachers to engage in innovative teaching to meet the unique needs of learners.
- Classrooms should be learner-centred; where learners are actively engaged in the learning process as a result of teachers adopting appropriate approaches to planning and classroom practice.
- Teaching and learning approaches and programmes are appropriately differentiated to meet the needs of learners.
- Schools shift from an emphasis on summative to the formative, a philosophy that espouses the need to employ multiple sources of evidence about learning, which will guide instructional decisions and support each learner's learning trajectory.
- Schools should be teacher-centred with its related practices (teacher self-evaluation, performance appraisal/professional review, effective use of assessment data, strong curriculum subject knowledge, use of appropriate pedagogy, accountability, continuing professional development, work-life balance and well-being) adopted so that a culture of high challenge, high trust and high performance in a professional environment is established - from which every student benefits (see Sherrington, 2016).
- Appropriate pedagogies are employed in the curriculum delivery. Because schools are teacher-centred, teachers are able to use pedagogies, equipment, and materials of instruction that transform classrooms from the traditional teacher-centred classrooms to environments that provide opportunities for the use of dialogic learning and teaching approaches - the use of inquiry-oriented learning approaches, thematic teaching, independent projects, computer simulations and technological devices such as phones, cameras, tablets and computers.

It is very important that teachers understand this philosophy and apply it consistently in their work.

4. Aims of Basic Education in Ghana.desc:
- The Aim of the National Pre-tertiary Education Curriculum of Ghana is to turn out graduates who are good problem solvers, have the ability to think creatively and have both the confidence and competence to participate fully in the Ghanaian society as responsible local and global citizens.

# Objectives

The following objectives form the basis of all teaching and learning programmes and classroom activities at the basic education level:

- Provide learners with broad up-to-date knowledge, skills, values and attitudes in both existing and new subject disciplines and in a range of foundational literacies.
- Develop in learners the ability to apply what they have learned with confidence and competence in the world of work.
- Instill in learners an understanding of Ghana's history, culture and traditions and of their rights and responsibilities as citizens.
- Encourage learners to fully participate in the learning process and to promote learning as a lifelong endeavour.
- Encourage and support teachers to engage in creative and dialogic instructional practices.
- Promote an inclusive educational system where individual strengths and potentials are valued and maximised, regardless of ethnicity, religion, gender, geographical location or disability.
- Encourage learners to learn mathematics as a foundational building block to learning other subjects.
- Instill in learners the importance of making responsible choices regarding the environment and climate.
- Promote financial literacy to include the development of entrepreneurial skills for all learners.
- Encourage learners to learn a Ghanaian language and a modern foreign language as part of becoming a global citizen.
ex: Aim: produce graduates who are problem solvers, creative thinkers, confident, competent, responsible local and global citizens.

Objectives: provide broad up-to-date knowledge/skills/values, develop ability to apply learning, instill understanding of Ghana's history/culture/rights/responsibilities, encourage lifelong learning, support creative/dialogic teaching, promote inclusive education, encourage mathematics learning, instill environmental responsibility, promote financial literacy/entrepreneurship, encourage Ghanaian and foreign language learning.
1992 Constitution: Basic Education = minimal formal education every Ghanaian child is entitled to as a right.
Purpose: equip graduates with 21st Century attributes of well-rounded, engaged citizens.

2. Basic Education.desc:
- The 1992 Constitution of Ghana defines Basic Education as the Minimal Formal Education with which every Ghanaian child is entitled to be equipped as a right, in order to function effectively in society.
- That is, Basic Education connotes the provision of opportunities that will provide graduates with the 21st Century attributes of well-rounded and engaged citizens.
- Specifically, Article 25 states that, Basic Education is free, compulsory and available to all and Secondary Education including TVET is to be generally available and accessible and will be progressively made free.

4. Competences and Standards in the Basi.desc:
- The expected competences are combinations of attitudes, skills and knowledge that enhance learners' ability to draw upon and build on what they know, how they think and what they can do in school, for lifelong learning, living and working.
- Ghana's curriculum is expected to promote the development of the following competences:

# Critical thinking and Problem solving

- Developing learners' cognitive and reasoning abilities to enable them to analyze issues and situations, leading to the resolution of problems.
- This skill enables learners to draw on and demonstrate what they have learned and from their own experiences to analyse situations and choose the most appropriate out of a number of possible solutions.
- It requires that learners embrace the problem at hand, persevere and take responsibility for their own learning.

# Creativity and innovation

- Promoting in learners, entrepreneurial skills through their ability to think of new ways of solving problems and developing technologies for addressing the problem at hand.
- It requires ingenuity of ideas, arts, technology and enterprise.
- Learners that possess this competency are also able to think independently and creatively.

# Communication and collaboration

- Promoting in learners the ability to make use of languages, symbols and texts to exchange information about themselves and their lived experiences.
- Learners actively participate in sharing their ideas and engage in dialogue with others by listening to and learning from others in ways that respect and value the multiple perspectives of all persons involved.

# Cultural identity and global citizenship

- Developing learners who put country and service as foremost through an understanding of what it means to be active citizens, by inculcating in them a strong sense of environmental, social, and economic awareness.
- Learners make use of the knowledge, skills, attitudes acquired to contribute effectively towards the socioeconomic development of the country and on the global stage.
- They build skills to critically analyse cultural trends, identify and contribute to the global world community.

# Personal development and leadership

- Improving self-awareness, self-knowledge, skills, health; building and renewing self-esteem; identifying and developing talents, fulfilling dreams and aspirations and developing other people or meeting other people's needs.
- It involves recognising the importance of values such as honesty and empathy; seeking the well-being of others; distinguishing between right and wrong; fostering perseverance, resilience and self-confidence; exploring leadership, self-regulation and responsibility, and developing a love for lifelong learning.

# Digital literacy

- Developing learners to discover, acquire skills in and communicate through Information Communication Technology (ICT) to support their learning and make use of digital media responsibly.

3. Structure/Phases of Basic Education P.desc:
- Formally, the formal Basic Education was for all Ghanaian pupils from Kindergarten (KG) to Junior High School (JHS 3), after which learners could progress to formal Senior High Education, Vocational Education and Training, direct entry to employment or distance learning.
- However, in the current education reforms, Basic Education has been redefined as a concept to include Senior High School, and the system sub-divided into five key phases as follows:

# Key Phase 1

[Foundation level comprising Kindergarten 1 & 2]

# Key Phase 2

[Lower primary level made up of B1 to B3]

# Key Phase 3

[Upper primary level of B4 to B6]

# Key Phase 4

[Junior high school level of B7 to B9]

# Key Phase 5

[Senior high school level comprising SHS1 - SHS3]

- Key Phases 1 and 2 constitute Early Childhood.
- Education at Key Phase 1 begins at age 4 with Kindergarten (KG) education. The child is expected to attend KG for two years (KG1 and KG2) and connect with Lower Primary education up to age 8 (Primary 1, 2 and 3).
- KG education predisposes children to conditions of formal schooling, imbuing in them the desire for learning during future years.
- The second, or Upper Primary phase, seeks to lay a strong foundation for inquiry, creativity and innovation, and lifelong learning in general, and to provide building blocks for higher levels of education (Anamuah-Mensah Report, 2002).
- The third phase of basic education is the three-year Junior High School or JHS (age 12-15 years), which is lower secondary education and provides the opportunity for pupils to discover their interests, abilities, aptitudes and other potential.
- The final phase of basic education is the three-year SHS (age 15-18 years), which is upper Secondary education and allows learners to specialize in any one of the following programmes: Science, General Arts, Technical and Vocational, Business, and a not less than one-year appropriate apprenticeship training programme.
- The SHS education is the platform that delivers an extensive gamut of academic knowledge and skills required for entry into further education and training in the tertiary institutions of Ghana and elsewhere.
- After sitting and passing the West Africa Secondary School Certificate Examination (WASSCE) conducted by the West African Examination Council (WAEC), SHS graduates may gain admission into the Universities, Polytechnics and such specialized institutions as Nursing Training Colleges, Colleges of Education, Ghana Police Command, etc.
ex: Previously: Basic Education was KG to JHS 3.
Current reforms: Basic Education includes SHS, divided into five key phases.
Key Phase 1: Kindergarten 1 & 2 (Foundation level).
Key Phase 2: Lower Primary (B1 to B3).
Key Phase 3: Upper Primary (B4 to B6).
Key Phase 4: Junior High School (B7 to B9).
Key Phase 5: Senior High School (SHS1 to SHS3).
Key Phases 1 & 2 = Early Childhood.
KG begins at age 4 for 2 years, connects to Lower Primary to age 8.
KG prepares children for formal schooling.
Upper Primary (B4-B6): foundation for inquiry, creativity, innovation, lifelong learning.
JHS (age 12-15): lower secondary; discover interests, abilities, aptitudes.
SHS (age 15-18): upper secondary; specialize in Science, General Arts, Technical/Vocational, Business, plus apprenticeship.
SHS prepares for tertiary education (Universities, Polytechnics, Nursing, Colleges of Education, etc.) through WASSCE.

- Developing learners who put country and service as foremost through an understanding of what it means to be active citizens, by inculcating in them a strong sense of environmental, social, and economic awareness.
- Learners make use of the knowledge, skills, attitudes acquired to contribute effectively towards the socioeconomic development of the country and on the global stage.
- They build skills to critically analyse cultural trends, identify and contribute to the global world community.

# Personal development and leadership

- Improving self-awareness, self-knowledge, skills, health; building and renewing self-esteem; identifying and developing talents, fulfilling dreams and aspirations and developing other people or meeting other people's needs.
- It involves recognising the importance of values such as honesty and empathy; seeking the well-being of others; distinguishing between right and wrong; fostering perseverance, resilience and self-confidence; exploring leadership, self-regulation and responsibility, and developing a love for lifelong learning.

# Digital literacy

- Developing learners to discover, acquire skills in and communicate through Information Communication Technology (ICT) to support their learning and make use of digital media responsibly.
ex: Competences: combinations of attitudes, skills, knowledge for lifelong learning.

Six core competences promoted: Critical thinking and Problem solving, Creativity and innovation, Communication and collaboration, Cultural identity and global citizenship, Personal development and leadership, Digital literacy.
Critical thinking: analyze issues, resolve problems, persevere, take responsibility.
Creativity: entrepreneurial skills, new problem-solving ways, independent thinking.
Communication: use languages/symbols/texts, share ideas, dialogue, respect multiple perspectives.
Cultural identity: active citizenship, environmental/social/economic awareness, contribute to national/global development.
Personal development: self-awareness, self-esteem, talent development, values (honesty, empathy), perseverance, leadership, lifelong learning.
Digital literacy: discover, acquire ICT skills, communicate, responsible digital media use.
Previously: Basic Education was KG to JHS 3.
Current reforms: Basic Education includes SHS, divided into five key phases.
Key Phase 1: Kindergarten 1 & 2 (Foundation level).
Key Phase 2: Lower Primary (B1 to B3).
Key Phase 3: Upper Primary (B4 to B6).
Key Phase 4: Junior High School (B7 to B9).
Key Phase 5: Senior High School (SHS1 to SHS3).
Key Phases 1 & 2 = Early Childhood.
KG begins at age 4 for 2 years, connects to Lower Primary to age 8.
KG prepares children for formal schooling.
Upper Primary (B4-B6): foundation for inquiry, creativity, innovation, lifelong learning.
JHS (age 12-15): lower secondary; discover interests, abilities, aptitudes.
SHS (age 15-18): upper secondary; specialize in Science, General Arts, Technical/Vocational, Business, plus apprenticeship.
SHS prepares for tertiary education (Universities, Polytechnics, Nursing, Colleges of Education, etc.) through WASSCE.
3. Structure/Phases of Basic Education P.desc:
- Formally, the formal Basic Education was for all Ghanaian pupils from Kindergarten (KG) to Junior High School (JHS 3), after which learners could progress to formal Senior High Education, Vocational Education and Training, direct entry to employment or distance learning.
- However, in the current education reforms, Basic Education has been redefined as a concept to include Senior High School, and the system sub-divided into five key phases as follows:

# Key Phase 1

[Foundation level comprising Kindergarten 1 & 2]

# Key Phase 2

[Lower primary level made up of B1 to B3]

# Key Phase 3

[Upper primary level of B4 to B6]

# Key Phase 4

[Junior high school level of B7 to B9]

# Key Phase 5

[Senior high school level comprising SHS1 - SHS3]

- Key Phases 1 and 2 constitute Early Childhood.
- Education at Key Phase 1 begins at age 4 with Kindergarten (KG) education. The child is expected to attend KG for two years (KG1 and KG2) and connect with Lower Primary education up to age 8 (Primary 1, 2 and 3).
- KG education predisposes children to conditions of formal schooling, imbuing in them the desire for learning during future years.
- The second, or Upper Primary phase, seeks to lay a strong foundation for inquiry, creativity and innovation, and lifelong learning in general, and to provide building blocks for higher levels of education (Anamuah-Mensah Report, 2002).
- The third phase of basic education is the three-year Junior High School or JHS (age 12-15 years), which is lower secondary education and provides the opportunity for pupils to discover their interests, abilities, aptitudes and other potential.
- The final phase of basic education is the three-year SHS (age 15-18 years), which is upper Secondary education and allows learners to specialize in any one of the following programmes: Science, General Arts, Technical and Vocational, Business, and a not less than one-year appropriate apprenticeship training programme.
- The SHS education is the platform that delivers an extensive gamut of academic knowledge and skills required for entry into further education and training in the tertiary institutions of Ghana and elsewhere.
- After sitting and passing the West Africa Secondary School Certificate Examination (WASSCE) conducted by the West African Examination Council (WAEC), SHS graduates may gain admission into the Universities, Polytechnics and such specialized institutions as Nursing Training Colleges, Colleges of Education, Ghana Police Command, etc.

5. Length of School Day and Contact Time.desc:
- The length of school day and contact time needed as a medium to deliver the new curriculum is 7 hours a day at Primary School and 8 - 9 hours at Secondary School, of which one hour is to be allocated to extra-curricular activities at primary school level and cross-curriculum activities in later years.
- The length of school day for Kindergarten shall however be 6 hours with an hour break.
ex: Primary School: 7 hours per day (1 hour for extra-curricular).
Secondary School: 8-9 hours per day.
Kindergarten: 6 hours per day with 1 hour break.

# Introduction

Dear learner, in this session student teachers would discuss the rationale, importance and implications of some policies affecting teaching and learning in basic schools in Ghana. The extent to which such policies promote or inhibit learning would be examined and ways of addressing them explored. There are many educational policies which seek to influence and promote education in Ghana. For the purpose of our study the focus will be on inclusive education policy, Free Compulsory Universal Basic Education (FCUBE), School Feeding Programme and Safe School Policy.

# Learning Indicators

3. The Free Compulsory Universal Basic E.desc:
- The 1992 constitution of the Republic of Ghana under Article 25(1) guarantees that all persons shall have the right to equal educational opportunities and facilities and that basic education shall be free, compulsory and available to all.
- It was based on this that the government of Ghana re-launched the policy of Free Compulsory Basic Education (FCUBE) in 1995 supported by the World Bank Primary School Development Project (PSDP).
- This initiative was meant to ensure that all school-age children received free and compulsory quality primary education by 2005.

# Challenges Addressed by FCUBE

The FCUBE seeks to address three major concerns or challenges:

1. limited access to basic education.
2. the poor quality of education resulting in poor numeracy, literacy and problem-solving skills.
3. ineffective and inefficient school management practices.

# Objectives of the FCUBE Programme

- Improving the Quality of Teaching and Learning: Much focus is on enhancing specific teaching skills through pre-service and in-service teacher training, improving teacher motivation through incentive programmes, promoting quality of students learning and performance through curriculum reviews and improved teacher-student interaction, provision of adequate and timely learning materials to all schools, improvement of teacher - community relationships.
- Improving Efficiency in Management: emphasis will be on re-organization and re-orientation of management practices in the education delivery system.
- Increasing Access and Participation: Efforts will be made to ensure total access and retention to all school-age children in the nine-year basic education programme, and all stakeholders participate fully in educational services/programmes within their localities. It will involve expanding infrastructural facilities and service to enhance access, addressing issues of enrollment and retention for all school-age children; enhancing quality in the provision of educational services and facilities, ensuring good quality teaching through the setting of performance targets, encouraging all stakeholders to participate fully in educational services/programmes. School Management Committee (SMC's) and Parent-Teacher Association (PTA's) activities increased with the intention to enhance communities' sense of ownership and participation in education service delivery.

# Measures to Deliver Quality Education

To deliver the objectives of FCUBE, government adopted a large-scale Whole School Development (WSA) programme that was designed and managed by the Ministry of Education with funding from the United Kingdom (UK) Department for International Development (DFID). To ensure delivery of quality education, government has put in place the measures below:

- in-service training for all categories of teachers to make them abreast with modern practices.
- encouraging all certificate 'A' teachers to upgrade themselves to at least the diploma certificate.
- all initial teacher training colleges are offering diploma programmes.
- constant review of the curriculum and textbooks.
- provision of incentives for teachers in deprived areas and the institution of the Best Teacher Award.
- Re-training of pupil teachers to diploma status.

# Management Arrangements

Looking at the area of management, the following arrangements were made:

- district directors, circuit supervisors and managers of schools are benefiting from training programmes.
- accommodation is being provided for head teachers close to their schools.
- heads of schools are appointed according academic and professional qualifications.
- the existence of SMCs and DEOC.
ex: FCUBE: based on 1992 Constitution Article 25(1) - free, compulsory, available basic education for all.
Re-launched 1995 with World Bank PSDP support; target: universal quality primary education by 2005.

Objectives: improve teaching/learning quality (training, motivation, curriculum, materials, community relations), improve management efficiency, increase access/participation (infrastructure, enrollment/retention, SMCs/PTAs).

Quality measures: in-service training, upgrade certificate A teachers to diploma, diploma in all colleges, curriculum/textbook review, incentives for deprived areas, Best Teacher Award, retrain pupil teachers.
