Inclusive Education Policy: strategic focus on equitable access, teaching materials, capacity development, service delivery.
Goal: redefine education delivery to respond to diverse needs within Universal Design for Learning and Child Friendly School Concept.

Standard 1 (Access): integral building design, smooth surfaces (no cobblestones), rest areas every 60m, no obstructions, guiding blocks, doors 915mm minimum width, separate toilets for boys/girls/PWDs, accessible toilet compartments.

Standard 2 (Health & Safety): first aid kits/sick bay, safety measures on roads, fenced compound, maintained grounds (no obstacles), slip-resistant floors (pile ≤13mm), protruding objects safe (700-200mm height, ≤100mm protrusion), spacious/well-lit/ventilated classrooms, age-appropriate furniture, protected electrical wiring.

Standard 3 (Learning Opportunities): Process (tailored curriculum, appropriate objectives/targets, diverse strategies, additional time for SEN, participation in all activities, qualified support staff), Learners (screening all learners, family involvement, registers of diverse needs, referral for assessment), Personnel (qualified/licensed teachers, inclusive education competence), Material Resources (adequate/appropriate materials).
Standard 4 (Monitoring): continuous classroom monitoring (observation, projects, tests), periodic stakeholder review meetings, disseminate performance information.
2. Inclusive Education Policy.desc:
- The Inclusive Education Policy defines the strategic path of the government for the education.
- The strategic focus includes improving equitable access to quality education for all children of diverse educational needs; provision of requisite teaching and learning materials; capacity development for professional and specialized teachers and managers as well as improvements in education service delivery.
- The IE Policy primarily seeks to provide direction for the management and delivery of education services to be re-packaged to ensure that the school curriculum, the processes of teaching and learning, and the professional development of professionals (human resources) and the provision of teaching and learning material resources is enhanced to address the diverse needs of various categories of persons of school age to stimulate efficient and effective learning for these pupils.
- The overarching goal of Ghana's Inclusive Education (IE) policy is to redefine and recast the delivery and management of educational services to respond to the diverse needs of all learners within the framework of Universal Design for Learning and Child Friendly School Concept.

# Standards and Guidelines for The Implementation of the IE Policy

Inclusive education standards shall satisfy anyone who is hindered from participating successfully in learning as a result of obstacles put in their way by the siting of the school, design of buildings, materials, arrangement of out- or inside space, attitude of staff, equipment and materials, as well content of instruction (curriculum) and pedagogy.

## Standard 1: Provide Access to Schools

a. To accommodate all learners including those with disabilities each building and its site shall be planned and designed as an integral unit from the very beginning of the design process.
b. All entrance path/sidewalks, walkways, ramps, roads and/or walkways shall be smooth, devoid of non-slip materials and shall have a firm level surface suitable for walking and wheeling. Irregular surfaces as cobble stones, coarsely exposed aggregate concrete, bricks etc. often cause bumpy rides.
c. When walks exceed 60 meters in length, a rest area is provided adjacent to the walkway at convenient intervals with space for bench seats.
d. Obstructions like manhole, tree or any other obstructions in the walkway shall be avoided.
e. Guiding block at the start and completion of a walkway shall be provided.
f. Main entrance doors and other accessible entrances and exit doors shall have a clear minimum opening width of 915mm from the most protruding edge of the door to the most protruding edge of the frame on the opposite side, regardless of whether the door is hinged, pivoted, sliding and double door type. This will allow safe passage of persons who use mobility aids.
g. All schools shall provide separate toilets and urinals for boys, girls and individuals with disabilities.
h. Where toilet facilities are accessible to wheelchair users, at least one toilet compartment in each bank of same-sex toilets shall be accessible.

## Standard 2: Providing Health and Safety in All Schools

a. Every school shall have adequate first aid kits, sick bay with qualified first aiders.
b. Roads and routes leading to the schools shall be provided with the necessary safety measures (speed ramps, marked out zebra crossings, light and sound ambers).
c. Every school compound shall be fenced and secured with locks and keys.
d. Every school compound shall be well maintained, and free from any obstacles such as poorly aligned hedges, tree trunks, and open gutters. Where there are boulders or pieces of rocks, the area shall be secured with safety fence. There shall be walkways and pavements to all schools.
e. Floor or ground surfaces shall be stable, firm and slip resistant. Carpet, carpet tiles or other floor finishes shall be securely attached and levelled across all types of pile. Pile height shall not exceed 13mm.
f. Protruding objects or objects with leading edges shall be between 700mm and 200mm above the floor or ground and shall protrude no more than 100mm into the clear width or circulation space including handrails. Classrooms shall be spacious (to allow movement), they shall well lighted and ventilated.
g. Furniture in every classroom shall be well arranged; all fixtures in classrooms shall be appropriately positioned to ensure safety.
h. Every classroom shall have adequate appropriate age-specific furniture such as tables, chairs, and lockers.
i. All electrical wiring in school premises shall be well protected covered.

## Standard 3: Every School (Private or Public) Should Provide All Learning Opportunities for Quality Learning

Provision of opportunities for quality learning involves focusing on four key areas namely: Process, Learner, Personnel and Resources.

a. The Process highlights the content and instruction and must address the following:
   - The National Curriculum should be made (tailored) suitable for every child. The curriculum should be broad and balanced. Teachers should adapt the content of the national curriculum (teaching syllabi) to make them relevant and functional to all learners.
   - Teachers should set appropriate objectives and achievable targets for all learners, ensure learners can cope with the levels set for them; use appropriate pace for all learners, do not slow down or hurry some learners through the curriculum (syllabus).
   - Teachers should use diverse strategies in teaching. For example, multi-sensory approaches, demonstration, project. Teachers should provide additional time for learners with SEN to complete learning activities/tasks and assignments.
   - Teachers should provide learners with SEN opportunities to participate in all activities, both within and without the schools. Every school should have adequate qualified related services staff such as Guidance and Counselling Coordinator, Social Workers, and Speech therapist etc.

b. The focus on Learners should consider the following:
   - Teachers shall screen all learners for special educational needs.
   - Families should be invited to witness and learn about the process and importance of screening and early intervention of special educational needs.
   - Teachers shall complete school registers indicating the diverse learning needs in their classrooms.
   - Teachers shall refer learners suspected of having special educational needs for further assessment.

c. Personnel - All schools should have ways of confirming that staff involved in the teaching of learners are qualified and competent to do so.
   - All teachers should have the requisite qualifications and license.
   - All schools should ensure that their staff recruitment and appointment procedures include a means of making certain that all new staff have at least the minimum necessary level of competence in Inclusive Education.

d. Material Resources - Ensure that material resources available for the support of the learner shall be adequate and appropriate for each programme and subjects offered.

## Standard 4: Monitor and Conduct Periodic Review of Programmes and Learners' Progress

- Classroom monitoring shall be a continuous activity of every school and diverse means (e.g. observation, projects, pen and paper based, etc.) shall be adopted to assess and document pupils/learners' performance.
- They shall conduct periodic review meeting of all stakeholders on school performance targets at national, regional, district and schools.
- Provide and disseminate requisite information on school performance.

5. Safe School Programme/Policy.desc:
- Safe schools' policy is the protection from 'any act of gender-based violence that results in, or is likely to result in physical, sexual, or psychological harm or suffering, including threats of such acts, coercion, or arbitrary deprivations of liberty, whether occurring in public or private life.'
- Safe Schools is a comprehensive approach to keep girls and boys safe from violence, conflict and hazards in and from school.
- The Safe School Programme is comprehensive, focusing on students, teachers, parents, community members, local organizations and government officials.

There are six discrete interventions that have been designed under the Safe Schools Programme that contribute to preventing and responding to school-related gender-based violence:

1. The student life skills violence prevention program.
2. The teacher professional development program.
3. The revision of the teachers' code of conduct.
4. The community counselor programme.
5. Community mobilization campaigns.
6. National advocacy efforts.

# Purpose of Safe School Programme

The following are the purposes for promoting safe school programmes in our pre-tertiary institutions:

- To create gender safe environments and reduce school-related-gender-based violence (SRGBV).
- Provides training, resources and technical assistance to establish a school/community environment which is physically and emotionally safe, well disciplined, and conducive to learning.
- Prevent and respond to bullying incidents of all students.
- Adopt a whole-school approach to preventing discrimination, harassment and bullying.
- Develop student-led activities to create positive, inclusive change, and equip other staff with skills and ideas to create inclusive environments.
- To provide a network of services, supports, and activities that promote a safe learning environment where students are drug and alcohol free and safe from bullying and violence.
- Engage students, staff, parents, agencies, and other community members to ensure a safe and supportive place of learning to improve student attendance, graduation rates, academic achievement, and reduce the number of suspensions for violent incidents.
- Create an advocacy network to improve awareness of school-related gender-based violence at the national level.
- Strengthen teachers' codes of conduct and the reporting of violations within the Ministry of Education (MOE).
- Strengthen curricula and teaching methods related to SRGBV prevention and response.
- Provide opportunities and support to children to build healthy relationships through school-based life skills activities and life skills after school clubs.
- Reduce school-related gender-based violence (SRGBV), which encompasses sexual, physical and psychological violence or abuse experienced by girls and boys in and around school.
ex: Safe School Policy: protection from gender-based violence (physical, sexual, psychological harm, threats, coercion, deprivation of liberty).
Comprehensive approach: keeps children safe from violence, conflict, hazards in/from school.
Focuses on: students, teachers, parents, community, local organizations, government officials.

Six interventions: student life skills violence prevention, teacher professional development, revision of teachers' code of conduct, community counselor programme, community mobilization campaigns, national advocacy efforts.

Purposes: create gender-safe environments, reduce SRGBV, provide training/resources for safe environment, prevent/respond to bullying, whole-school approach to discrimination/harassment, student-led inclusive activities, network of services for safe learning (drug/alcohol free), engage stakeholders to improve attendance/achievement/reduce suspensions, national advocacy network, strengthen teacher codes of conduct/reporting, strengthen curricula for SRGBV prevention, build healthy relationships through life skills clubs, reduce sexual/physical/psychological violence in/around school.

4. The Ghana School Feeding Programme (G.desc:
- The Ghana School Feeding Programme (GSFP) was initiated in 2005 as a social protection intervention in the context of the Comprehensive African Agricultural Development Programme (CAADP) Pillar III and in response to the first and second Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) on eradicating extreme poverty and hunger and achieving universal primary education.
- Over the period of implementation, the basic idea of the program has been to provide children in public primary schools and kindergartens with one hot nutritious meal, prepared from locally grown foodstuffs, on every school-going day.

# Policy Objectives

The broad and specific policy objectives were that:

1. school feeding efforts in Ghana would seek to improve school enrolment, attendance and retention among pupils in the most deprived communities in Ghana as a strategy.
2. promote an increase in domestic food production and consumption.
3. increase the incomes of poor rural households and improve the health and nutritional status of the pupils.

# Vision

Rapid national socio-economic development achieved through a coordinated, integrated and accountable national school feeding programme delivering improved nutrition for school children, reliable domestic markets for local farmers, effective local catering services and enhanced local incomes.

# Goal of the Policy

A well-organized, decentralized intervention providing disadvantaged school children with nutritionally adequate, locally produced food thereby reducing poverty through improved household incomes and effective local economic development.

# The Policy Formulation Process

To secure and sustain school feeding efforts and promote equity, a policy development process has been undertaken by the Government of Ghana in collaboration with its partners, Partnership for Child Development (PCD) and the World Food Programme (WFP).
- The policy is expected to anchor the gains made over the years as well as underpin effective delivery through adequate and reliable resources, robust management systems and local and national ownership.
- It is also anticipated that the policy will insulate school feeding through changing political circumstances and provide a basis for legislation.
- The first phase of the policy shall be implemented over a fifteen-year period from January 2016 to December, 2031.
ex: GSFP initiated 2005: social protection intervention under CAADP Pillar III, responding to MDGs 1 & 2 (poverty/hunger, universal primary education).
Core idea: one hot nutritious meal per school day from locally grown food for public primary/KG children.
Objectives: improve enrolment/attendance/retention in deprived communities, promote domestic food production/consumption, increase rural household incomes, improve pupil health/nutrition.
Vision: national socio-economic development through coordinated programme delivering nutrition, reliable markets for farmers, local catering services, enhanced incomes.
Goal: well-organized decentralized intervention providing nutritious local food, reducing poverty, improving local economic development.
Policy process: collaboration with PCD and WFP; aims to sustain gains, ensure effective delivery, insulate from political change, provide legislative basis.
Implementation: 15-year first phase (Jan 2016 - Dec 2031).

2. Inclusive Education Policy.desc:
- The Inclusive Education Policy defines the strategic path of the government for the education.
- The strategic focus includes improving equitable access to quality education for all children of diverse educational needs; provision of requisite teaching and learning materials; capacity development for professional and specialized teachers and managers as well as improvements in education service delivery.
- The IE Policy primarily seeks to provide direction for the management and delivery of education services to be re-packaged to ensure that the school curriculum, the processes of teaching and learning, and the professional development of professionals (human resources) and the provision of teaching and learning material resources is enhanced to address the diverse needs of various categories of persons of school age to stimulate efficient and effective learning for these pupils.
- The overarching goal of Ghana's Inclusive Education (IE) policy is to redefine and recast the delivery and management of educational services to respond to the diverse needs of all learners within the framework of Universal Design for Learning and Child Friendly School Concept.

# Standards and Guidelines for The Implementation of the IE Policy

Inclusive education standards shall satisfy anyone who is hindered from participating successfully in learning as a result of obstacles put in their way by the siting of the school, design of buildings, materials, arrangement of out- or inside space, attitude of staff, equipment and materials, as well content of instruction (curriculum) and pedagogy.

## Standard 1: Provide Access to Schools

a. To accommodate all learners including those with disabilities each building and its site shall be planned and designed as an integral unit from the very beginning of the design process.
b. All entrance path/sidewalks, walkways, ramps, roads and/or walkways shall be smooth, devoid of non-slip materials and shall have a firm level surface suitable for walking and wheeling. Irregular surfaces as cobble stones, coarsely exposed aggregate concrete, bricks etc. often cause bumpy rides.
c. When walks exceed 60 meters in length, a rest area is provided adjacent to the walkway at convenient intervals with space for bench seats.
d. Obstructions like manhole, tree or any other obstructions in the walkway shall be avoided.
e. Guiding block at the start and completion of a walkway shall be provided.
f. Main entrance doors and other accessible entrances and exit doors shall have a clear minimum opening width of 915mm from the most protruding edge of the door to the most protruding edge of the frame on the opposite side, regardless of whether the door is hinged, pivoted, sliding and double door type. This will allow safe passage of persons who use mobility aids.
g. All schools shall provide separate toilets and urinals for boys, girls and individuals with disabilities.
h. Where toilet facilities are accessible to wheelchair users, at least one toilet compartment in each bank of same-sex toilets shall be accessible.

## Standard 2: Providing Health and Safety in All Schools

a. Every school shall have adequate first aid kits, sick bay with qualified first aiders.
b. Roads and routes leading to the schools shall be provided with the necessary safety measures (speed ramps, marked out zebra crossings, light and sound ambers).
c. Every school compound shall be fenced and secured with locks and keys.
d. Every school compound shall be well maintained, and free from any obstacles such as poorly aligned hedges, tree trunks, and open gutters. Where there are boulders or pieces of rocks, the area shall be secured with safety fence. There shall be walkways and pavements to all schools.
e. Floor or ground surfaces shall be stable, firm and slip resistant. Carpet, carpet tiles or other floor finishes shall be securely attached and levelled across all types of pile. Pile height shall not exceed 13mm.
f. Protruding objects or objects with leading edges shall be between 700mm and 200mm above the floor or ground and shall protrude no more than 100mm into the clear width or circulation space including handrails. Classrooms shall be spacious (to allow movement), they shall well lighted and ventilated.
g. Furniture in every classroom shall be well arranged; all fixtures in classrooms shall be appropriately positioned to ensure safety.
h. Every classroom shall have adequate appropriate age-specific furniture such as tables, chairs, and lockers.
i. All electrical wiring in school premises shall be well protected covered.

## Standard 3: Every School (Private or Public) Should Provide All Learning Opportunities for Quality Learning

Provision of opportunities for quality learning involves focusing on four key areas namely: Process, Learner, Personnel and Resources.

a. The Process highlights the content and instruction and must address the following:
   - The National Curriculum should be made (tailored) suitable for every child. The curriculum should be broad and balanced. Teachers should adapt the content of the national curriculum (teaching syllabi) to make them relevant and functional to all learners.
   - Teachers should set appropriate objectives and achievable targets for all learners, ensure learners can cope with the levels set for them; use appropriate pace for all learners, do not slow down or hurry some learners through the curriculum (syllabus).
   - Teachers should use diverse strategies in teaching. For example, multi-sensory approaches, demonstration, project. Teachers should provide additional time for learners with SEN to complete learning activities/tasks and assignments.
   - Teachers should provide learners with SEN opportunities to participate in all activities, both within and without the schools. Every school should have adequate qualified related services staff such as Guidance and Counselling Coordinator, Social Workers, and Speech therapist etc.

b. The focus on Learners should consider the following:
   - Teachers shall screen all learners for special educational needs.
   - Families should be invited to witness and learn about the process and importance of screening and early intervention of special educational needs.
   - Teachers shall complete school registers indicating the diverse learning needs in their classrooms.
   - Teachers shall refer learners suspected of having special educational needs for further assessment.

c. Personnel - All schools should have ways of confirming that staff involved in the teaching of learners are qualified and competent to do so.
   - All teachers should have the requisite qualifications and license.
   - All schools should ensure that their staff recruitment and appointment procedures include a means of making certain that all new staff have at least the minimum necessary level of competence in Inclusive Education.

d. Material Resources - Ensure that material resources available for the support of the learner shall be adequate and appropriate for each programme and subjects offered.

## Standard 4: Monitor and Conduct Periodic Review of Programmes and Learners' Progress

- Classroom monitoring shall be a continuous activity of every school and diverse means (e.g. observation, projects, pen and paper based, etc.) shall be adopted to assess and document pupils/learners' performance.
- They shall conduct periodic review meeting of all stakeholders on school performance targets at national, regional, district and schools.
- Provide and disseminate requisite information on school performance.
ex: Inclusive Education Policy: strategic focus on equitable access, teaching materials, capacity development, service delivery.
Goal: redefine education delivery to respond to diverse needs within Universal Design for Learning and Child Friendly School Concept.

Standard 1 (Access): integral building design, smooth surfaces (no cobblestones), rest areas every 60m, no obstructions, guiding blocks, doors 915mm minimum width, separate toilets for boys/girls/PWDs, accessible toilet compartments.

Standard 2 (Health & Safety): first aid kits/sick bay, safety measures on roads, fenced compound, maintained grounds (no obstacles), slip-resistant floors (pile ≤13mm), protruding objects safe (700-200mm height, ≤100mm protrusion), spacious/well-lit/ventilated classrooms, age-appropriate furniture, protected electrical wiring.

Standard 3 (Learning Opportunities): Process (tailored curriculum, appropriate objectives/targets, diverse strategies, additional time for SEN, participation in all activities, qualified support staff), Learners (screening all learners, family involvement, registers of diverse needs, referral for assessment), Personnel (qualified/licensed teachers, inclusive education competence), Material Resources (adequate/appropriate materials).
Standard 4 (Monitoring): continuous classroom monitoring (observation, projects, tests), periodic stakeholder review meetings, disseminate performance information.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
# Introduction

Dear learner, in this session student teachers would discuss the rationale, importance and implications of some policies affecting teaching and learning in basic schools in Ghana. The extent to which such policies promote or inhibit learning would be examined and ways of addressing them explored. There are many educational policies which seek to influence and promote education in Ghana. For the purpose of our study the focus will be on inclusive education policy, Free Compulsory Universal Basic Education (FCUBE), School Feeding Programme and Safe School Policy.

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. Explain the meaning of inclusive education policy in basic education.
2. Discuss the importance of Free Compulsory Universal Basic Education (FCUBE).
3. Explain the rationale and objectives of the School Feeding Programme and Safe School Policy.
4. examine their implications of this policies for teaching and learning in basic schools in Ghana.
ex: Session examines key educational policies: Inclusive Education, FCUBE, School Feeding Programme, Safe School Policy.
Focus on rationale, importance, implications for teaching and learning.
Learning indicators: explain inclusive education policy, discuss FCUBE importance, explain rationale/objectives of School Feeding and Safe School policies, examine implications.

4. The Ghana School Feeding Programme (G.desc:
- The Ghana School Feeding Programme (GSFP) was initiated in 2005 as a social protection intervention in the context of the Comprehensive African Agricultural Development Programme (CAADP) Pillar III and in response to the first and second Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) on eradicating extreme poverty and hunger and achieving universal primary education.
- Over the period of implementation, the basic idea of the program has been to provide children in public primary schools and kindergartens with one hot nutritious meal, prepared from locally grown foodstuffs, on every school-going day.

# Policy Objectives

The broad and specific policy objectives were that:

1. school feeding efforts in Ghana would seek to improve school enrolment, attendance and retention among pupils in the most deprived communities in Ghana as a strategy.
2. promote an increase in domestic food production and consumption.
3. increase the incomes of poor rural households and improve the health and nutritional status of the pupils.

# Vision

Rapid national socio-economic development achieved through a coordinated, integrated and accountable national school feeding programme delivering improved nutrition for school children, reliable domestic markets for local farmers, effective local catering services and enhanced local incomes.

# Goal of the Policy

A well-organized, decentralized intervention providing disadvantaged school children with nutritionally adequate, locally produced food thereby reducing poverty through improved household incomes and effective local economic development.

# The Policy Formulation Process

To secure and sustain school feeding efforts and promote equity, a policy development process has been undertaken by the Government of Ghana in collaboration with its partners, Partnership for Child Development (PCD) and the World Food Programme (WFP).
- The policy is expected to anchor the gains made over the years as well as underpin effective delivery through adequate and reliable resources, robust management systems and local and national ownership.
- It is also anticipated that the policy will insulate school feeding through changing political circumstances and provide a basis for legislation.
- The first phase of the policy shall be implemented over a fifteen-year period from January 2016 to December, 2031.
ex: GSFP initiated 2005: social protection intervention under CAADP Pillar III, responding to MDGs 1 & 2 (poverty/hunger, universal primary education).
Core idea: one hot nutritious meal per school day from locally grown food for public primary/KG children.
Objectives: improve enrolment/attendance/retention in deprived communities, promote domestic food production/consumption, increase rural household incomes, improve pupil health/nutrition.
Vision: national socio-economic development through coordinated programme delivering nutrition, reliable markets for farmers, local catering services, enhanced incomes.
Goal: well-organized decentralized intervention providing nutritious local food, reducing poverty, improving local economic development.
Policy process: collaboration with PCD and WFP; aims to sustain gains, ensure effective delivery, insulate from political change, provide legislative basis.
Implementation: 15-year first phase (Jan 2016 - Dec 2031).

5. Safe School Programme/Policy.desc:
- Safe schools' policy is the protection from 'any act of gender-based violence that results in, or is likely to result in physical, sexual, or psychological harm or suffering, including threats of such acts, coercion, or arbitrary deprivations of liberty, whether occurring in public or private life.'
- Safe Schools is a comprehensive approach to keep girls and boys safe from violence, conflict and hazards in and from school.
- The Safe School Programme is comprehensive, focusing on students, teachers, parents, community members, local organizations and government officials.

There are six discrete interventions that have been designed under the Safe Schools Programme that contribute to preventing and responding to school-related gender-based violence:

1. The student life skills violence prevention program.
2. The teacher professional development program.
3. The revision of the teachers' code of conduct.
4. The community counselor programme.
5. Community mobilization campaigns.
6. National advocacy efforts.

# Purpose of Safe School Programme

The following are the purposes for promoting safe school programmes in our pre-tertiary institutions:

- To create gender safe environments and reduce school-related-gender-based violence (SRGBV).
- Provides training, resources and technical assistance to establish a school/community environment which is physically and emotionally safe, well disciplined, and conducive to learning.
- Prevent and respond to bullying incidents of all students.
- Adopt a whole-school approach to preventing discrimination, harassment and bullying.
- Develop student-led activities to create positive, inclusive change, and equip other staff with skills and ideas to create inclusive environments.
- To provide a network of services, supports, and activities that promote a safe learning environment where students are drug and alcohol free and safe from bullying and violence.
- Engage students, staff, parents, agencies, and other community members to ensure a safe and supportive place of learning to improve student attendance, graduation rates, academic achievement, and reduce the number of suspensions for violent incidents.
- Create an advocacy network to improve awareness of school-related gender-based violence at the national level.
- Strengthen teachers' codes of conduct and the reporting of violations within the Ministry of Education (MOE).
- Strengthen curricula and teaching methods related to SRGBV prevention and response.
- Provide opportunities and support to children to build healthy relationships through school-based life skills activities and life skills after school clubs.
- Reduce school-related gender-based violence (SRGBV), which encompasses sexual, physical and psychological violence or abuse experienced by girls and boys in and around school.
ex: Safe School Policy: protection from gender-based violence (physical, sexual, psychological harm, threats, coercion, deprivation of liberty).
Comprehensive approach: keeps children safe from violence, conflict, hazards in/from school.
Focuses on: students, teachers, parents, community, local organizations, government officials.

Six interventions: student life skills violence prevention, teacher professional development, revision of teachers' code of conduct, community counselor programme, community mobilization campaigns, national advocacy efforts.

Purposes: create gender-safe environments, reduce SRGBV, provide training/resources for safe environment, prevent/respond to bullying, whole-school approach to discrimination/harassment, student-led inclusive activities, network of services for safe learning (drug/alcohol free), engage stakeholders to improve attendance/achievement/reduce suspensions, national advocacy network, strengthen teacher codes of conduct/reporting, strengthen curricula for SRGBV prevention, build healthy relationships through life skills clubs, reduce sexual/physical/psychological violence in/around school.
National development: nation's ability to improve citizens' lives within growing economy.
Emphasis: improvement of lives, community good.
Governments draw plans according to citizen needs.
5. The Concept of National Development.desc:
- In your own words explain the concept of national development.
- National development refers to a nation's ability to improve the lives of its citizens within the context of a growing economy.
- Emphasis is on the improvement of lives and the good of the community as a whole.
- It should be noted however that, various governments draw their developmental plans according to the needs of the citizens.

4. Importance of CPD.desc:
- Continuing Professional Development (CPD) is important for high quality performance of all teacher educators – tutors, link tutors, mentors, and school leaders.
- CPD needs to occur in a range of forms, depending on the participants and their level of knowledge.
- Providing the serving professional teachers ample opportunities of updating their knowledge and skills towards better objective performance and advancement in status, without having to resign their employment.
- Providing the schools system, the opportunities of retaining the services of trained and qualified teachers while in sandwich/in-service education programme.
- Providing opportunities for increasing the number of trained and qualified teachers at all levels of education.
- Providing opportunities for teacher education institutions to provide service in an essential area of national development, especially by putting their facilities and staff into use during the long vacations.
- CPD helps teachers to demonstrate appropriate professional values and conduct that is expected of them.
- Helps teachers to express commitment to life-long learning and improving life chances and outcomes for all pupils.
- It reminds teachers of the value and purpose of the teacher preparation and affords development of additional skills, knowledge and understanding.
- It guides serving teachers to evaluate how effective what they do is helping develop the quality of students' teaching, assessment, classroom management strategies and use of reflection and action research to improve their teaching.
ex: CPD important for all teacher educators (tutors, link tutors, mentors, school leaders).
Occurs in various forms based on participants' knowledge level.
Updates knowledge/skills without resigning employment.
Retains trained teachers while they pursue further education.
Increases number of trained/qualified teachers.
Utilizes teacher education facilities/staff during vacations.
Helps demonstrate professional values/conduct.
Expresses commitment to lifelong learning; improves pupil outcomes.
Reminds of teacher preparation value; develops additional skills.
Guides evaluation of teaching effectiveness, assessment, classroom management, reflection, action research.

2. Teacher Education Process.desc:
# Entry Requirements and Duration

- The entry requirement into any of the Countries Colleges of Education is Senior Secondary School Certificate.
- Minimum entry requirement for the teacher education and universities has been a Senior Secondary Certificate.
- Currently, the Colleges of Education require aggregate 24 in six subjects, (three core and three electives) same as the entry requirements to the universities.
- At both levels, teaching experience is not a mandatory requirement.
- The duration for teacher training in the Colleges of Education is three years, earning them diplomas and four years in the universities earning them degrees.
- The duration for sandwich programmes is two semesters for candidates with university degree in their subject areas, earning them post graduate diploma in education.
- The emphasis of training is on both content and methodology for candidates with only secondary education, but tilted towards methodology for entrants with appropriate qualifications in their subject (content).

# Teacher Preparation Programmes

- The best teacher-preparation programs emphasize subject-matter mastery and provide many opportunities for student teachers to spend time in real classrooms under the supervision of an experienced mentor.
- Just as professionals in medicine, architecture, and law have opportunities to learn through examining case studies, learning best practices, and participating in internships, exemplary teacher-preparation programs allow teacher candidates the time to apply their learning of theory in the context of teaching in a real classroom.
ex: Entry requirement: Senior Secondary Certificate, aggregate 24 in six subjects (3 core, 3 electives).
Teaching experience not mandatory.
Duration: 3 years for diploma (Colleges of Education), 4 years for degree (universities).
Sandwich: 2 semesters for post-graduate diploma in education (degree holders).
Training emphasis: content and methodology for secondary leavers; methodology-focused for degree holders.
Best programmes: subject mastery, real classroom experience under mentor supervision.
Apply theory in real classroom context.
Education aim: develop productive individuals fit for self and society.
Equips intellectually, physically, morally; provides vocational skills.
Teacher: key personnel bringing behavioral change; vital for societal transformation.
NCTE (1998): teacher most important element in educational program; responsible for implementation.
Resources for teacher education secure nation's future.
Schooling competencies prepare for national development contribution.
Teacher education focuses on three skills: teaching skills, pedagogical theory, professional skills.
Teaching skills: techniques/approaches/strategies for planning instruction, reinforcement, assessment, classroom management, instructional materials, communication.
Pedagogical theory: sociological, philosophical, psychological basis for teaching practice.
Professional skills: counseling, interpersonal, IT, management, lifelong learning skills.
Integration of three skills promotes holistic student development useful to self and society.
7. The Role of Teachers in National Deve.desc:
- The aim of education is to develop an individual to be able to use the knowledge and skills acquired to be productive. That is to say, the individual should be fit for him/herself and the society at large.
- Education developed the individual by equipping him/her intellectually, physically and morally.
- The individual acquires vocational skills to face realities of life. He/she should be able to contribute meaningfully to society.
- The teacher is the significant personnel in the school who can bring about this change in behaviour.
- Teachers play vital roles to bring about transformation in society.
- According to NCTE (1998), the teacher is the most important element in any educational program. It is the teacher who is mainly responsible for the implementation of the educational processes and programmes at any stage.
- Therefore, there is no doubt that devoting resources to teacher education in the preparation of teachers will secure the future of a nation.
- As you are aware, the collective competencies we acquire through schooling prepare us to contribute to national development.
- Teacher Education programmes focus on skills training. This incorporates three main skills which are: teaching skills, pedagogical theory and professional skills.

# Teaching skills

- This aspect of teacher education provides training and practice in the different techniques, approaches and strategies that equip teachers with guidelines and skills to plan and impart instruction, provide effective, efficient and reliable reinforcement as well as conduct effective assessment.
- The teaching skills training also includes effective classroom management skills, preparation and use of instructional materials (aids) and effective communication skills.

# Pedagogical theory

- This describes the sociological, philosophical, and psychological considerations that motivate the teachers with sound basis for practicing the teaching skills in the classroom.

# Professional skills

- It involves the techniques, strategies and approaches that help teachers to develop themselves in the profession as they work towards the growth of the profession.
- This includes skills such as; counseling skills, interpersonal skills, skills in information technology, management skills and life-long learning skills.
- It is convincing that an integration of these three skills: teaching skills, pedagogical theory and professional skills would create the right knowledge, attitude and skills needed to promote holistic development of students who will be useful to themselves and the society at large.

ex: Education: most important factor in human development.
Promotes productive, informed citizenry; creates opportunities for underprivileged.
Strong correlation between education and national development.
Reduces poverty by building viable workforce for competitive global economy.
Developing countries need access to basic education.
Universal literacy key to advancement of US, Western countries, Japan, Korea, Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand.
DFID: education at heart of development; combines investment in education with sound economic policies; enables people to extend capabilities, improve livelihood, increase earning potential.
6. Education and National Development.desc:
- Education is the most important factor which plays a leading role in human development.
- It promotes a productive and informed citizenry and creates opportunities for the socially and economically underprivileged sections of society.
- Numerous empirical studies conducted by social scientists have established a strong correlation between education and national development; all attach utmost importance to education as an effective tool in reducing poverty by building a viable workforce capable of competing in an increasingly competitive (Parveen, 2013:8) and global economy.
- It is imperative that people in the developing countries have access to basic education, health and other relevant facilities.
- It is an acknowledged fact that universal literacy played a significant role in the phenomenal advancement of the United States and other western countries in almost every field of life.
- Similarly, countries like Japan, Korea, Singapore, Malaysia and Thailand had achieved near universal literacy before joining the coveted club of developed nations.
- The role of education in national development is, again, succinctly stated in the quotation below: 'Education is at the heart of development. Countries which made the greatest progress in reducing poverty in recent decades are those which have combined effective and equitable investment in education with sound economic policies. Education enables people to use and extend their capabilities to develop skills to improve their livelihood and increase their earning potential.' (DFID, 2012)

3. Stages of Teacher Education Programme.desc:
# Pre-Service Training

- This is usually provided in the Colleges of Education where the student teacher is introduced to the knowledge and skills needed to do a professional job in teaching.
- The teacher is introduced to the principles that underline teaching aims of education, the curriculum, the nature and characteristics of child development, methods of teaching and learning and resources from which pupils and teachers can draw to enhance teaching and learning.
- The pre-service training introduces the student teacher to practical skills, which finally helps the teacher to perform efficiently on the profession.

# Teacher-Induction

- This is a stage when a teacher has taken full responsibility of a full-time teaching. However, this teacher is not fully qualified and is said to be on probation.
- During this induction period the teacher receives some guidance and supervision from veteran teachers.
- Most of the time head teachers who have been trained as mentors are expected to be mentoring or coaching the student teachers on the job.
- These mentors act like critical friends to see to the successful development of a good teacher.
- Induction programs therefore, create opportunities for novice teachers to learn from best practices and analyze and reflect on their teaching.

# In-service Training (Continuing Professional Development (CPD))

- It is critical for veteran teachers to have ongoing and regular opportunities to learn from each other and adapt to new ways of doing things.
- In-service training keeps teachers up-to-date on new research on how children learn, emerging technology tools for the classroom, new curriculum resources, teaching strategies etc.
- The best professional development is focusing on learning as a teacher by attending courses, conferences, reading relevant books and collaborating with your colleagues to improve your lot.
ex: Pre-Service: in Colleges of Education; introduces knowledge, skills, principles of teaching, curriculum, child development, methods, resources; provides practical skills.
Induction: full teaching responsibility but on probation; guidance/supervision from veteran teachers; head teachers as mentors; opportunities to learn from best practices, analyze, reflect.
In-service/CPD: ongoing learning for veteran teachers; keeps up-to-date on research, technology, curriculum, strategies; includes courses, conferences, reading, collaboration.

4. Key Policies Influencing Teacher Educ.desc:
- The teacher education policy in Ghana is designed to develop and equip prospecting teachers as well as serving professional teachers with knowledge, skill, the right attitudes and values to meet the needs of quality education in the 21st century.
- The policy draws on the 2008 Education Act which calls for the establishment of the National Teaching Council (NTC) with responsibility of setting professional standards and code of practice for teacher education, ensuring facilities for professional development, registration and licensing of teachers.
- The policy in its overall focus is to equip teachers with the needed capacities so that they can undertake their essential and demanding tasks to enable them continually enhance teaching and learning as well as their professional competence.
- The policy defines teacher education as the process through which individuals are equipped with knowledge, skills, the right attitudes and values for inducing desirable changes in learners.
- The teacher education process in Ghana has three complementary sub-systems comprising Initial Professional Education of Teachers (IPET), Induction Training Programme and Continuing Professional Teacher Development (CPTD).
- Continuous professional development should be seen as part of the teaching profession. As such, the GES has institutionalized in-service education and training for all teachers.

There are some critical policy documents that set the stage for the reform of teacher education in the country. These are:

1. The Pre-tertiary Teacher Development and Management (PTPDM) policy
2. National Teacher Education Curriculum Framework (NTECF) policy
3. National Teachers' Standards (NTS, 2016)

## 1. Pre-tertiary Teacher Professional Development and Management (PTPDM) policy

### Vision

The policy seeks to produce quality, highly skilled, knowledgeable and committed teachers to teach in Ghanaian schools. In effect it will support teachers to enable them function effectively in the basic and second cycle schools in Ghana and to develop and nurture them to become reflective and proficient practitioners capable of providing quality education for all Ghanaian children.

### Core Values

The core values for teachers shall reflect commitment, attitudes, ethics and morals that should promote quality education for all children in Ghana.

### Policy Goal

The policy goal is to ensure that teachers are recruited, trained to teach and to continue to develop their competencies and skills through lifelong learning.

## 2. National Teachers' Standards

### Vision

National Teachers' Standards 'set a clear baseline of expectations for the professional practice and conduct of teachers, and define the minimum level of practice expected of teachers at the pre-tertiary level.'
- National Teachers' Standards replaces the diversity of standards being used in the various institutions offering initial teacher education and in the professional practice.
- The National Teachers' Standards (NTS) set out the minimum levels of practice that all trained teachers must reach by the end of their pre-service teacher education course in order to play a critical role in inspiring and challenging all pupils to achieve their potential.
- All initial teacher education must explicitly provide the opportunity for student teachers to fully meet all the NTS.
- The National Teachers' Standards for Ghana Guidelines document provides the standards for both pre-service and in-service teachers.
- The Standards have been developed as a professional tool to guide teacher educators, teachers, student teachers and other stakeholders in education to identify in clear and precise terms what teachers are expected to know and be able to do, qualities they are expected to possess and some behaviour they are supposed to exhibit.
- The National Teachers' Standards for Ghana Guidelines document provides the standards for both pre-service and in-service teachers. Thus, teachers in the training institutions and those practicing as full-time teachers.

### Aim

The Standards aim at ensuring that as teachers move up from one rank to the other in their career; they are empowered to engender effective learning among those they are expected to teach.

## 3. The National Teacher Education Curriculum Framework (NTECF)

The National Teacher Education Curriculum Framework (NTECF) defines the overarching vision, critical content areas, pedagogy, linkages, and assessment from which Teacher Education curricula will be developed.
- It is a tool that allows flexibility and diversity among different training institutions while ensuring consistency and quality in teacher education curriculum through compliance with the defined elements of the Curriculum Framework.
ex: Teacher education policy: equips teachers with knowledge, skills, attitudes, values for 21st century quality education.
Based on 2008 Education Act: establishes NTC for standards, code of practice, professional development, registration, licensing.
Three sub-systems: IPET (Initial Professional Education of Teachers), Induction Training, CPTD (Continuing Professional Teacher Development).
GES institutionalized in-service education for all teachers.
Three key policy documents: PTPDM, NTECF, NTS (2016).
PTPDM vision: produce quality, skilled, committed teachers; develop reflective, proficient practitioners.
PTPDM core values: commitment, attitudes, ethics, morals for quality education.
PTPDM goal: recruit, train teachers, develop competencies through lifelong learning.
NTS: sets clear baseline expectations for professional practice/conduct; defines minimum practice levels.
NTS replaces diverse standards; all ITE must enable students to meet NTS.
NTS aim: empower teachers as they advance in career to engender effective learning.
NTECF: defines vision, content areas, pedagogy, linkages, assessment for teacher education curricula; ensures consistency and quality across institutions.
Phases: 4-year Cert A → 2-year Cert B → 2-year Post-B Cert A → 2-year Cert A (Post-middle) → 2-year Specialist/3-year Diploma → 3-year Cert A Post-secondary → 3-year DBE → 4-year B.Ed.
Three key policies: PTPDM, NTS, NTECF.
Self-assessment: outline phases, compare DBE vs B.Ed., discuss three key policies, examine policy implications.
5. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

We have come to the end of the session, we wish to remind you that in the session, we discussed the Changing phases of teacher education after independence in Ghana as pertain to teacher training colleges now colleges of education, thus from 4 year Certificate 'A', 2 year Certificate 'B', 2-year Post-B Certificate 'A', 2 year Certificate 'A' (Post-middle), 2-year Specialist/Three-year Diploma, Certificate 'A' 3-year Post-secondary, 3-year Diploma in Basic Education and currently we have 4-year Bachelor of Education in Basic Education.

# Self-Assessment

1. Outline the changing phases of teacher education in teacher colleges after independence in Ghana.

2. Compare and contrast previous teacher education programme (DBE) to current one (B.Ed.) and indicate what makes the current one unique.

3. Discuss the THREE (3) main key policies influencing teacher education in Ghana.

4. Examine the policy implications for teaching and learning in basic schools in Ghana.

1. Introduction and Learning Objectives.desc:
# Introduction

Dear learner, congratulations for coming so far in the study of our course on teacher education in Ghana. We welcome you to session 3 on the changing phases of teacher education after independence in Ghana. In this session, we introduced you to the history of teacher education in Ghana and the key policies influencing teacher education in Ghana.

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. Outline the changing phases of teacher education in teacher colleges after independence in Ghana.
2. Compare and contrast previous teacher education programme (DBE) to current one (B.Ed.) and indicate what makes the current one unique.
3. Discuss the THREE (3) main key policies influencing teacher education in Ghana.
4. Examine the policy implications for teaching and learning in basic schools in Ghana.
ex: Session covers changing phases of teacher education after independence and key policies.
Learning indicators: outline changing phases, compare DBE vs B.Ed., discuss three key policies, examine policy implications.

2. Changing Phases of Teacher Education.desc:
- Teacher training in Ghana has a history of its own. Historians agree that during the colonial era, teacher training was closely associated with the work of the various religious denominations.
- In many cases, the headmasters of the schools also acted as caretakers of the village church.
- The secularization of the teaching profession occurred with the introduction of the 1951 Accelerated Education Plan.
- Also, because of the rapid expansion of the school system, the need for teachers were recruited as 'pupil-teachers.' Even as late as 1966, around 63 percent of the nation's primary school teachers were uncertified.
- The rapid expansion of teacher training facilities throughout the country took place in the decade of the 1960s. The goal was to provide 4-year 'Certificate A' training for teachers.
- Until it was phased out in 1963, 2-year 'Certificate B' colleges also operated to provide a quick turnover of certified teachers.
- Students who had completed the traditional 5-year secondary education could also be certified to teach in the elementary school system by attending specialized 2-year postsecondary teacher institutions.
- The rapid expansion of teacher education yielded results and by 1971, approximately 71 percent of all primary school teachers were certified.
- Confident that the nation's teacher supply could be met in the near future, many of the teacher training institutions were converted to secondary schools. Of course, this was prior to the severe economic crisis of the late 1970s through the early 1980s that forced many to seek better paying jobs in Nigeria.
- Furthermore, the educational reforms that began in 1987 have brought further changes in teacher training.
- All teacher training colleges in the country were operated as postsecondary institutions for awarding 3-year Certificate 'A' until 2007.

# Summary of Phases

- 4-year Certificate 'A'
- 2-year Certificate 'B'
- 2-year Post-B Certificate 'A'
- 2-year Certificate 'A' (Post-middle)
- 2-year Specialist/Three-year Diploma
- Certificate 'A' 3-year Post-secondary
- 3-year Diploma in Basic Education
- Currently: 4-year Bachelor of Education in Basic Education
ex: Colonial era: teacher training associated with religious denominations.
1951 Accelerated Education Plan: secularization of teaching profession.
Rapid expansion led to recruitment of pupil-teachers; 63% uncertified by 1966.
1960s: rapid expansion of training facilities; goal of 4-year Certificate A.
Until 1963: 2-year Certificate B colleges for quick certified teacher turnover.
5-year secondary leavers could attend 2-year postsecondary teacher institutions.
By 1971: 71% primary teachers certified.
Late 1970s-80s: economic crisis; teachers left for Nigeria.
1987 reforms: further changes.
Until 2007: all training colleges awarded 3-year Certificate A.

Phases timeline: 4-year Cert A → 2-year Cert B → 2-year Post-B Cert A → 2-year Cert A (Post-middle) → 2-year Specialist/3-year Diploma → 3-year Cert A Post-secondary → 3-year Diploma in Basic Education → 4-year B.Ed. in Basic Education.

3. Teacher Education under the 2007 Educ.desc:
- Anamuah-Mensah committee was set up in 2007 to review the entire educational system in Ghana by making it more responsive to current challenges threatening Ghana's development.
- Among these recommendations included the upgrading of teacher training colleges to diploma awarding institutions with a new name 'Colleges of Education'.
- The colleges of education were also recommended to be affiliated to education-oriented universities.
- In line with the demands of the 2007 Education Reforms, all colleges of education in Ghana were affiliated to the University of Cape Coast.
- In October 2018, all the Colleges of Education in Ghana have been upgraded to a Bachelor degree in Basic Education (B.Ed.) awarding institutions.
- Each of the Colleges has been affiliated to one of these five public Universities in Ghana for mentoring. They are: University of Education, Winneba, University of Cape Coast, University of Ghana, Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology and the University of Development Studies.
ex: 2007 Anamuah-Mensah Committee: reviewed education system to respond to challenges.
Recommendation: upgrade teacher training colleges to diploma-awarding 'Colleges of Education'.
Colleges affiliated to education-oriented universities (initially all to UCC).
October 2018: all Colleges upgraded to B.Ed. awarding institutions.
Affiliated to five public universities for mentoring: UEW, UCC, UG, KNUST, UDS.
Four-year, eight-semester B.Ed. with three specialisms: Early Years (KG1-P3), Upper Primary (P4-6), Junior High School.
Ensures depth of knowledge and connection to learners' developmental levels.
Early Years (KG1-P3): thematic approach, developmentally appropriate practices, play-based learning.
Upper Primary (P4-6): skills to be class teachers, teach all Basic School subjects, support middle childhood learners.
JHS: specialist subject knowledge, address early adolescent developmental needs.
Four pillars: Subject and Curriculum Knowledge, Literacy Studies (Ghanaian Languages & English), Pedagogic Knowledge, Supported Teaching in School.
Subject knowledge: secure content and pedagogic knowledge; includes misconceptions, barriers, curriculum studies; specific to each specialism.
Literacy Studies: Ghanaian languages and English; Early Grade Literacy L1/L2; key to accessing curriculum.
Pedagogic Knowledge: general pedagogy, assessment, cross-cutting issues, education studies, preparation for supported teaching, classroom enquiry, inclusion/SEN, ICT.
Supported Teaching: 30% of training in field; KG-P3 and P4-6 in schools with mentors; TVET in schools and industry; mentors assess against NTS.
3. Structure for the new teacher educati.desc:
- The teacher education curriculum for the new Basic Education Degree provides Ghana's teachers with the range of practical competencies, skills, knowledge and values to deliver high-quality education.
- In order to achieve this overarching vision for the NTECF and to enable all student teachers to meet the NTS, a four-year, eight semester curricula, B.Ed. made up of three distinct specialism programmes: Early Years' (KG 1- P3); Upper Primary (P4- 6) and Junior High School was developed.
- This will ensure depth of knowledge of what is to be taught and enable them to connect with the developmental level of the learners they teach.

# Figure 1: conceptual framework of 4-year B.Ed. curriculum

- The Early Grade Education Programme, KG1 to P3, will use a thematic approach to training and equipping student teachers with developmentally appropriate practices, which promote play-based learning.
- The Upper Primary Education Programme, P4 to P6, will equip student teachers with the needed skills to be class teachers, able to teach all the subjects of the Basic School Curriculum and to support learning and development of the middle childhood learners.
- The Junior High School Education Programme will equip student teachers with specialist subject knowledge and the ability to address the developmental needs of learners in early adolescence.

# The four pillars of the curriculum

The NTECF consists of the four pillars of teacher education. These represent the knowledge, skills and understanding necessary for effective teaching:

1. Subject and Curriculum Knowledge;
2. Literacy Studies: Ghanaian Languages and English;
3. Pedagogic Knowledge and
4. Supported Teaching in School.

The four pillars of the curriculum: these set out the essential knowledge, skills and understanding necessary for effective teaching, as defined by the NTECF.

- Subject and curriculum knowledge: secure, subject-specific content and pedagogic knowledge are the key to being able to teach the school curriculum including: subject knowledge for teaching; progress in learning in subjects; misconceptions, potential contextual barriers to learning and curriculum studies. All school curriculum subjects are addressed and made specific to each specialism.
- Literacy Studies (Ghanaian Languages and English), including Early Grade Literacy in L1 and L2. As language is the key to enabling children to access the curriculum, this learning area is a pillar in its own right.
- Pedagogic Knowledge, including: general pedagogic knowledge, assessment strategies, introduction to and development of cross cutting issues, education studies, preparation for supported teaching in school, classroom enquiry and research, Inclusion and equity, SEN and ICT.
- Supported teaching in school: student teachers will spend 30% of their training in the field. For the KG-P3 and P4-6 specialisms this training period will be spent in schools with the support of mentors. For TVET this part of the training will be divided between TVET schools and industry with the support of mentors. The mentors will assess student teachers on the development of competencies and skills set out in the National Teachers' Standards.

7. Summary and Self-Assessment.desc:
# Summary

We have discussed about the structure of the new 4-year B.Ed. programme, the rationale behind the change. Model of progress in student teacher learning across the four Years, beginning teaching, developing teaching, embedding teaching and extending teaching. We also look at the specialization across the curriculum thus Early Grade (KG 1- P3); Upper Primary (P4- 6) and Junior High School. Assessment of the programme. Student teachers pursuing this degree will be fully prepared to meet the NTS and to be assessed against it.

# Self-Assessment

1. Explain the main rationale of the new B.Ed. programme.

2. Outline the factors that led to the development of the new B.Ed. programme.

3. State and explain the mode of progress of the B.Ed. programme.
ex: Structure: 4-year B.Ed., three specialisms (Early Years KG1-P3, Upper Primary P4-6, JHS).
Model of progress: Beginning (Year 1), Developing (Year 2), Embedding (Year 3), Extending (Year 4).
Assessment: 40% coursework, 30% STS, 30% examination.
Student teachers prepared to meet and be assessed against NTS.
Self-assessment: explain rationale, outline development factors, state/explain mode of progress.
Year 1 (Beginning teaching): foundation year; transition support; introduces key principles/practices; locate specialisms within wider curriculum.
Year 2 (Developing teaching): elect specialism; identify/assess learner weaknesses/barriers; small-scale action research under mentors.
Year 3 (Embedding teaching): build skills in specialism; co-plan/co-teach; classroom enquiries; evidence toward NTS; preparation for year 4.
Year 4 Semester 1 (Extending teaching): final internship; significant classroom enquiry/action research.
Year 4 Semester 2: complete courses.
By end of year 4: plan/teach/assess independently; exhibit ethical code/values/attitudes; extensive action research; full evidence of meeting NTS.
4. How specialisms are developed in the.desc:
- The first year is a foundation year for all student teachers. It means student teachers will be able to locate their specialism within the wider curriculum.
- The specialism is introduced in year two and continues through to the end of year four.
- All subjects and learning areas share core content across programmes and there is an opportunity for student teachers to work in opposing specialisms during year four supported teaching in schools.
- Thus, these will help them achieve depth and breadth of knowledge in and across specialisms.

# Figure 2: Model of Progress of Student Teachers Across the Four Years

Figure 2, above, shows the model of progress for student teachers across the four years. Each year of the curriculum builds on the outcomes of the previous year. This is achieved through: college-based training, school-based experience and training, course work, practical work, work-based learning and independent study.

# Year one: (Beginning teaching)

- Provides support for the transition from school to college and recognizes that many student teachers will have come from school level education and from a wide range of backgrounds and experience.
- It introduces the key principles and practices of the subjects and learning areas and supported teaching in school within the wider curriculum thereby ensuring that student teachers can locate their specialisms.

# Year two: (Developing teaching)

- Student teachers elect for one of the three specialism programmes but key features from year one continues to be developed as relevant to each specialism.
- The second year prepares student teachers to identify and assess weaknesses and barriers to learning for learners and carry out small-scale action research under the guidance of mentors.

# Year three: (Embedding teaching)

- Student teachers will continue to build skills, knowledge and understanding in their chosen specialism.
- They will co-plan and co-teach groups of learners and whole classes; carry out small scale classroom enquiries and provide evidence of working towards meeting the NTS.
- Year three includes preparation for year four.

# Year four semester one: (Extending teaching)

- Final supported teaching in school (internship) and for significant classroom-based enquiry and action research projects.

# Year four semester two

- Students will return to school to complete some courses.

By the end of the fourth year, extending teaching, student teachers will:
- plan, teach and assess their learners independently and with increasing consistency;
- exhibit the ethical codes of conduct, values and attitudes expected of a teacher;
- carry out extensive action research projects and
- provide evidence of meeting the National Teachers' Standards in full.

# Introduction

# Learning Indicators

By the end of the session, you will be able to:

1. Examine the rationale of the new B.Ed. programme.
2. Analyse the factors that led to the development of the new B.Ed. programme.
3. Explain the structure of the B.Ed. programme.

2. Rationale of the new 4-year B.Ed. pro.desc:
- Ghana's education landscape has been experiencing a set of ambitious and wide-ranging reforms including teacher education reforms which are designed to ensure that the country's classrooms are staffed with trained, skilled and motivated teachers.
- A key pillar of the teacher education reform is the shift from a Diploma in Basic Education to a new 4-year Bachelor of Education (B.Ed.) Degree in Basic Education.
- The intention of this B.Ed. curriculum is to transform initial teacher education (ITE) and to prepare teachers imbued with the professional skills, attitudes and values, and depth and breadth of content knowledge.
- Also, teacher education in Ghana should create in teachers the spirit of enquiry, innovation and creativity that will enable them to adapt to changing conditions, use inclusive strategies and engage in life-long learning (see National Teachers' Standards (NTS) [NTC, 2017]).
- The teachers are required to be change agents and reflective practitioners with a passion for teaching and leadership. They are required to engage with members not only in the school community but also in the wider community, and act as potential agents of change (see National Teacher Education Curriculum Framework (NTECF) [NCTE, 2018]).
- Student teachers pursuing this degree will be fully prepared to meet the NTS and to be assessed against it.
- Raising the status of the teaching profession and making it more attractive to new entrants.
- Improving the quality of teachers by ensuring that they undergo a rigorous and practically focused, high-quality degree level programme.
- Improve learning outcomes and life chances for all children.
- Raise teachers who demonstrates attainment of the minimum levels of practice, as set out in the National Teachers' Standards in order to inspire and challenge learners to achieve their potential.
- Develop teachers who have the ability to integrate his/her curriculum, subject and pedagogical knowledge, and plans for and uses differentiated interactive instructional strategies and resources to improve the learning outcomes of all learners irrespective of gender, disabilities, or geographical location.
- Train teachers who view learners as active constructors of knowledge and is therefore able to create learning contexts that are learner-centred and encourages learners to collaborate with others.
- Equip teachers with professional skills, attitudes and values, secure content knowledge as well as the spirit of enquiry, innovation and creativity that will enable them to adapt to changing conditions, use inclusive teaching strategies, engage in life-long learning and demonstrate honesty, integrity and good citizenship in all they do.
- Train teachers who have passion for teaching and learning, and are able to reflect on their practice, engage with members not only in the school community but also in the wider community, and act as potential agents of change.

5. Assessment for the B.Ed. programme.desc:
- The practice of assessment in teacher education institutions in Ghana shall ensure the validity, reliability, transparency and fairness of assessment of student teachers and learners (in school) in line with the NTS.
- Assessment of student teachers shall adhere to the guidelines as established in the NTS and NTECF to influence their progress to become a teacher.
- It shall occur at the beginning, developing, embedding and extending stages of progression in the teacher training process and shall include formative and summative methods; educative assessment for, of and as learning.
- All assessments must be underpinned by an awareness of equity and inclusivity of opportunity, both in relation to student teachers' own learning experience and to the experience of their learners.

# Course Assessment Components

- Course assessment shall have different components including assignments, presentations, laboratory work, projects, practical activities, performance, quizzes and examinations.
- These components need to conform to the NTS.
- The weightings of course components vary across the four years according to the credit hours allocated to each course.
- As such, different subjects or learning area courses carry different credits and assessment components throughout the four years.
- 40% Course work, assignments, presentations, quizzes, etc.
- 30% Supported Teaching in School (STS); and
- 30% Examination.
- The weighting is an indication of the importance placed on extended periods of supported teaching in school (STS) and the use of interactive learner focused approach to training teachers through formative assessment of course work where the focus would be on performance and shall be adhered to.
ex: Assessment ensures validity, reliability, transparency, fairness in line with NTS.
Adheres to NTS and NTECF guidelines; occurs at beginning, developing, embedding, extending stages.
Includes formative and summative methods; assessment for, of, as learning.
Underpinned by equity and inclusivity for student teachers and their learners.
Components: assignments, presentations, lab work, projects, practical activities, performance, quizzes, examinations.
Weightings vary by credit hours; conform to NTS.
Standard weighting: 40% coursework, 30% Supported Teaching in School, 30% Examination.
Emphasis on STS and interactive, learner-focused formative assessment.

6. Admission Requirements.desc:
The entry requirements for admission to the new 4-Year B.Ed. degree is as follows:

i. WASSCE Holders: CREDIT (A1-C6) in Six (6) subjects comprising Three (3) Core subjects, including English Language and Core Mathematics, and Three (3) Elective subjects relevant to the course of study.
ii. SSSCE Holders: CREDIT (A-D) in Six (6) subjects comprising Three (3) Core subjects, including English Language and Core Mathematics, and Three (3) Elective subjects relevant to the course of study.
iii. Holders of TVET Qualifications: CREDIT in Three Core subjects including English Language and Mathematics and PASSES in Three Elective subjects relevant to the course of study.
ex: WASSCE: Credit (A1-C6) in 6 subjects (3 Core incl. English & Maths, 3 Electives relevant to course).
SSSCE: Credit (A-D) in 6 subjects (3 Core incl. English & Maths, 3 Electives relevant to course).
TVET: Credit in 3 Core (incl. English & Maths) and Passes in 3 relevant Electives.
